Multi-task learning jointly estimates valence-arousal, 8 facial expressions, and 12 Action Units. Post-hoc threshold calibration addresses class imbalance.
AI.result: Rectified-flow decoding improves CCC-V by +0.058 for valence-arousal. Post-hoc calibration recovers Fear from 3.8% to 33.1%. Final P_MTL=1.177 substantially outperforms the baseline (0.45).
AI.conclusion: Conditional rectified-flow decoding combined with threshold calibration effectively models ambiguity and imbalance in facial behavior analysis, significantly advancing state-of-the-art on the ABAW benchmark.

title: Attitude Estimation Using Inertial and Barometric Measurements
summary: Accurate and robust attitude estimation is a key challenge for autonomous vehicles, particularly in GNSS-denied conditions and during highly accelerated flight. In such conditions, Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) alone are insufficient for reliable tilt estimation due to the ambiguity between gravitational and inertial accelerations. Although auxiliary velocity sensors such as GNSS, Pitot tubes, Doppler radar, or Visual Inertial Odometry are commonly used, they may be unavailable, intermittent, or costly. This paper introduces a barometer-aided attitude estimation architecture that exploits barometric altitude measurements to provide complementary information on the vehicle's vertical motion, thereby enhancing attitude estimation within nonlinear observers on SO(3). The contributions are twofold. First, we design a deterministic Riccati observer cascaded with a complementary filter, ensuring almost-global asymptotic stability (AGAS) under a uniform observability (UO) condition while preserving the geometric structure of the attitude dynamics. Second, we propose a nonlinear observer evolving on SO(3)xR2, which integrates IMU measurements as inputs and barometer and magnetometer measurements as outputs within a unified framework, guaranteeing local exponential stability (LES) under relaxed uniform observability conditions. The proposed approaches are validated using both simulated and real flight data. The results demonstrate that barometer-aided estimation provides a lightweight, reliable, and effective complementary sensing modality for attitude estimation in minimal-sensing configurations, offering a practical alternative when conventional velocity measurements are unavailable or degraded.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces barometer-aided attitude estimation for autonomous vehicles using nonlinear observers on SO(3), validated with simulated and real flight data.
AI.motivation: Accurate attitude estimation is challenging in GNSS-denied and highly accelerated flight conditions, where IMUs alone cannot distinguish gravitational from inertial accelerations, and auxiliary velocity sensors may be unavailable or costly.
AI.method: Two approaches: (1) a deterministic Riccati observer cascaded with a complementary filter ensuring AGAS; (2) a nonlinear observer on SO(3)×R² integrating IMU, barometer, and magnetometer measurements, guaranteeing LES under relaxed conditions.
AI.result: Validated on simulated and real flight data; barometer-aided estimation provides lightweight, reliable, and effective complementary sensing for attitude estimation.
AI.conclusion: Barometer-aided attitude estimation offers a practical alternative for minimal-sensing configurations when conventional velocity measurements are unavailable or degraded.

title: STKAN: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks for Spatio-Temporal Forecasting
summary: Real-world traffic data exhibit heterogeneous spatial correlations and nonlinear temporal dynamics, posing substantial challenges for accurate spatio-temporal forecasting. Existing approaches have developed increasingly sophisticated graph, attention, and decomposition architectures, while the influence of the underlying nonlinear function approximator has received comparatively less attention. In this work, we propose STKAN, a spatio-temporal forecasting architecture that introduces Taylor-polynomial Kolmogorov--Arnold Network modules into spatial and temporal token mixing. STKAN first constructs high-level spatial representations through a learnable soft node-group assignment mechanism, applies group-wise spatial mixing, and subsequently models temporal dependencies over the compressed sequence. Spatial and temporal self-attention layers are further employed to capture long-range interactions. Experiments on five traffic forecasting benchmarks show that STKAN achieves competitive performance and performs better than the evaluated MLP-based variant in the tested settings. These results suggest that the design of nonlinear function approximators can serve as a useful complement to architectural design in spatio-temporal forecasting.

AI.tldr: STKAN introduces Taylor-polynomial KAN modules for spatial and temporal mixing in spatio-temporal forecasting, achieving competitive results on traffic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing spatio-temporal forecasting methods focus on complex graph, attention, and decomposition architectures but overlook the impact of the underlying nonlinear function approximator on performance.
AI.method: STKAN uses learnable soft node-group assignment for spatial representations, group-wise spatial mixing via Taylor-polynomial KAN, temporal modeling over compressed sequences, and self-attention for long-range interactions.
AI.result: STKAN achieves competitive performance on five traffic forecasting benchmarks and outperforms the MLP-based variant in tested settings.
AI.conclusion: Designing better nonlinear function approximators can be a useful complement to architectural innovation in spatio-temporal forecasting.

title: CayleyR: Solving the TopSpin puzzle via cycle intersection
summary: We present cayleyR, an R package for solving permutation puzzles by detecting cycle intersections in Cayley graphs. The core algorithm performs an iterative bidirectional search: from both the initial and target permutation states, random operation sequences generate cycles in the Cayley graph of the symmetric group Sn; their intersection yields a connecting path. When no direct intersection is found, a distance-guided bridge selection narrows the gap, and the process repeats. The package targets the TopSpin(n,k) puzzle, whose state space is a Cayley graph of Sn generated by a cyclic shift and a prefix reversal. We describe the mathematical framework, the algorithm, and its implementation, which combines a C++ hash-indexed state store with optional Vulkan GPU acceleration. The software is publicly available on CRAN.

AI.tldr: cayleyR is an R package that solves permutation puzzles (like TopSpin) using bidirectional search on Cayley graphs, detecting cycle intersections between random operation sequences from start and goal states, with C++ backend and optional GPU acceleration.
AI.motivation: To provide an efficient, accessible computational tool for solving permutation puzzles by exploiting the algebraic structure of Cayley graphs, specifically targeting the TopSpin(n,k) puzzle.
AI.method: Iterative bidirectional search: (1) generate random operation sequences from both initial and target states to create cycles in the Cayley graph, (2) detect intersections between these cycles to find a connecting path, (3) if no intersection found, use distance-guided bridge selection to reduce the gap, (4) repeat. Implemented in R with C++ hash-indexed state store and optional Vulkan GPU acceleration.
AI.result: A publicly available CRAN R package (cayleyR) that successfully solves TopSpin(n,k) permutation puzzles using the described bidirectional cycle-intersection algorithm.
AI.conclusion: The cayleyR package demonstrates a practical algorithmic approach combining Cayley graph theory, bidirectional search, and cycle intersection detection for solving permutation puzzles, with performance benefits from C++ and GPU acceleration.

title: On the Blockage Effect in Pinching-Antenna Systems (PASS)
summary: Pinching-antenna systems (PASS) offer considerable potential for wireless communications due to their unique ability to dynamically reconfigure radiation positions along a waveguide. However, the performance of PASS remains a critical challenge in the presence of random line-of-sight (LoS) blockage, leading to significant attenuation and even communication outages. In this paper, the performance of PASS in the presence of LoS blockage is investigated from the perspective of stochastic geometry. Obstacles are modeled through a homogeneous Poisson point process (PPP), where the geometric dimensions, numbers, and positions are treated as random variables. To conduct a concrete characterization of LoS blockage, the random-height-and-random-radius (RHRR) blockage model and the deterministic-height-and-deterministic-radius (DHDR) blockage model are proposed. In particular, closed-form analytical and asymptotic expressions for the outage probability are obtained, along with analytical and approximate expressions for the ergodic rate. Our simulation results reveal that denser obstacle environments or statistically larger obstacles substantially increase the probability of LoS blockage and degrade the system performance. Moreover, owing to its ability to dynamically reposition PAs, PASS can consistently outperform conventional antenna systems in the presence of LoS blockage.

AI.tldr: This paper uses stochastic geometry to analyze pinching-antenna systems (PASS) under random line-of-sight blockage, proposing RHRR and DHDR blockage models, deriving closed-form outage probability and ergodic rate expressions, and showing PASS outperforms conventional antennas despite denser/larger obstacles.
AI.motivation: PASS offer dynamic radiation position reconfiguration but face performance degradation due to random LoS blockage; the paper aims to analytically characterize this performance loss.
AI.method: Models obstacles as a homogeneous PPP; proposes two blockage models (RHRR and DHDR); derives closed-form analytical and asymptotic expressions for outage probability and ergodic rate using stochastic geometry.
AI.result: Denser obstacle environments or statistically larger obstacles increase LoS blockage probability and degrade performance; PASS consistently outperforms conventional antenna systems under LoS blockage.
AI.conclusion: PASS is robust against LoS blockage due to dynamic repositioning capability, and the proposed analytical framework effectively quantifies its performance advantages.

title: Square-Root Law for Covert Communication with Warden-Favorable Side Information
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Communications
summary: Covert communication enables Alice to transmit to Bob while making the transmission difficult for Willie to detect. We study a scalar Gaussian covert-overlay model in which Alice's low-power covert signal is superimposed on an aggregate public component generated by Alice or other trackable sources. Willie is given all physically obtainable side information, including protocol details, timing, pilots, channel estimates, and calibration information, and subtracts his best estimate of the public component before testing. Covertness is imposed on the resulting residual through a relative-entropy constraint with budget $δ$ conditioned on Willie's side information. In the stationary case, the residual under no covert transmission has variance $σ_0^2=σ_W^2+σ_e^2$, where $σ_W^2$ is Willie's receiver-noise variance and $σ_e^2$ is the irreducible cancellation error. Over $n$ channel uses, the maximal reliably transmissible covert payload is $R_C^\star\sqrt{n}(1+o(1))$ bits, where $R_C^\star=\frac{σ_0^2}{σ_B^2\ln 2}\sqrtδ$, and $σ_B^2$ is Bob's receiver-noise variance. Thus, the square-root-law (SRL) constant is governed by the variance at Willie's actual detector input, not by receiver noise alone. Low-power Gaussian signaling achieves this constant, and a matching converse establishes first-order optimality within the conditioned additive Gaussian innovation model. For known time-varying conditioned residual variances, we also derive the first-order allocation, which assigns more covert power to larger residual variances. The results require a Gaussian post-cancellation null residual with known conditioned variance; non-Gaussian residuals and fixed non-vanishing variance uncertainty are outside the scope of this paper.

AI.tldr: This paper studies scalar Gaussian covert-overlay communication, where Alice's covert signal is hidden within a public component. Covertness is enforced via a relative-entropy constraint conditioned on Willie's side information. The maximal covert payload follows a square-root law with a constant governed by the total variance at Willie's detector (including cancellation error), not just receiver noise. Low-power Gaussian signaling achieves optimality, and time-varying residuals are handled via optimal power allocation.
AI.motivation: To establish fundamental limits and optimal strategies for covert communication when Willie has access to all physically obtainable side information and can subtract estimated public components, focusing on the practical overlay scenario where covert and public signals coexist.
AI.method: The paper models the residual after Willie's cancellation as a Gaussian innovation with known conditioned variance. Covertness is measured via conditional relative-entropy (KL divergence) budget δ. The analysis derives the maximal covert payload scaling (square-root law), identifies the optimal constant, and proves matching achievability (low-power Gaussian signaling) and converse results. For time-varying variances, a first-order optimal power allocation strategy is provided.
AI.result: The maximal covert payload is R_C^* √n (1+o(1)) bits, where R_C^* = (σ_0^2)/(σ_B^2 ln 2) √δ, with σ_0^2 = σ_W^2 + σ_e^2 (Willie's receiver noise plus cancellation error). Low-power Gaussian signaling achieves this constant, and the converse proves first-order optimality. For time-varying residuals, optimal allocation assigns more covert power to larger residual variances.
AI.conclusion: The square-root-law constant for covert communication depends on the total variance at Willie's detector input (including cancellation errors), not just receiver noise. The results hold under Gaussian post-cancellation residual with known conditioned variance; non-Gaussian residuals or fixed variance uncertainty are outside scope.

title: Differentiable Polarized Path Tracing
summary: Physically based differentiable rendering has proven to be a powerful tool for inverse rendering problems (e.g., 3D reconstruction, reflectance estimation, lighting estimation). However, most existing methods operate solely on radiometric intensity, discarding valuable polarization cues that constrain scene geometry and material properties. While forward simulation of polarized light is well-defined via Mueller-Stokes calculus, extending reverse-mode differentiation to this domain presents significant challenges. The rank-deficient nature of common polarimetric operators, such as linear polarizers and diffuse reflections, violates the invertibility assumptions of standard gradient estimators like path replay backpropagation and results in numerical instability. We address this by proposing a robust, polarization-aware differentiable path tracing method. Our approach estimates unbiased gradients through a combination of path replay and local caching. This formulation enables efficient and stable optimization of material and lighting parameters in complex scenes, broadening the applicability of physically based inverse rendering. Project page: https://vcai.mpi-inf.mpg.de/projects/DPPT/

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a polarization-aware differentiable path tracing method that handles rank-deficient polarimetric operators (like linear polarizers and diffuse reflections) using path replay with local caching, enabling stable gradient estimation for inverse rendering problems involving polarization cues.
AI.motivation: Existing differentiable rendering methods operate only on radiometric intensity, discarding valuable polarization cues that provide constraints on scene geometry and material properties. Standard gradient estimators fail due to the rank-deficient nature of common polarimetric operators violating invertibility assumptions.
AI.method: The method combines path replay backpropagation with local caching to estimate unbiased gradients through polarization-aware differentiable path tracing, handling rank-deficient polarimetric operators without numerical instability.
AI.result: The approach enables efficient and stable optimization of material and lighting parameters in complex scenes, demonstrating robust gradient estimation where standard methods fail.
AI.conclusion: This work broadens the applicability of physically based inverse rendering by incorporating polarization information through a robust differentiable rendering framework, opening new possibilities for inverse rendering with polarization cues.

title: Parsimonious disturbance-aware minimum-time planning with parametric uncertainty
summary: This study presents and validates a minimum-lap-time planning (MLTP) framework for motorsport applications that embeds robustness against both state disturbances and parameter uncertainty. The methodology builds upon a prior disturbance-aware framework that, at each track point, propagates stochastic vehicle dynamics over a short horizon and tightens tyre-friction constraints based on the worst-case scenario at horizon end. We extend the formulation to account for uncertainty in key vehicle parameters: moment of inertia, centre-of-mass position, and aerodynamic drag coefficient. To keep the extended formulation computationally tractable, a spatially selective, parsimonious activation strategy confines the robust constraints to the circuit segments where they are most critical. We demonstrate the improved driveability of the robust references by employing a model predictive controller (MPC) as a virtual test driver. For each reference, the same MPC drives a simulated FSAE (Formula SAE) car over 1000 runs on a representative Barcelona-Catalunya sector, with randomly realised impulsive disturbances and parameter scatter. We compare a nominal reference, planned without robustness, against its robust counterparts. The latter yield consistently fewer failed runs and, at a moderate sector-time cost, show tighter dispersion of key signals (vehicle inputs, axle saturations) around the reference values, evidence of better trackability.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a robust minimum-lap-time planning framework for motorsport that handles both state disturbances and parameter uncertainty, using a selective activation strategy for computational efficiency, and demonstrates through 1000 MPC-driven simulations on an FSAE car that robust references significantly reduce failures and improve trackability vs. nominal planning.
AI.motivation: Existing minimum-lap-time planning lacks robustness against state disturbances AND parameter uncertainty (e.g., inertia, center-of-mass, drag). The authors aim to extend a prior disturbance-aware framework to handle both sources of uncertainty while keeping computation tractable.
AI.method: Extends a disturbance-aware MLTP by incorporating parameter uncertainty (moment of inertia, CoM position, drag coefficient) into stochastic propagation. Uses a spatially selective, parsimonious activation strategy to apply robust constraints only where critical. Validated via an MPC virtual test driver over 1000 runs with random disturbances and parameter scatter.
AI.result: Robust references yield consistently fewer failed runs than nominal references, with tighter dispersion of vehicle inputs and axle saturations around reference values, at a moderate sector-time cost. This demonstrates improved trackability and driveability.
AI.conclusion: Embedding robustness against both state and parameter uncertainty in MLTP significantly enhances reliability and trackability for motorsport applications, and the selective activation strategy makes it computationally feasible.

title: A Hybrid Mamba for Audio-Visual Navigation
comment: Main paper (6 pages). Accepted for publication by IEEE International Conference on Systems and Man and Cybernetics 2026 (IEEE SMC 2026)
summary: Since the paradigm centered on convolutional neural networks and recurrent architectures was established in 2020, the fundamental backbone networks for audio-visual navigation have undergone no essential changes for more than five years, making them inadequate to support efficient representation of dynamic multimodal sequences. This paper proposes Samba(A Hybrid Mamba for Audio-Visual Navigation). It uses the adaptive selection-enabled Mamba State Encoder (M-SE) to replace conventional GRUs for temporal aggregation, and constructs an Audio Mamba Encoder (AME) to remedy the limitations of convolutional operators in capturing global time-frequency dependencies in spectrograms. Experiments demonstrate that Samba exhibits exceptional generalization performance when facing unheard sound sources and unseen scenes. On the Matterport3D dataset, it improves the navigation success rate (SR) by 11.3\% compared with existing state-of-the-art models, and the performance gain is even more pronounced on the Replica dataset, which features finer scene structures. Such modernized architectural reconstruction unlocks stronger embodied representation capabilities at a lower computational cost, thereby providing a highly robust technical pathway for paradigm evolution in the field of audio-visual navigation.

AI.tldr: Samba replaces traditional GRU and CNN backbones in audio-visual navigation with Mamba-based encoders (M-SE and AME), achieving 11.3% higher success rate on Matterport3D and stronger generalization with lower computational cost.
AI.motivation: CNN/RNN-based navigation backbones have remained unchanged since 2020 and cannot efficiently represent dynamic multimodal sequences.
AI.method: Replaces GRUs with adaptive Mamba State Encoder (M-SE) for temporal aggregation, and uses Audio Mamba Encoder (AME) to capture global time-frequency dependencies in spectrograms, overcoming CNN limitations.
AI.result: Improves navigation success rate by 11.3% on Matterport3D; performs even better on Replica with finer scene structures; shows strong generalization to unseen sounds and scenes.
AI.conclusion: Modernized Mamba-based architectural reconstruction unlocks stronger embodied representation at lower cost, providing a robust pathway for audio-visual navigation paradigm evolution.

title: Networked Intelligence: Active Shared Context Graphs for Human-AI Team Science
summary: Most AI-for-science systems focus on scaling a single reasoning process through better models, larger context windows, long-horizon agentic execution, or digital co-scientists working with one principal user. However, challenging scientific problems are rarely solved by one reasoner alone. They are solved by teams whose members bring different priors, experimental backgrounds, tacit knowledge, and domain-trained intuitions. The open problem is therefore not only how to scale models, but how to cultivate networked intelligence: scaling the connections between humans and AI systems so that a result or hypothesis produced in one context reaches another person, agent, instrument, or robot that can act on it. We introduce Mycelium, an active shared workspace that automatically connects researchers and AI agents as a multi-user co-scientist. As human users and agents work, the system captures important observations and hypotheses, tracks how they relate to the team's evolving model, and routes them to the person or agent whose next decision they can inform. We evaluate Mycelium in its first empirical test, a biological multi-omics campaign in which routed shared context turned a local analytical finding into a cross-expert mechanistic constraint and ultimately into an experimental design. We also give networked intelligence a computational account as sparse conditional computation over distributed scientific contexts. This account distinguishes when a scaled standalone agent can match the network from when independent expertise and non-mergeable contexts make the network irreducible.

AI.tldr: Mycelium is an active shared workspace that connects multiple researchers and AI agents as a multi-user co-scientist, enabling networked intelligence by capturing, tracking, and routing scientific observations and hypotheses across team members. A biological multi-omics case study shows it transforms local findings into cross-expert mechanistic insights and experimental designs.
AI.motivation: Challenging scientific problems are solved by diverse teams, not single reasoners. The open problem is not scaling individual models but cultivating networked intelligence—connecting humans and AI systems so that discoveries in one context reach others who can act on them.
AI.method: Mycelium is an active shared workspace that automatically captures important observations and hypotheses as users and agents work, tracks their relationships to the team's evolving model, and routes relevant context to the person or agent whose next decision it can inform.
AI.result: In a biological multi-omics campaign, routed shared context turned a local analytical finding into a cross-expert mechanistic constraint and ultimately into an experimental design. Networked intelligence is formalized as sparse conditional computation over distributed scientific contexts.
AI.conclusion: Standalone agents can sometimes match networked intelligence, but independent expertise and non-mergeable contexts make the network irreducible in many cases. The computational account distinguishes when each scenario applies.

title: RIS Beamforming under Element-Level Variations: Statistical Characterization and Robust Design
summary: In this paper, a novel analytical framework to characterize the impact of element-level variations on the radiation characteristics of reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) is introduced. Specifically, a statistical model is proposed to capture the effects of varactor capacitance fluctuations on the RIS reflection coefficients, and, subsequently, on the resulting power radiation pattern; both low- and large-variance independent perturbation scenarios, are investigated. Leveraging the proposed statistical model, a low complexity greedy optimization methodology is presented, having the goal to optimize the expected RIS radiation power, thereby, generating inherently robust configurations. Furthermore, the analytical proposed model serves as an efficient alternative to computationally expensive Monte Carlo simulations, enabling the quantification of element sensitivity to manufacturing and operational tolerances. As demonstrated, optimizing the mean power pattern significantly enhances system performance under element-level variations. For typical RIS sizes (e.g., 32x32 or 64x64), a main lobe gain exceeding 2 dB and a sidelobe suppression of approximately 10 dB are achieved.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a statistical model to analyze how element-level variations (like varactor capacitance fluctuations) affect RIS radiation patterns, and proposes a low-complexity greedy optimization for robust configurations that achieves >2 dB gain and ~10 dB sidelobe suppression.
AI.motivation: To characterize and mitigate the impact of manufacturing/operational tolerances and element-level variations on RIS radiation performance, replacing costly Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.method: Proposes a statistical model for varactor capacitance fluctuations affecting RIS reflection coefficients, followed by a low-complexity greedy optimization to maximize expected radiation power under both low- and large-variance perturbation scenarios.
AI.result: For 32x32 or 64x64 RIS, the method achieves >2 dB main lobe gain improvement and ~10 dB sidelobe suppression under element-level variations.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing the mean power pattern significantly enhances RIS robustness to element-level variations, and the analytical model efficiently replaces Monte Carlo simulations for sensitivity quantification.

title: FOLIO: Focused Semantic Memory for Streaming Video Understanding
summary: In online streaming video understanding, a video stream continues to arrive and queries may be issued at any time. Because streaming frames grow without bound, the system must continuously compress and retain information from the observed video prefix while future frames and future queries remain unknown. The core challenge is deciding what information to retain and how to organize the maintained history: as this history grows with the stream, memory cost increases and many redundant visual details are retained, whereas later queries often depend on specific entities, actions, and their temporal changes. To address this challenge, we introduce FOLIO, a training-free focused semantic memory system that records important parts of the stream in higher detail while keeping surrounding context compact. As the stream arrives, FOLIO updates memory at the segment level, guided by a dynamic focus state, combining a short-term visual buffer with a long-term semantic memory organized around observed entities and linked to a visual-evidence cache. At query time, lightweight hybrid retrieval combines direct matching over the structured memory with semantic query expansion. FOLIO achieves state-of-the-art performance, reaching 82.0/69.1 Perception/Backward accuracy on OVO-Bench with Qwen3-VL-8B and 74.5 overall accuracy on StreamingBench, while substantially reducing the cost of maintaining streaming memory by reserving detailed records for focused entities and storing surrounding context compactly.

AI.tldr: FOLIO is a training-free semantic memory system for streaming video that dynamically retains focused entity details while compacting context, achieving SOTA results on OVO-Bench and StreamingBench.
AI.motivation: Streaming video understanding faces unbounded frame growth; retaining all history is memory-inefficient, yet later queries often depend on specific entities, actions, and temporal changes, creating a need for selective, structured memory.
AI.method: FOLIO uses a dynamic focus state, a short-term visual buffer, and a long-term semantic memory organized around entities with a visual-evidence cache. At query time, lightweight hybrid retrieval combines direct matching over structured memory with semantic query expansion.
AI.result: 82.0/69.1 Perception/Backward accuracy on OVO-Bench with Qwen3-VL-8B, and 74.5 overall accuracy on StreamingBench, with substantially reduced memory cost.
AI.conclusion: Training-free selective memory compression focused on entities is effective and efficient for streaming video understanding, achieving SOTA while reducing memory overhead.

title: Adapting Generalist Vehicle Models for High-Speed MPC Across Terrains
summary: High-speed off-road autonomy requires precise closed-loop control for a target vehicle while remaining robust across changing terrains. Recent forward kinodynamic (FKD) prediction foundation models suggest a promising path, starting from a generalist model and specializing it to the target platform. However, effective specialization remains challenging, as it often requires substantial real-world data, and models adapted to one setting can still overfit to specific terrains or driving regimes. We present OptCar (Optimized Car), a recipe for bridging the gap from generalist to specialist FKD models that preserves cross-terrain generalization while optimizing performance for a specific vehicle. $\texttt{OptCar}$ introduces a history-conditioned dynamics adaptation module that encodes recent state-action observations into a dynamics context token, and then fine-tunes the generalist model using limited real-world data together with targeted synthetic rollouts from environment-specific system identification. In closed-loop model predictive control (MPC) experiments across three terrains and an out-of-distribution cart-pulling task, the largest gains appear at 6~m/s, the highest speed evaluated and the regime in which slip dominates tracking error. On vegetation and dirt, the most slip-diverse terrain, OptCar reduces 6~m/s trajectory tracking error by roughly 55% relative to a fine-tuned AnyCar baseline, and remains the most accurate even when an unseen cart payload changes the dynamics. With only 5 minutes of real data per terrain, OptCar is competitive on road with a specialist trained on 30 minutes of road data, and substantially outperforms it once the terrain changes.

AI.tldr: OptCar is a recipe for efficiently specializing generalist forward kinodynamic prediction models to specific off-road vehicles using minimal real-world data (5 minutes per terrain) and targeted synthetic rollouts, achieving ~55% trajectory tracking error reduction at high speeds on slip-diverse terrains while maintaining cross-terrain generalization.
AI.motivation: Generalist FKD prediction models require substantial real-world data to specialize to target platforms, and adapted models can overfit to specific terrains or driving regimes, losing cross-terrain generalization. A method is needed to bridge the generalist-to-specialist gap efficiently.
AI.method: OptCar introduces: (1) a history-conditioned dynamics adaptation module that encodes recent state-action observations into a dynamics context token, (2) fine-tuning the generalist model using limited real-world data combined with targeted synthetic rollouts from environment-specific system identification.
AI.result: At 6 m/s (highest speed evaluated), OptCar reduces trajectory tracking error by ~55% on vegetation and dirt compared to fine-tuned AnyCar baseline. With only 5 minutes of real data per terrain, it matches a specialist trained on 30 minutes of road data, and substantially outperforms it on changed terrains. It also remains robust to unseen cart-payload dynamics changes.
AI.conclusion: OptCar effectively bridges the generalist-to-specialist gap for FKD models, preserving cross-terrain generalization while optimizing performance for a specific vehicle using minimal real-world data.

title: CoDiffGRN: Rethinking Gene Regulatory Network Inference via the BEELINE-KGC Benchmark and Co-evolutionary Discrete Diffusion
summary: Inferring gene regulatory networks (GRNs) from single-cell transcriptomic data is crucial for biological discovery, yet existing approaches suffer from a fundamental misalignment with real-world needs. Researchers typically seek a small set of high-confidence regulatory interactions for experimental validation, often involving previously unseen genes. However, current benchmarks rely on transductive splits with global classification metrics, while prevailing models struggle to generalize under inductive settings. To bridge this gap, we reformulate GRN inference as an inductive, ranking-centric graph completion problem and introduce \textbf{\benchmark}, a new benchmark that incorporates an inductive gene-holdout split together with knowledge graph completion metrics to better evaluate top-ranked predictions. Building on this, we propose \textbf{\method}, the first co-evolutionary discrete diffusion framework that jointly models biologically coherent discretized gene expression states and regulatory interactions for robust inductive generalization and improved top-ranked regulatory discovery. We further introduce TF-ALL Subgraph Sampling (TASS) for scalable training. Extensive experiments on {\benchmark} show that {\method} establishes new state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing methods in novel regulatory discovery, and ablation studies further verify the effectiveness of our design.

AI.tldr: The paper reformulates gene regulatory network inference as an inductive ranking problem and proposes a co-evolutionary discrete diffusion model that outperforms existing methods in discovering novel regulatory interactions from single-cell data.
AI.motivation: Existing GRN inference methods misalign with real-world needs: researchers want high-confidence novel regulatory interactions, but current benchmarks use transductive splits and global metrics that fail to measure generalization to unseen genes.
AI.method: The authors introduce a new benchmark with inductive gene-holdout splits and knowledge graph completion metrics. They propose a co-evolutionary discrete diffusion framework that jointly models discretized gene expression states and regulatory interactions, along with TF-ALL Subgraph Sampling for scalability.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the new benchmark, significantly outperforming existing methods in novel regulatory discovery, with ablation studies confirming the effectiveness of each design component.
AI.conclusion: Reformulating GRN inference as an inductive ranking problem and using co-evolutionary diffusion modeling enables more biologically meaningful and practically useful regulatory network discovery.

title: AI-Native Insurance for Agentic AI: Pricing, Underwriting, and End-to-End Automation
summary: Agentic AI introduces new insurance challenges because autonomous AI systems can make decisions, invoke tools, modify external environments, and interact with third-party services. This paper develops an AI-native mathematical framework for underwriting, pricing, and contract design for agentic AI deployments. A deployment is represented by a risk state that captures autonomy level, operational authority, permission exposure, governance maturity, and dependency concentration. The framework maps the risk state to event probabilities, loss severities, governance costs, premiums, deductibles, coverage allocation, and policy covenants, and formulates an optimization problem for insurance contract design under participation, profitability, and incentive compatibility constraints. The paper establishes structural properties of insurability, including characterization of an insurability region, monotone deterioration of feasibility with increasing exposure, and governance certification thresholds. Insurance is further interpreted as both an operational cost and a regulatory mechanism for AI deployment. A healthcare case study illustrates contract optimization, sensitivity analysis, and automated claims processing for agentic AI systems.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an AI-native mathematical framework for underwriting, pricing, and designing insurance contracts specifically for agentic AI deployments, addressing the unique risks posed by autonomous AI systems that can make decisions, interact with environments, and invoke third-party services.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI systems introduce novel insurance challenges due to their autonomous decision-making, tool invocation, and external environment interaction capabilities, for which traditional insurance frameworks are inadequate.
AI.method: The authors represent AI deployments as a risk state capturing autonomy level, operational authority, permission exposure, governance maturity, and dependency concentration. They map this state to event probabilities, loss severities, governance costs, and insurance contract parameters, then formulate an optimization problem under participation, profitability, and incentive compatibility constraints.
AI.result: The framework establishes structural properties of insurability, including an insurability region, monotone deterioration of feasibility with increasing exposure, and governance certification thresholds. A healthcare case study demonstrates contract optimization and sensitivity analysis.
AI.conclusion: Insurance for agentic AI can serve both as an operational cost and a regulatory mechanism, with a formal mathematical foundation for contract design that preserves incentive compatibility.

title: M3F-UAV: A Missing-Modality Multimodal Foundation Model for Low-Altitude Wireless Sensing
summary: Low-altitude unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are emerging as key platforms for wireless intelligence tasks. However, practical low-altitude wireless systems usually operate in complex urban environments, where visual occlusion, sparse geometric observations, multipath propagation, and sensor failures may degrade the reliability of single-modality models. To address these challenges, this paper proposes M3F-UAV, a missing-modality multimodal foundation model for low-altitude wireless sensing. The proposed framework learns a unified multimodal representation from visual, geometric, and wireless observations. Specifically, modality-specific pretrained feature extractors are adopted for RGB/depth images, LiDAR point clouds, and CSI matrices, respectively. Through cross-modal fusion and missing-modality-aware pretraining with feature-level masked reconstruction and UAV localization objectives, M3F-UAV can extract fixed-size features from different modality combinations and adapt them to downstream low-altitude wireless tasks with lightweight task heads. Experiments on the LAMBDA dataset show that M3F-UAV outperforms single-modality baselines and maintains robust performance under missing-modality settings.

AI.tldr: M3F-UAV is a missing-modality multimodal foundation model for low-altitude wireless sensing that fuses visual, geometric, and wireless data, achieving robust performance even when some modalities are unavailable.
AI.motivation: Low-altitude UAV wireless systems in urban environments suffer from visual occlusion, sparse geometry, multipath propagation, and sensor failures, which degrade single-modality model reliability.
AI.method: M3F-UAV uses modality-specific pretrained feature extractors (RGB/depth, LiDAR, CSI), cross-modal fusion, and missing-modality-aware pretraining with masked reconstruction and UAV localization objectives to learn unified multimodal representations adaptable to downstream tasks.
AI.result: On the LAMBDA dataset, M3F-UAV outperforms single-modality baselines and maintains robust performance under missing-modality conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multimodal approach effectively handles missing modalities in complex urban low-altitude scenarios, enhancing the reliability of UAV-based wireless sensing.

title: A VAE-Driven Multi-Task Satellite-Aided Semantic Communication Framework for 6G-Enabled Connected Autonomous Vehicles
summary: The development of smart transportation systems and the introduction of 6G wireless communication technologies have significantly changed vehicle network topologies. Future connected autonomous vehicle (CAV) networks require bandwidth-efficient, reliable, and low-latency communication for safety-critical applications such as traffic sign recognition and decision-making. Conventional communication systems transmit raw data regardless of task relevance, which is inefficient in resource-constrained satellite channels where uplink bandwidth is scarce and propagation losses are large. Semantic communication addresses this limitation by transmitting task-relevant information instead of full signal representations. It extracts and conveys essential semantic features and leverages deep learning to optimize task performance at the receiver. Therefore, we present a Variational Autoencoder (VAE)-based multi-task semantic communication framework for satellite-assisted autonomous driving. Unlike deterministic autoencoder-based methods, the proposed model uses probabilistic latent representations for more robust and efficient encoding. The learned features are transmitted over noisy wireless channels to perform traffic sign reconstruction and classification. The framework is trained end-to-end to jointly optimize both tasks. Results show that the proposed approach achieves significant bandwidth reduction of up to 87.23\% to 98.17\% while maintaining stable performance across varying signal-to-noise ratio conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a VAE-based multi-task semantic communication framework for satellite-assisted autonomous driving, achieving 87-98% bandwidth reduction while maintaining stable traffic sign reconstruction and classification performance across varying SNR conditions.
AI.motivation: Conventional communication systems transmit raw data regardless of task relevance, which is inefficient in resource-constrained satellite channels with scarce uplink bandwidth and large propagation losses. Semantic communication addresses this by transmitting only task-relevant information.
AI.method: The authors present a Variational Autoencoder (VAE)-based multi-task semantic communication framework that uses probabilistic latent representations (unlike deterministic autoencoders) for robust and efficient encoding. The framework is trained end-to-end to jointly optimize traffic sign reconstruction and classification tasks over noisy wireless channels.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves significant bandwidth reduction of 87.23% to 98.17% while maintaining stable performance across varying signal-to-noise ratio conditions.
AI.conclusion: The VAE-based semantic communication framework effectively balances bandwidth efficiency and task performance for satellite-assisted autonomous driving, demonstrating robustness under noisy channel conditions.

title: Improving Medical Image Generative Models with Fréchet Distance Loss
summary: Diffusion generative models have demonstrated immense potential for synthetic medical image generation. However, these models often struggle to capture complex morphological characteristics of heterogeneous tumors with irregular boundaries, limiting their utility for downstream clinical tasks such as segmentation. This limitation stems from the standard denoising objective: minimizing a per-pixel error, which smooths high-variance irregular structures characteristic of tumors. To address this, we propose finetuning these generative models with Fréchet Distance loss (FD-loss). FD-loss aligns the first and second order feature statistics of real and generated images in a pretrained encoder space, encouraging the generator to capture complex structural variations characteristic of heterogeneous tumors. We integrate FD-loss across diverse architectural settings, using both natural- and medical-image encoders on multiple liver and brain cancer datasets spanning CT and MRI modalities. Downstream segmentation networks trained on our FD-regularized synthetic data consistently achieve superior performance, improving tumor DSC by $>$$5\%$ over unregularized synthetic augmentation alone. Qualitative analysis suggests these gains are associated with more faithful tumor synthesis and fewer segmentation hallucinations. Our results show FD-loss as an effective regularizer for medical image generative models to improve clinical workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes FD-loss to fine-tune diffusion models for better medical image generation of heterogeneous tumors, improving downstream segmentation by over 5% DSC.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models struggle to capture complex morphological characteristics of heterogeneous tumors with irregular boundaries due to the standard per-pixel denoising objective that smooths high-variance structures.
AI.method: Fine-tuning generative diffusion models with Fréchet Distance loss (FD-loss), which aligns first and second order feature statistics of real and generated images in a pretrained encoder space.
AI.result: Downstream segmentation networks trained on FD-regularized synthetic data improve tumor DSC by >5% over unregularized synthetic augmentation, with more faithful tumor synthesis and fewer segmentation hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: FD-loss is an effective regularizer for medical image generative models to improve clinical workflows.

title: Design and Characterization of a Limb Encircling Actuator
summary: Lower-limb powered exoskeletons have demonstrated substantial improvements in mobility and function, but most designs place actuation components lateral to the legs or remotely at the waist or back. These configurations often extend beyond the body's natural envelope, making devices intrusive in everyday use and potentially limiting societal adoption. We posit that rethinking actuation geometry could enable exoskeletons that conform more closely to the body. Here, we explored an actuation layout in which the actuator encircles the limb in a plane orthogonal to the limb axis, potentially reducing its spatial footprint around the body. We developed the Limb-Encircling Actuator (LEA) and characterized its electromechanical properties using a custom-built testbed. The LEA also features a novel radial bearing layout with potential as a lightweight or lower-cost alternative to traditional large-diameter bearings. The actuator achieved a continuous torque density of 7.5 Nm/kg with a mass of 894 g. Despite this high torque density and innovative layout, the system remained difficult to contain close to the body. These results highlight opportunities and challenges in limb-encircling actuation and provide insights into torque-dense exoskeleton designs that could integrate more readily into everyday apparel if challenges in actuator sizing and geometry are overcome.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a Limb-Encircling Actuator (LEA) for exoskeletons that wraps around the limb to reduce spatial footprint, achieving 7.5 Nm/kg torque density but facing challenges in keeping the system contained close to the body.
AI.motivation: Current lower-limb exoskeletons place actuation components lateral to the legs or at the waist/back, extending beyond the body's natural envelope and making devices intrusive for everyday use, potentially limiting societal adoption.
AI.method: The authors designed a Limb-Encircling Actuator (LEA) with actuation geometry that encircles the limb in a plane orthogonal to the limb axis, featuring a novel radial bearing layout as a lightweight alternative to traditional bearings. They characterized its electromechanical properties using a custom-built testbed.
AI.result: The LEA achieved a continuous torque density of 7.5 Nm/kg with a mass of 894 g. However, despite high torque density and innovative layout, the system remained difficult to contain close to the body.
AI.conclusion: Limb-encircling actuation offers opportunities for torque-dense exoskeletons that could integrate into everyday apparel, but challenges in actuator sizing and geometry must be overcome before practical adoption.

title: ShortOPD: Recovering Pruned LLMs with Short-to-Long On-Policy Distillation
summary: Structured pruning is a hardware-friendly way to compress LLMs, but it is mostly validated on multiple-choice recognition tasks, while the same compressed checkpoints can collapse on the free-form generation that deployment actually requires. Two observations trace this gap. First, greedy \textsc{pass}@$1$ nearly vanishes after compression, yet \textsc{pass}@$k$ recovers substantially under repeated sampling: useful generations are demoted, not erased. Second, the recoverable regime fails mainly through suffix repetition. Recovery should therefore train on the compressed model's own on-policy states with dense token-level supervision, which On-Policy Distillation (OPD) provides by reusing the pre-compression model as a frozen teacher. However, long on-policy rollouts spend early recovery budget on low-information repetitive suffixes, delaying loss descent. To mitigate this waste, we propose \textbf{\shortopd}, a short-to-long OPD schedule that detects teacher-confirmed repetitive suffixes, treats the surviving prefix as each rollout's effective length, and allocates future rollout budgets to the effective lengths the policy can currently use. Across math, code, and open-ended generation, \shortopd\ raises the compressed model's score to about $9\times$ its unrecovered value and $1.6$--$4.4\times$ standard recovery recipes (SFT w/o KD, KD, and SeqKD), and it matches a fixed $8192$-token rollout horizon within two points using a quarter of the training time ($8.5$ vs.\ $35.9$ hours) and $71\%$ fewer rollout tokens. We hope this recipe helps move structured pruning beyond marginal gains on perplexity and multiple-choice benchmarks, a step closer to deployment-ready generation quality.

AI.tldr: Proposes Short-to-Long On-Policy Distillation (ShortOPD) to recover structured-pruned LLMs for free-form generation, achieving 9× score improvement over unrecovered models and 1.6-4.4× over standard methods, while using 71% fewer tokens and 76% less training time.
AI.motivation: Structured pruning improves perplexity/accuracy on multiple-choice tasks but causes collapse on free-form generation (greedy pass@1 nearly vanishes). The gap exists because useful tokens are demoted (not erased) and recovery fails mainly due to suffix repetition.
AI.method: ShortOPD: a short-to-long on-policy distillation schedule. It detects teacher-confirmed repetitive suffixes, treats the surviving prefix as effective rollout length, and allocates future budgets to effective lengths the policy can use. Uses frozen pre-compression model as teacher for dense token-level supervision.
AI.result: 9× score improvement over unrecovered models, 1.6-4.4× over SFT/KD/SeqKD baselines. Matches fixed 8192-token horizon within 2 points using 71% fewer rollout tokens and 8.5h vs 35.9h training time.
AI.conclusion: Short-to-long OPD schedule is an effective recovery recipe for structured-pruned LLMs, bridging the gap between perplexity/benchmark gains and real deployment-ready generation quality.

title: Cost-Optimal Foundation Model Deployment Portfolio for Transportation Management
summary: Foundation models, including large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs), are increasingly used for transportation management center (TMC) tasks such as anomaly detection, incident reporting, and traveler information. Deploying multiple such models across TMC functions raises a portfolio question: which model should serve each function, in which deployment mode, and under what shared hardware budget? We formulate this as the Foundation Model Deployment Portfolio (FMDP) problem, a mixed-integer program minimizing total cost of ownership (TCO) subject to per-function quality, latency, and safety constraints over shared GPU capacity. We prove the problem NP-hard by reduction from the 0-1 knapsack problem and propose a polynomial-time greedy heuristic. In an illustrative case study with five TMC functions and 19 candidate (model, mode) pairs, FMDP identifies a mixed portfolio costing $34/mo (97% below the cheapest feasible all-closed-API baseline) by routing four functions to open-source APIs and the one function whose quality floor no open-source model meets to a closed API. Break-even analysis shows that on-premise GPU investment becomes reasonable only above approximately 309 vision queries/hour or if API prices double.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Foundation Model Deployment Portfolio (FMDP) problem—a mixed-integer program for selecting which foundation models (LLMs, VLMs) to deploy for different TMC functions, under quality, latency, safety, and hardware budget constraints. It proves NP-hardness, provides a greedy heuristic, and shows in a case study a mixed portfolio reducing costs by 97% compared to all-closed-API baselines.
AI.motivation: Multiple foundation models are increasingly used in TMC tasks, creating a need for optimal model-to-function assignment and deployment mode selection under shared GPU/hardware constraints to minimize total cost of ownership.
AI.method: Formulates FMDP as a mixed-integer program, proves NP-hardness via reduction from 0-1 knapsack, and proposes a polynomial-time greedy heuristic. Evaluated via a case study with 5 TMC functions and 19 candidate (model, mode) pairs.
AI.result: The mixed portfolio costs $34/month, 97% lower than the cheapest all-closed-API baseline. On-premise GPU investment becomes reasonable above ~309 vision queries/hour or if API prices double.
AI.conclusion: Mixed portfolios (open-source + closed APIs) are highly cost-effective for TMC. Open-source models suffice for most functions except when strict quality floors cannot be met.

title: From Metric to Mechanism: Designing Wireless Resilience through Finite Blocklength Dynamics
summary:
Next-generation wireless networks must maintain reliable operation under abrupt and severe disruptions, particularly in ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) scenarios where strict time constraints dominate system design. This work addresses network resilience from a time-centric perspective by explicitly integrating finite blocklength (FBL) communication, thereby exposing transmission duration as a controllable resource for system recovery. To this end, we propose a unified cross-layer framework that jointly couples queue dynamics, rate adaptation, and blocklength optimization, enabling the system to actively absorb, adapt to, and recover from diverse resilience events.
  To systematically evaluate these mechanisms, we introduce an interpretable resilience metric that decomposes disruption impact into absorption loss, adaptation efficiency, and recovery behavior, enabling a direct and intuitive assessment of system resilience. Building on this framework, we develop a three-stage alternating optimization approach that jointly optimizes PHY-layer parameters, including beamforming, reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) phase shifts, and blocklength, revealing the importance of time-aware resource allocation in the FBL regime. Numerical results demonstrate strong resilience performance under repeated channel disruptions and AI-driven traffic surges, highlighting the effectiveness of cross-layer resource adaptation. Finally, the proposed resilience metric enables an intuitive and consistent comparison of resilience performance across different approaches and disruption types, while revealing their respective strengths and limitations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a cross-layer framework integrating finite blocklength communication for time-centric network resilience, with a novel interpretable resilience metric and alternating optimization to enable recovery from severe disruptions in URLLC scenarios.
AI.motivation: Next-generation wireless networks must maintain reliable operation under abrupt and severe disruptions, particularly in URLLC scenarios where strict time constraints dominate system design. Existing approaches lack a time-centric resilience perspective that treats transmission duration as a controllable resource.
AI.method: Proposes a unified cross-layer framework coupling queue dynamics, rate adaptation, and blocklength optimization. Introduces an interpretable resilience metric decomposing disruption impact into absorption loss, adaptation efficiency, and recovery behavior. Develops a three-stage alternating optimization approach jointly optimizing beamforming, RIS phase shifts, and blocklength.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong resilience performance under repeated channel disruptions and AI-driven traffic surges. The proposed metric enables intuitive and consistent comparison of resilience performance across different approaches and disruption types.
AI.conclusion: Time-aware resource allocation in the FBL regime is critical for network resilience. The cross-layer framework and interpretable metric effectively capture, evaluate, and compare resilience behaviors in URLLC scenarios.

title: Accuracy Without Grounding: Diagnosing Visual Dependency Dissociation in Video LLM Benchmarks
comment: Accepted, ACM International Conference on Multimedia 2026 (ACM MM)
summary: Benchmark accuracy in video large language models (LLMs) is often treated as evidence of visual understanding. We audit this assumption across twenty models spanning 2-78B parameters and ten architecture families. We introduce the Visual Dependency Gap (VDG), the difference in per-question correctness between original-video and black-screen conditions. Paired McNemar tests on MVBench show that accuracy and visual dependency are separable: models differ on original video (p = 0.0003) but not on black screens (p = 0.53). Across models, task-type rankings are stable: Attribute Perception is strongly visual, whereas Temporal Reasoning approaches the language-only baseline. A diagnostic ladder from black screen to single frame, shuffled frames, and original video reveals that frame diversity supplies most of the visual benefit, while temporal order contributes near-zero accuracy across sixteen open-weight models. An ablation from 0.5 to 24 FPS rules out sparse sampling as the cause. H.264 experiments further show that stable aggregate accuracy conceals bidirectional question-level answer flips. The diagnostic also generalizes to four API-accessed models, whose VDG values range from 0.025 to 0.315. These results motivate VDG as a standard audit for whether video benchmarks measure visually grounded capability. Code is available at https://github.com/JaeLee18/accuracy-without-grounding.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Visual Dependency Gap (VDG) metric to audit whether video LLM accuracy on benchmarks reflects genuine visual understanding, finding that many models achieve strong scores without depending on visual information.
AI.motivation: The paper questions an implicit assumption in video LLM research: that benchmark accuracy necessarily indicates visual understanding. The authors aim to develop a diagnostic tool to separate genuine visual reasoning from language-only performance.
AI.method: The authors define VDG as the per-question accuracy difference between original-video and black-screen conditions. They use paired McNemar tests across 20 models, create a diagnostic ladder (black screen → single frame → shuffled frames → original video), run FPS ablations (0.5-24), and conduct H.264 experiments to detect answer flips.
AI.result: Models differ significantly on original video (p=0.0003) but not on black screens (p=0.53). Frame diversity drives most visual benefit; temporal order contributes near-zero accuracy. FPS ablation rules out sparse sampling. Bidirectional answer flips are hidden by stable aggregate accuracy. VDG varies widely (0.025-0.315) across API models.
AI.conclusion: Accuracy and visual dependency are separable. VDG is proposed as a standard audit metric to determine whether video benchmarks measure genuinely visually grounded capability rather than language priors.

title: Safe Overtaking for Autonomous Racing Using Hierarchical Optimization and Learning-Based Control
summary: Autonomous racing overtaking requires balancing competitive performance with safety under nonlinear vehicle dynamics and real-time constraints. Model Predictive Control (MPC) combined with Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) provides a principled mechanism for certifying forward invariance of a safe set. However, commonly used fixed-decay discrete-time CBF formulations can become overly conservative in interactive racing scenarios, limiting overtaking performance and requiring manual tuning across track conditions. This paper proposes a hierarchical overtaking framework that explicitly separates maneuver-level decision making from safety-certified trajectory control, reducing conservatism while preserving safety. A high-level Mixed-Integer Quadratic Program (MIQP) resolves the combinatorial passing-side selection problem by selecting a feasible overtaking topology, while a nonlinear Frenet-frame MPC enforces vehicle dynamics and safety through embedded discrete-time CBF constraints. This decomposition isolates the combinatorial complexity of maneuver selection from the continuous trajectory optimization. To further mitigate the sensitivity of fixed-decay barrier constraints, a reinforcement learning policy adapts the discrete-time CBF decay parameter online, enabling context-dependent modulation of safety margins without directly controlling vehicle inputs. Simulation and scaled-hardware experiments show that no single fixed decay parameter achieves uniformly strong performance across tracks, whereas the adaptive strategy attains the highest aggregate success rate and consistently strong safety--performance trade-offs without per-track tuning, improving robustness to environment variation while maintaining safety constraint satisfaction in nominal operation.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical overtaking framework combining MIQP for maneuver selection and adaptive-CBF MPC for safe trajectory control, using RL to modulate safety margins online, achieving robust performance across diverse tracks without manual tuning.
AI.motivation: Fixed-decay discrete-time CBFs in MPC-based autonomous racing overtaking are overly conservative, limiting performance and requiring manual tuning across different track conditions.
AI.method: Proposes a hierarchical framework: (1) high-level MIQP for combinatorial passing-side selection, (2) nonlinear Frenet-frame MPC with embedded CBF constraints, (3) RL policy to adapt the CBF decay parameter online.
AI.result: No single fixed decay parameter achieves uniformly strong performance; the adaptive strategy attains the highest aggregate success rate and consistently strong safety-performance trade-offs without per-track tuning.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive CBF-MPC framework improves robustness to environment variation while maintaining safety constraint satisfaction in nominal operation, removing the need for manual per-track tuning.

title: HEDGEHOG: Hierarchical Evaluation of Drug Generators Through Rigorous Filtration
comment: 29 pages (including References and Appendix sections), 8 tables, 7 figures, 2 supplementary files
summary: Generative molecular models can support early drug discovery by proposing new candidate compounds de novo. In practice, useful candidates must balance target-relevant activity, synthetic accessibility, physicochemical properties, and other multiparameter design constraints. However, metrics commonly used to evaluate molecular generators only weakly reflect whether the generated compounds are medicinally plausible and suitable for downstream computation. This can produce false positives in model evaluation, incorrect assumptions, and inefficient use of computational resources. We introduce HEDGEHOG, a unified six-stage filtration benchmark that is inspired by industrial hit identification workflows: (i) preprocessing; (ii) physicochemical descriptor screening; (iii) structural alerts and graph-sanity checks; (iv) synthesis feasibility; (v) docking and binding affinity estimation; and (vi) three-dimensional pose and interaction checks. We evaluate 23 molecular generators across three model classes under a standardized protocol. Across 230,000 generated molecules, only 0.65% of initial molecules survive all stages. Our results expose a central limitation of current molecular generators: molecules that appear acceptable under isolated criteria rarely satisfy medicinal chemistry, synthesis, docking, and 3D pose filters simultaneously.

AI.tldr: Only 0.65% of molecules generated by 23 molecular models survive HEDGEHOG, a comprehensive six-stage medicinal chemistry benchmark, revealing that current generators rarely produce compounds satisfying real-world drug discovery constraints.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation metrics for molecular generators fail to reflect medicinal plausibility and downstream suitability, leading to false positives and wasted computational resources in drug discovery.
AI.method: HEDGEHOG is a six-stage filtration benchmark inspired by industrial hit identification: (i) preprocessing; (ii) physicochemical screening; (iii) structural alerts/graph-sanity; (iv) synthesis feasibility; (v) docking/binding affinity; (vi) 3D pose/interaction checks. 23 generators across 3 model classes were evaluated on 230,000 molecules.
AI.result: Only 0.65% of initial molecules pass all six stages, indicating a severe bottleneck in generating medicinally plausible compounds that satisfy real-world drug discovery constraints simultaneously.
AI.conclusion: Current molecular generators have a fundamental limitation: molecules acceptable under isolated criteria rarely satisfy combined medicinal chemistry, synthesis, docking, and 3D pose filters, highlighting the need for more constrained optimization approaches.

title: Harness Handbook: Making Evolving Agent Harnesses Readable,Navigable, and Editable
summary: The capability of a modern AI agent depends not only on its foundation model but also on its harness, which constructs prompts, manages state, invokes tools, and coordinates execution. As models, APIs, environments, and requirements evolve, the harness must be continually modified. Before such a change can be made, a developer or coding agent must identify all code locations that implement the target behavior. This is difficult because production harnesses are large, tightly coupled, and behaviorally distributed, while modification requests describe what the system should do and repositories are organized by files and modules. Code search, repository indexing, and long-context processing ease inspection, but still leave this behavior-to-code mapping to be recovered by hand. Behavior localization is therefore a central bottleneck in harness evolution. We introduce the Harness Handbook, a behavior-centric representation synthesized automatically from a harness codebase via static analysis and LLM-assisted structuring, linking each behavior to its corresponding source. We also introduce Behavior-Guided Progressive Disclosure (BGPD), which guides agents from high-level behaviors to relevant implementation details and verifies candidate locations against the current source. On diverse modification requests from two open-source harnesses, Handbook-Assisted planning improves behavior localization and edit-plan quality while using fewer planner tokens, with the largest gains on scattered sites, rarely executed paths, and cross-module interactions. Evolving complex agentic systems thus depends not only on generating edits, but also on determining where those edits should be made.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Harness Handbook, a behavior-centric representation automatically generated from AI agent harness codebases via static analysis and LLM assistance, along with Behavior-Guided Progressive Disclosure (BGPD) to help developers and coding agents localize where code changes are needed when modifying complex agent systems.
AI.motivation: Modifying AI agent harnesses is difficult because production harnesses are large, tightly coupled, and behaviorally distributed; developers must manually map behavioral modification requests to specific code locations across files and modules, making behavior localization a central bottleneck in harness evolution.
AI.method: The authors propose (1) Harness Handbook, a behavior-centric representation automatically synthesized via static analysis and LLM-assisted structuring that links each behavior to its source code, and (2) Behavior-Guided Progressive Disclosure (BGPD), which guides agents from high-level behaviors to relevant implementation details and verifies candidate locations against current source code.
AI.result: On diverse modification requests from two open-source harnesses, Handbook-Assisted planning improves behavior localization and edit-plan quality while using fewer planner tokens, with the largest gains on scattered sites, rarely executed paths, and cross-module interactions.
AI.conclusion: Evolving complex agentic systems depends not only on generating correct edits but also on determining where those edits should be made; the proposed approach addresses the behavior-to-code mapping bottleneck effectively.

title: Design and Analysis of a Higher-Order Enhanced Phase-Locked Loop via the Ahmadi-Chaudhry-Zhang Newton Framework
summary:
The enhanced phase-locked loop (EPLL) is widely used in power systems to estimate the amplitude, phase, and frequency of sinusoidal voltages. Existing EPLL formulations are primarily derived from first- or second-order optimization methods, which may exhibit slow convergence, saddle-point attraction, or undesired oscillatory behavior. This paper presents a new higher-order EPLL based on the recently proposed Ahmadi-Chaudhry-Zhang (ACZ) higher-order Newton framework. Since the ACZ method was originally developed for scalar discrete-time optimization, a continuous-time higher-order Newton flow is formulated for adaptive systems. The resulting framework is then applied to the EPLL through a coordinate optimization strategy, whereby the higher-order Newton flow is applied only to the phase update, while the amplitude update retains its classical form because its cost function is exactly quadratic.
  The proposed autonomous system is analyzed through phase portraits and compared with the standard, Newton, and modified EPLL formulations. Phase-portrait analysis of the autonomous model shows that the proposed flow eliminates the spurious equilibria and saddle points present in the autonomous Newton EPLL, substantially enlarges the basin of attraction associated with the desired equilibrium, and shares several desirable convergence characteristics with the modified EPLL.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new higher-order enhanced phase-locked loop (EPLL) based on the ACZ Newton framework, which eliminates spurious equilibria and saddle points while enlarging the basin of attraction compared to existing EPLL formulations.
AI.motivation: Existing EPLL formulations derived from first- or second-order optimization methods exhibit slow convergence, saddle-point attraction, or undesired oscillatory behavior.
AI.method: A continuous-time higher-order Newton flow (based on the ACZ framework) is formulated and applied to the EPLL through a coordinate optimization strategy, where the higher-order flow is applied only to phase update while amplitude retains its classical form.
AI.result: The proposed method eliminates spurious equilibria and saddle points present in autonomous Newton EPLL, substantially enlarges the basin of attraction for the desired equilibrium, and shares desirable convergence characteristics with modified EPLL.
AI.conclusion: The higher-order ACZ-based EPLL improves convergence properties and stability characteristics over standard, Newton, and modified EPLL formulations for sinusoidal voltage estimation in power systems.

title: Reflecting Process Expertise in Procedural Material Generation
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Project page: https://materialapprentice.github.io
summary: Procedural material creation underpins applications in digital content creation, visual effects, and 3D asset design. Achieving high-quality results requires more than reproducing node graphs -- it demands understanding the process by which experts construct materials. We formulate procedural material generation as retrieval-time process reasoning over expert demonstrations, elevating process to a first-class representation beyond graph-only synthesis. Concretely, we represent expert workflows as process traces: textual records of construction steps, parameters, and design intent. To instantiate this idea, we use a pretrained LLM-based ProcessSynthesizer to synthesize a process trace aligned with a user's intent and a pretrained LLM-based Compiler to ground the process trace into an executable Blender material graph. Because procedural expertise is most naturally conveyed through demonstrations, we leverage tutorial videos as a source of process knowledge and extract textual, LLM-compatible traces using automated video analysis tools. In an expert study with five Blender artists (avg. 7.5 years of experience), materials generated by reflecting expert demonstrations were found to produce workflows requiring fewer edits, and more closely match professional design strategies than methods operating solely on static artifacts. A user study with 150 participants further shows that our approach achieves superior generation and editing performance compared to prior procedural systems. All code, models, and data will be available at https://materialapprentice.github.io

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a procedural material generation approach that retrieves and reasons over expert process traces (textual records of construction steps) using LLMs, then compiles them into executable Blender material graphs. The method leverages tutorial videos as process knowledge sources and outperforms prior systems in expert and user studies.
AI.motivation: Procedural material creation requires understanding the process by which experts construct materials, not just reproducing node graphs. Existing methods operate solely on static artifacts (node graphs) and fail to capture the underlying design strategies and workflows.
AI.method: The system uses a pretrained LLM-based ProcessSynthesizer to generate process traces (textual records of steps, parameters, intent) aligned with user intent, and a pretrained LLM-based Compiler to ground these traces into executable Blender material graphs. Process knowledge is extracted from tutorial videos using automated video analysis tools.
AI.result: Expert study with 5 Blender artists (avg. 7.5 years experience) showed materials generated by reflecting expert demonstrations required fewer edits and more closely matched professional design strategies. A 150-participant user study demonstrated superior generation and editing performance over prior systems.
AI.conclusion: Elevating process to a first-class representation (beyond graph-only synthesis) is crucial for high-quality procedural material generation. Process traces capture expert design intent and workflows, leading to more editable and professionally aligned results.

title: A Hybrid Sampling-Based Trajectory Planner with Game-Theoretic Guidance for Autonomous Racing
summary: Autonomous racing demands planning algorithms that balance vehicle dynamics at the limits of handling with strategic decision-making in competitive multi-agent scenarios. Game theory provides a mathematical framework for modeling these interactions, enabling interactive trajectory planning and strategic behaviors, such as blocking. However, directly solving full dynamic games online is computationally prohibitive and challenging to integrate into robust, high-frequency autonomous software stacks. This paper proposes a hybrid architecture that integrates game-theoretic reasoning into a sampling-based motion planner, combining strategic interactions with robust trajectory generation. Building upon an $α$-potential game formulation, we utilize an offline-learned potential function to capture multi-agent interactions. During online operation, a gradient-based optimization dynamically refines interaction parameters to generate an \textit{Interaction Reference Path}. This path serves as a dynamic cost bias within a high-frequency sampling planner. We evaluate our approach in a high-fidelity simulation environment on the Yas Marina Circuit. Qualitative and quantitative results demonstrate that our approach successfully induces defensive behaviors like blocking without carrying the computational burden of full dynamic game solvers.

AI.tldr: A hybrid architecture integrating game-theoretic reasoning into sampling-based motion planners for autonomous racing, using offline-learned potential functions and online gradient-based optimization to produce interaction-aware trajectories with defensive behaviors like blocking, without the computational cost of full dynamic game solvers.
