We show that it is a characteristic direction of a robotic impact and largely independent of contact properties. The results are directly applicable to large classes of backdrivable robotic systems with rigid links. We address particularities of systems with nonelastic and flexible joints, unconstrained as well as constrained systems. Further, we show that the approach direction w.r.t the NSID sets the direction of the impulsive force in frictional, inelastic impacts. The comprehensive theoretical analysis of this work supported by an experimental validation may serve as a foundation for future planning and control algorithms for various robotic impact applications. These can include humanoid locomotion on a slippery surface or repetitive hammering.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes pre- and post-impact velocities in rigid robotic collisions, identifying a characteristic "nonsmooth impact direction" (NSID) that is largely independent of contact properties, with experimental validation supporting its use for future impact planning and control algorithms.
AI.motivation: Rigid robot-environment collisions are typically modeled as instantaneous events with impulsive forces and nonsmooth velocities. A systematic understanding of impact velocity relationships is needed to improve planning and control for robotic impact applications.
AI.method: The authors systematically analyze pre- and post-impact velocities, identifying the nonsmooth impact direction (NSID) as a characteristic direction of robotic impacts. They examine backdrivable rigid-link systems with nonelastic/flexible joints, and both unconstrained and constrained systems.
AI.result: The NSID is shown to be largely independent of contact properties. The approach direction relative to NSID determines the impulsive force direction in frictional, inelastic impacts. Theoretical analysis is supported by experimental validation.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide a theoretical foundation for developing future planning and control algorithms for robotic impact applications such as humanoid locomotion on slippery surfaces and repetitive hammering.

title: Nexus: Native Mesh Generation with Diffusion
summary: Generating high-quality triangle meshes is essential for film, gaming, and interactive 3D applications. Mainstream methods rely on mesh serialization and autoregressive processes, which stuggles in effective inference and is sensitive to error accumulation. In this paper, we present Nexus, a diffusion method that achieves holistic mesh generation via decoupled vertex and topology generation. First, we view mesh vertices as sparse voxels organized as an octree and adopt a diffusion model to generate the vertices in a coarse-to-fine manner. Second, for topology modeling, we propose Spacetime Interval, as an extension of Spacetime Distance to encode arbitrary edge and face topology into continuous per-vertex embeddings. It allows for a global and efficient recovery of complex topology. We then employ a diffusion model to generate the continuous embeddings on the generated vertices. Extensive experiments on the Objaverse and Toys4K datasets and in-the-wild images demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art autoregressive and two-stage baselines, effectively circumventing the inherent limitations of sequential mesh modeling. A blind user study from 3D practitioners confirms strong perceptual preference for our results.

AI.tldr: Nexus is a diffusion-based method for holistic 3D mesh generation that decouples vertex and topology generation, outperforming autoregressive approaches on Objaverse and Toys4K datasets.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive mesh generation methods suffer from ineffective inference and error accumulation. The authors aim to overcome these limitations by proposing a diffusion-based approach that avoids sequential modeling.
AI.method: Mesh generation is split into two decoupled stages: (1) Vertex generation via a diffusion model that treats vertices as sparse voxels in an octree, generated coarse-to-fine. (2) Topology generation using Spacetime Interval (an extension of Spacetime Distance) to encode edge/face topology into continuous per-vertex embeddings, recovered via another diffusion model.
AI.result: Nexus outperforms state-of-the-art autoregressive and two-stage baselines on Objaverse and Toys4K datasets, and demonstrates strong performance on in-the-wild images. A blind user study confirmed perceptual preference by 3D practitioners.
AI.conclusion: The proposed diffusion-based decoupled approach effectively circumvents limitations of sequential mesh modeling, achieving high-quality holistic mesh generation.

title: Dynamical Vehicle Orienteering Problem for Multi-Rotor Unmanned Aerial Vehicles
comment: 28 pages, 10 figures, preprint submitted to European Journal of Operational Research
summary: This paper introduces the Dynamical Vehicle Orienteering Problem (DVOP), a generalization of the Orienteering Problem (OP). The OP maximizes the reward collected from spatial targets under a limited travel budget; the DVOP extends it by accounting for both external and vehicle-actuated forces. We study the DVOP in the context of multi-rotor Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) flight planning, using a three-dimensional Point-Mass Model (PMM) constrained by maximum velocity and acceleration magnitudes and subject to gravitational acceleration, with the travel budget expressed as a maximum flight time. Because the DVOP couples reward maximization with time-optimal trajectory planning, it cannot be formulated as a simple graph problem and solved exactly without relaxing or under-actuating the vehicle dynamics. We therefore propose two solution approaches: a Branch-and-Bound (BnB) procedure that combines Non-Linear Programming (NLP) and Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) to provide high-quality solutions, and a Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) metaheuristic that supplies an initial reward bound and scales to instances intractable for the BnB. The BnB relies on a novel MILP formulation of travel costs based on minimum-time trajectory primitives through target triplets, yielding a tight reward upper bound, while the LNS uses limited thrust decomposition to compute fast, high-quality PMM trajectories. Experiments on benchmark instances show improvements of up to 37 % over state-of-the-art solutions for the Kinematic Orienteering Problem, and a real-world deployment on a multi-rotor UAV verifies the proposed PMM solution trajectories.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Dynamical Vehicle Orienteering Problem (DVOP) for multi-rotor UAV flight planning, combining reward maximization with time-optimal trajectory planning under realistic dynamics. Two solution approaches are proposed: a Branch-and-Bound (BnB) procedure combining NLP and MILP, and a Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) metaheuristic, with improvements up to 37% over state-of-the-art solutions.
AI.motivation: The Orienteering Problem (OP) is limited to simple graph-based formulations that ignore realistic vehicle dynamics. The authors aim to generalize the OP by incorporating external and vehicle-actuated forces, specifically for multi-rotor UAV flight planning, where dynamics significantly impact achievable trajectories and rewards.
AI.method: Two solution approaches: (1) A Branch-and-Bound (BnB) procedure combining Non-Linear Programming (NLP) and Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP), using a novel MILP formulation of travel costs based on minimum-time trajectory primitives through target triplets. (2) A Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) metaheuristic using limited thrust decomposition for fast trajectory computation.
AI.result: Experiments show improvements of up to 37% over state-of-the-art solutions for the Kinematic Orienteering Problem. Real-world deployment on a multi-rotor UAV verifies the proposed PMM solution trajectories.
AI.conclusion: The DVOP effectively generalizes the OP to realistic dynamic vehicle scenarios. The BnB provides high-quality solutions while the LNS scales to larger instances. The approaches are validated both in benchmark tests and real-world UAV deployment.

title: GHR-VLM: Making Zero-Shot Transit Video Analytics Realizable with Grounded Hybrid Reasoning
summary: Transit video understanding can provide valuable fine-grained data that conventional passenger counters and fare systems cannot capture. However, supervised video models require task-specific annotations, while applying vision-language models (VLMs) directly to long onboard videos is unreliable and costly. To leverage the complementary strengths of both approaches, we propose GHR-VLM, a visual grounded hybrid reasoning framework for zero-shot transit-bus video analytics. It is motivated by the observation that explicit visual grounding can improve VLM reasoning by converting long surveillance streams into compact, passenger-centered spatiotemporal evidence. Specifically, we propose an edge-cloud design in which a lightweight edge-based monitor continuously tracks door status and segments passenger clips. A backend VLM then identifies boarding passengers and classifies payment behavior through a two-stage coarse-to-fine refinement of spatiotemporal evidence. By invoking the VLM only on grounded passenger clips and contact sheets, GHR-VLM reduces cloud inference, avoids payment-specific training data, and supplies the localized evidence that VLMs otherwise struggle to identify. Evaluation on 486 minutes of real-world bus surveillance video demonstrates the potential of grounded edge-cloud reasoning for passenger-level payment analytics while highlighting the challenges posed by degraded video conditions.

AI.tldr: GHR-VLM is a hybrid edge-cloud framework combining lightweight tracking and vision-language models for zero-shot transit video analytics, achieving passenger-level payment behavior classification while reducing cloud inference costs.
AI.motivation: Transit video understanding needs fine-grained data that conventional systems cannot capture, but supervised models require task-specific annotations and VLMs are unreliable/costly on long videos. The authors observed that explicit visual grounding can bridge this gap.
AI.method: Edge-cloud design: (1) lightweight edge monitor tracks door status and segments passenger clips, (2) backend VLM performs two-stage coarse-to-fine refinement on grounded passenger clips and contact sheets to identify boarding passengers and classify payment behavior.
AI.result: Evaluated on 486 minutes of real-world bus surveillance video, demonstrating potential for passenger-level payment analytics while highlighting challenges from degraded video conditions.
AI.conclusion: Grounded edge-cloud reasoning enables zero-shot transit video analytics, reducing cloud inference and avoiding payment-specific training data, though degraded video quality remains a challenge.

title: Vision-Based Obstacle Separation for Strawberry Harvesting in Clusters Using Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning
summary: Selective harvesting in clustered strawberry environments is challenging because ripe fruits are often occluded by surrounding unripe fruits, making direct grasping unreliable. To address this problem, this paper proposes a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework, termed VGPA, which integrates a vision-guided decision mechanism and a Progressive Adaptive Exploration Strategy (PAES) for vision-based obstacle separation and harvesting. The task was decomposed into two sequential stages: obstacle separation and target grasping. At the high level, the vision-guided mechanism improved option selection and accelerated policy convergence. At the low level, PAES improved exploration efficiency and training stability during continuous control learning. In simulation experiments, the learned policy achieved a success rate of 96.7%. In addition, sim-to-real transfer experiments on a self-developed parallel robot showed that the proposed method achieved success rates ranging from 71.7% to 88.3%, outperforming direct picking while requiring only 1.22~s more average harvesting time. These results verified the effectiveness, generalization ability, and practical potential of the proposed method for robotic harvesting in complex clustered environments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes VGPA, a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework with vision guidance and progressive adaptive exploration, for harvesting strawberries in clustered environments by first separating obstacles then grasping targets, achieving 96.7% success in simulation and 71.7–88.3% in real-world tests.
AI.motivation: Selective harvesting in clustered strawberry environments is challenging due to occlusion by unripe fruits, making direct grasping unreliable.
AI.method: A hierarchical reinforcement learning framework (VGPA) with a vision-guided decision mechanism and Progressive Adaptive Exploration Strategy (PAES), decomposing the task into obstacle separation and target grasping stages.
AI.result: 96.7% success rate in simulation; 71.7%–88.3% success rate in sim-to-real transfer, outperforming direct picking with only 1.22s extra average harvesting time.
AI.conclusion: The method demonstrates effectiveness, generalization ability, and practical potential for robotic harvesting in complex clustered environments.

title: The SIGReg Objective as Variational Free Energy: A Theoretical Active-Inference Account of JEPA World Models
comment: Theoretical paper; empirical validation of the stated predictions is left to separate work. 28 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables
summary: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) are the dominant design for latent world models, yet they are usually justified by empirical performance rather than a normative principle. We show that the choice of anti-collapse regulariser determines whether a JEPA's training objective, a prediction loss plus a weighted embedding regulariser, is a valid Active Inference (AIF) variational free energy. We organise four non-contrastive regularisers (VICReg, LogDet, PairDist, and SIGReg) into an entropy-estimator hierarchy indexed by a prior-miscalibration gap, and show that the gap's sign, whether the estimator bounds the latent entropy from above or below, decides whether the AIF surprise bound survives: VICReg and LogDet are unsafe upper bounds, PairDist a safe lower bound, and SIGReg eliminates the gap. We then prove a correspondence theorem: under the standard constant-noise encoder model and successful SIGReg enforcement (isotropic-Gaussian embeddings), the gap vanishes, the objective becomes an exact information bottleneck, the surprise bound is preserved, and the latent goal cost becomes an exact proxy for AIF pragmatic value, whereas VICReg leaves an irreducible second-order anisotropy term. We extend the correspondence to multi-step expected free energy, ensemble epistemic value, and a learned-policy regime, and we identify the one AIF term no current JEPA world model computes: the state-epistemic value, a future-state coverage signal. The predictions differ in kind, not degree, and are stated here as theoretical consequences left for empirical test in separate work; full proofs are in Appendix A, and the algebraic core of every result is machine-verified in Lean 4 (Appendix D).

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a formal connection between Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) and Active Inference (AIF), showing that the choice of anti-collapse regulariser determines whether a JEPA's objective is a valid AIF variational free energy. It organizes four regularisers into an entropy-estimator hierarchy and proves a correspondence theorem where SIGReg yields an exact information bottleneck, while VICReg and LogDet produce unsafe upper bounds.
AI.motivation: JEPAs are widely used for latent world models but lack a normative (principled) justification—they rely on empirical performance. The authors aim to ground JEPAs in Active Inference theory, providing theoretical rigor to their design choices.
AI.method: The authors analyze four non-contrastive regularisers (VICReg, LogDet, PairDist, SIGReg) for JEPAs, organizing them into an entropy-estimator hierarchy indexed by a prior-miscalibration gap. They prove a correspondence theorem connecting JEPA objectives to AIF variational free energy under specific regulariser choices, with machine-verified proofs in Lean 4.
AI.result: SIGReg eliminates the prior-miscalibration gap, making the JEPA objective an exact information bottleneck that preserves the AIF surprise bound. VICReg and LogDet are unsafe upper bounds; PairDist is a safe lower bound. VICReg leaves an irreducible second-order anisotropy term. The correspondence extends to multi-step expected free energy, ensemble epistemic value, and learned-policy regimes.
AI.conclusion: The choice of anti-collapse regulariser fundamentally determines whether a JEPA is theoretically aligned with Active Inference. Only SIGReg yields a fully valid AIF model. The paper identifies state-epistemic value as the one AIF term missing from current JEPA world models, providing clear theoretical predictions for empirical testing.

title: UniPhysGen: Unified Physical Grounding for Simulation-Ready 3D Assets
summary: Physically grounded 3D assets are increasingly important for embodied AI and robotic simulation. However, most existing 3D assets lack unified physical semantics, including articulation semantics and intrinsic physical properties, required for realistic interaction. Current approaches either treat these semantics independently or rely on canonicalized object structures, limiting robustness across heterogeneous 3D assets. We present UniPhys, a scalable framework for automatically transforming raw 3D assets into simulation-ready assets with unified physical semantics. Based on UniPhys, we construct UniPhys-40K, a large-scale physically grounded dataset, together with UniPhys-Bench, a carefully verified benchmark for unified physical grounding evaluation. We further introduce UniPhysGen, a unified physical grounding model that jointly reasons over articulation semantics and intrinsic physical properties. UniPhysGen incorporates geometry-robust articulation grounding to mitigate geometric shortcut bias under heterogeneous part decompositions. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across articulation grounding and intrinsic physical property estimation tasks, while the resulting assets can be directly deployed in robotic simulation environments for realistic physical interaction. Our code and dataset will be available at https://github.com/breezexian/UniPhysGen.

AI.tldr: UniPhys is a scalable framework that automatically transforms raw 3D assets into simulation-ready assets with unified physical semantics (articulation + intrinsic properties). It includes a large-scale dataset (UniPhys-40K), a benchmark (UniPhys-Bench), and a model (UniPhysGen) that achieves SOTA on physical grounding tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D assets lack unified physical semantics needed for realistic interaction in embodied AI and robotic simulation. Current approaches treat articulation semantics and physical properties independently or rely on canonicalized structures, limiting robustness across heterogeneous assets.
AI.method: UniPhys is a scalable framework for automatic transformation of raw 3D assets. It includes UniPhysGen — a unified physical grounding model that jointly reasons over articulation semantics and intrinsic physical properties, with geometry-robust articulation grounding to mitigate geometric shortcut bias under heterogeneous part decompositions.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance across articulation grounding and intrinsic physical property estimation tasks. The resulting assets are directly deployable in robotic simulation for realistic physical interaction.
AI.conclusion: UniPhys provides a complete pipeline (framework + dataset + benchmark + model) for converting raw 3D assets into physically grounded, simulation-ready assets, advancing embodied AI and robotic simulation capabilities.

title: A Deployed Hybrid Vehicle-in-the-Loop Platform for Validating Cooperative Perception
comment: 4 pages, 5 figures, to be presented in the IEEE ITSC 2026 Industry Track
summary: European safety regulation now permits a large share of automated-driving homologation evidence to be produced virtually, provided a validated physical-virtual facility generates it. We present a deployed hybrid Vehicle-in-the-Loop (ViL) platform that couples a real instrumented vehicle with a CARLA-based digital twin (DT) through a V2X message pipeline, and we report its first integrated operation on a public-road-representative test track. A real vehicle streams ETSI-compliant CAM/CPM messages into the DT, where a GPU-accelerated Cooperative Perception (CP) module fuses them into a probabilistic occupancy grid during scenario runtime. We demonstrate the platform on a multi-vehicle double T-intersection scenario, characterise the CP workload across nominal, rain and night conditions and five localization-noise levels, and discuss the platform's current architectural limits and the engineering targets they define. The results show that CP substantially widens field-of-view (FoV) coverage and improves occupied-cell recall, and that beyond a moderate localization-noise threshold, positioning uncertainty, and not weather, becomes the dominant error source. We outline the platform's trajectory toward a Mediterranean operational design domain (ODD) testing service.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a deployed hybrid Vehicle-in-the-Loop (ViL) platform combining a real instrumented vehicle with a CARLA-based digital twin via V2X communication, demonstrating cooperative perception on a test track under varying weather and localization noise conditions.
AI.motivation: European safety regulation now allows virtual homologation evidence for automated driving, but requires validated physical-virtual facilities. The authors aim to develop and demonstrate a deployed platform that can generate such evidence through real-virtual coupling.
AI.method: A hybrid ViL platform couples a real vehicle with a CARLA digital twin via a V2X (CAM/CPM) message pipeline. A GPU-accelerated Cooperative Perception module fuses virtual and real sensor data into probabilistic occupancy grids. The platform is tested on a multi-vehicle double T-intersection scenario under rain, night, and varied localization noise levels.
AI.result: Cooperative perception substantially widens FoV coverage and improves occupied-cell recall. Beyond a moderate localization-noise threshold, positioning uncertainty (not weather) becomes the dominant error source.
AI.conclusion: The platform is viable for hybrid homologation testing, with clear engineering targets identified (localization noise reduction). Its trajectory points toward a Mediterranean ODD testing service.

title: FastCentNN: Accelerating Centroid Neural Network with Entropy Proxy
summary: Centroid neural network (CentNN) is an unsupervised competitive learning algorithm in which centroid splitting is triggered only after strict local stabilization, often leading to prolonged low-movement training phases before model expansion. This report proposes FastCentNN, an accelerated variant that addresses this inefficiency by introducing an early splitting strategy based on the total centroid movement per epoch, which serves as a training entropy proxy. As a result, FastCentNN reduces unnecessary reassignment epochs while preserving the original winner-loser learning dynamics. FastCentNN supports both absolute and stage-relative movement thresholds, allowing the splitting criterion to remain either fixed or adaptive throughout training. Experiments on some benchmark datasets show that FastCentNN consistently achieves clustering quality comparable to CentNN while reducing runtime by up to 16% on synthetic 2D datasets and about 5% on high-dimensional datasets. FastCentNN therefore provides a practical and efficient drop-in replacement for CentNN, retaining its online adaptive learning behavior while offering a simple and interpretable speed-stability trade-off through configurable splitting thresholds.

AI.tldr: FastCentNN accelerates Centroid Neural Network training by introducing an early splitting strategy based on centroid movement (entropy proxy), achieving up to 16% faster runtime with comparable clustering quality.
AI.motivation: CentNN's strict local stabilization before splitting causes prolonged low-movement training phases, making it inefficient. The authors aim to reduce unnecessary reassignment epochs.
AI.method: FastCentNN uses an early splitting criterion based on total centroid movement per epoch as an entropy proxy, with both absolute and stage-relative movement thresholds for adaptive or fixed splitting.
AI.result: Up to 16% runtime reduction on synthetic 2D datasets and ~5% on high-dimensional datasets, with clustering quality comparable to CentNN.
AI.conclusion: FastCentNN is a practical drop-in replacement for CentNN that offers a simple, interpretable speed-stability trade-off through configurable splitting thresholds.

title: OvisOCR2 Technical Report
summary: We introduce OvisOCR2, a 0.8B document parsing model. OvisOCR2 is designed as an end-to-end parser: given a document page image, it generates a Markdown representation in natural reading order, covering text, formulas, tables, and visual regions. We build a data engine that combines filtered real-document annotations with synthetic pages whose rendered images and Markdown targets are derived from the same HTML source. The training recipe includes supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning on a 4B branch with a multi-component reward design, on-policy distillation into the 0.8B model, and model fusion. On OmniDocBench v1.6, OvisOCR2 achieves a state-of-the-art overall score of 96.58, placing an end-to-end model at the top of this leaderboard previously dominated by pipeline methods and highlighting the potential of end-to-end document parsing. On PureDocBench, OvisOCR2 also achieves the highest Avg3 score of 75.06. Beyond these two public benchmarks, we evaluate OvisOCR2 on an in-house benchmark designed to cover a broader set of long-tail and challenging scenarios. OvisOCR2 obtains the best overall performance among the compared methods, providing further evidence of its generalization and robustness. OvisOCR2 is available at https://huggingface.co/ATH-MaaS/OvisOCR2.

AI.tldr: OvisOCR2 is a 0.8B end-to-end document parsing model that converts document page images to Markdown, achieving state-of-the-art scores of 96.58 on OmniDocBench and 75.06 on PureDocBench through a training pipeline involving SFT, RL distillation, and model fusion.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient (0.8B parameters) end-to-end document parser that can outperform traditional pipeline methods on document parsing benchmarks, while maintaining robustness on long-tail scenarios.
AI.method: The model is trained with a data engine combining filtered real documents and synthetic HTML-derived pages. The recipe includes supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning on a 4B teacher branch with multi-component rewards, on-policy distillation into the 0.8B model, and model fusion.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art overall score of 96.58 on OmniDocBench v1.6 (top of leaderboard previously dominated by pipeline methods) and highest Avg3 score of 75.06 on PureDocBench. Also shows best performance on an in-house long-tail benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The results highlight the potential of end-to-end document parsing models to surpass pipeline-based approaches, demonstrating strong generalization and robustness even with a small 0.8B model.

title: Learning Robust Execution in Robotic Manipulation with Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Robotic manipulation poses fundamental challenges due to uncertainty, long-horizon execution, and compounding errors, which can easily destabilize execution and lead to task failure. Although recent vision-language-action (VLA) models exhibit strong generalization, they typically lack explicit mechanisms to assess execution stability and to recover when execution deviates from its nominal behavior. In this paper, we propose: (1) two complementary metrics to assess execution quality at runtime, and (2) an agentic reinforcement learning framework that learns to restore effective execution through high-level decision-making rather than directly learning low-level actions. In this framework, an agentic policy reasons over recent execution history and selects among a small set of execution modes to regulate the execution process. Under execution degradation, it triggers appropriate recovery mechanisms to restore the robot to previously visited nominal states, enabling the task to continue. We evaluate the proposed method on the LIBERO benchmark, achieving up to a 13.7% improvement in success rate under standard settings and up to a 39.2% improvement under disturbance settings, demonstrating substantially enhanced execution robustness.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an agentic reinforcement learning framework with two complementary metrics to assess robotic execution stability, enabling recovery through high-level decision-making and achieving 13.7-39.2% improvement in success rates under standard and disturbed settings.
AI.motivation: Robotic manipulation faces challenges from uncertainty, long-horizon execution, and compounding errors. Existing VLA models lack explicit mechanisms to assess execution stability and recover from deviations during runtime.
AI.method: (1) Two complementary metrics for runtime execution quality assessment, and (2) an agentic RL framework that learns high-level decision-making to restore effective execution by reasoning over recent history and selecting among execution modes to trigger recovery mechanisms.
AI.result: Up to 13.7% improvement in success rate under standard settings and up to 39.2% improvement under disturbance settings on the LIBERO benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method substantially enhances execution robustness for robotic manipulation by enabling runtime stability assessment and agentic high-level recovery from execution degradation.

title: How the Hessian-Spectrum of Neural Networks Depends on Data
summary: The Hessian matrix is an important quantity of interest when it comes to studying the loss landscape and optimization dynamics in deep learning, as well as designing measures of generalization, second-order learning algorithms, etc. Prior works have focused on empirical results or pursued a theoretical treatment under overly simplified settings. In this work, we derive the eigenvalues of the Hessian of linear networks with arbitrary widths and depths, and datasets with an arbitrary number of samples, features, and labels. Importantly, for classification tasks with MSE loss, we identify that the sharpness of the solution is directly related to the maximum proportion of samples belonging to any class. We empirically validate our predictions and systematically analyze the effects of shedding the impractical assumptions one at a time, as well as incorporating nonlinearities. We observe that our predictions are considerably robust in most cases, allowing us to extend our conclusions to more practical learning setups.

AI.tldr: This paper derives analytical eigenvalues of the Hessian matrix for linear neural networks of arbitrary depth/width on arbitrary datasets, showing that sharpness in classification tasks with MSE loss relates to the maximum class proportion. The theoretical predictions remain robust even when relaxing impractical assumptions and adding nonlinearities.
AI.motivation: The Hessian matrix is crucial for understanding loss landscapes, optimization dynamics, generalization, and second-order learning in deep learning, but prior work relied on empirical results or oversimplified theoretical settings. This paper aims to provide a more general theoretical treatment.
AI.method: The authors derive the eigenvalues of the Hessian for linear networks with arbitrary widths, depths, and datasets (arbitrary samples, features, labels). They analyze classification with MSE loss and empirically test predictions by relaxing assumptions one at a time and incorporating nonlinearities.
AI.result: Sharpness of the solution is directly related to the maximum proportion of samples belonging to any class. The theoretical predictions are considerably robust in most cases even when simplifying assumptions are removed or nonlinearities are added.
AI.conclusion: The findings extend to more practical learning setups, suggesting that the derived relationships between class proportions and Hessian sharpness hold beyond the simplified linear network setting.

title: Human4K: A Large-Scale 4K Multi-View Mocap Dataset for Whole-Body 3D Human Reconstruction
summary: Recent advances in 3D human reconstruction have improved overall performance, yet current models still fail in the most challenging real-world scenarios. They often produce unstable geometry, inaccurate limb articulation and unreliable predictions under depth ambiguity or self-occlusion. A key reason is that existing datasets still lack the combination of high-resolution images, high-precision annotations and diverse whole-body motions required to support robust reconstruction. To address this gap, we present Human4K, a large-scale 4K multi-view whole-body human reconstruction dataset with mocap-accurate SMPL-X annotations. Human4K contains over six million 4K images captured by an eight-view high-resolution camera system synchronized with a professional Vicon motion capture setup, covering 11 subjects performing complex, highly articulated and strongly self-occluded full-body motions. All sequences are processed by a Motion-Retargeting and Refinement Module (MRRM) to ensure precise alignment for the full body and extremities. Experimental results show that training with Human4K consistently improves whole-body reconstruction on standard benchmarks, with particularly large gains for hands, feet and depth-ambiguous limb configurations.

AI.tldr: Human4K is a large-scale 4K multi-view whole-body human reconstruction dataset with 6M+ images and mocap-accurate SMPL-X annotations, using a Motion-Retargeting and Refinement Module (MRRM) to improve whole-body reconstruction, especially for hands, feet, and depth-ambiguous limbs.
AI.motivation: Existing human reconstruction models fail in challenging real-world scenarios due to unstable geometry, inaccurate limb articulation, and unreliable predictions under depth ambiguity or self-occlusion, largely because current datasets lack high-resolution images, high-precision annotations, and diverse whole-body motions.
AI.method: The authors built Human4K using an eight-view 4K camera system synchronized with a Vicon motion capture setup, capturing 11 subjects performing complex motions. They developed a Motion-Retargeting and Refinement Module (MRRM) to ensure precise alignment of full body and extremities with SMPL-X annotations.
AI.result: Training with Human4K consistently improves whole-body reconstruction on standard benchmarks, with particularly large gains for hands, feet, and depth-ambiguous limb configurations.
AI.conclusion: High-resolution, high-precision datasets like Human4K are crucial for advancing robust 3D human reconstruction in challenging real-world scenarios, especially for extremities and ambiguous poses.

title: Recursive ArUco Markers: A Scalable Fiducial Marker Design for Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Landing Pads
summary: Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) increasingly rely on visual fiducial markers for autonomous navigation and precision landing. However, standard markers suffer from limited operational ranges, becoming undetectable when the camera is either too far or too close. While recursive and fractal markers have been proposed to address this issue, existing approaches either require the marker's center to remain visible, making them vulnerable to occlusion, or are limited in their recursion depth and placement. We propose a novel Recursive ArUco marker design. Our method allows any standard fiducial marker to be transformed into a recursive marker with an arbitrary depth. By employing a modified bit-sampling strategy during detection, we embed complete markers within both the black and white bits of the parent marker. This approach guarantees unlimited recursion depth and robust detection even with partial occlusion, as it does not rely on the marker's center being visible. Furthermore, by maintaining a single, unique identifier across all recursive scales, our proposal provides an extensive dictionary of multiple unique landing pads. This capability allows fleets of UAVs to operate simultaneously, with each drone landing at its designated location -- a feature not supported by existing Fractal and Harco markers due to their structural and dictionary constraints.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Recursive ArUco marker design that embeds complete markers within parent marker bits, enabling unlimited recursion depth, occlusion-robust detection, and unique IDs across scales for multi-UAV landing.
AI.motivation: Standard fiducial markers have limited operational ranges. Existing recursive/fractal markers either require the center to remain visible (vulnerable to occlusion) or have limited recursion depth and dictionary constraints.
AI.method: Transforms any standard ArUco marker into a recursive marker with arbitrary depth by embedding complete markers within both black and white bits of the parent marker, using a modified bit-sampling detection strategy.
AI.result: Achieves unlimited recursion depth, robust detection under partial occlusion (no center visibility needed), and maintains a single unique identifier across all recursive scales for an extensive dictionary of landing pads.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Recursive ArUco marker outperforms existing Fractal and Harco markers by supporting multi-UAV simultaneous landing with designated locations, overcoming occlusion vulnerabilities and dictionary limitations.

title: Consensus as Privileged Context for Label-Free Self-Distillation
summary: Sampling multiple solutions and returning the majority answer is among the most reliable ways to improve the reasoning accuracy of large language models without labels, and a growing family of methods converts this consensus signal into training supervision. However, existing approaches use consensus only in restricted forms: as a filter that selects solutions for fine-tuning, as a preference between answers, or as a scalar reward for reinforcement learning, discarding most of the information that the agreeing solutions contain. We present CANON (Consensus-ANchored self-distillatiON), a label-free training method that turns consensus into dense, token-level supervision. For each unlabeled prompt, CANON samples multiple solutions, extracts the majority answer, and conditions a frozen snapshot of the model on a solution that reaches it; this consensus-anchored teacher then supervises the model on its own rollouts at every token. Experiments on mathematical and scientific reasoning benchmarks show that CANON improves pass@1 by up to 12 points, outperforming label-free reinforcement learning by 6 points at a seventh of its compute and approaching a teacher conditioned on gold solutions; trained on pooled unlabeled data, it transfers to held-out benchmarks, matching training methods that use gold labels. Analysis suggests that the improvements are not pure distribution sharpening: after training, the model solves problems it previously never solved in 32 attempts, and its majority vote itself becomes more accurate.

AI.tldr: CANON is a label-free training method that converts majority vote consensus from multiple reasoning samples into dense, token-level supervision, outperforming existing label-free methods on math/science benchmarks while using significantly less compute.
AI.motivation: Existing consensus-based methods (filtering, preference, scalar reward) discard rich information from agreeing solutions; the authors aim to leverage consensus as dense token-level supervision for more effective self-distillation.
AI.method: CANON samples multiple solutions per unlabeled prompt, extracts the majority answer, conditions a frozen model snapshot on a solution reaching that answer (consensus-anchored teacher), and supervises the model on its own rollouts at every token position.
AI.result: Improves pass@1 by up to 12 points on math/science benchmarks; outperforms label-free RL by 6 points at 1/7th compute; matches gold-label training on transferred tasks; solves problems previously unsolvable in 32 attempts; improves majority vote accuracy.
AI.conclusion: CANON demonstrates that converting consensus into dense token-level supervision is more effective and efficient than prior label-free methods, enabling models to solve previously unsolvable problems without any labeled data.

title: Beyond Color Geometry: Evaluating Human-Like Color Representations in Vision Models
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables submitted to 2026 Joint 14th International Conference on Soft Computing and Intelligent Systems and 27th International Symposium on Advanced Intelligent Systems (SCIS&ISIS 2026)
summary: Do vision models see colors the way humans do? Existing evaluations of color representations usually compare them with geometric spaces such as CIELAB or with discrete color labels. These references capture perceptual distance or category membership, but not the graded way in which people organize colors. We evaluate color grounding against a fuzzy perceptual model with 86 graded categories fitted to human survey data. The framework can be applied to any image encoder and measures three complementary properties: category boundaries, category compactness, and graded alignment beyond what color geometry alone can explain. Across eleven Vision Transformer encoders, the category-level results are broadly similar, whereas graded alignment differs substantially. Masked Autoencoders achieve the strongest beyond-geometry alignment, with confidence intervals that do not overlap those of the other encoders. A layer-wise analysis further shows that masked reconstruction preserves this structure toward the output. On natural images, MAE represents surface color globally, while language-supervised models encode color more strongly in relation to the foreground object. These results show that human-like color grounding has several distinct aspects that should not be reduced to a single score.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates how well vision models represent colors in a human-like way, using a fuzzy perceptual model with 86 graded categories. Across Vision Transformers, MAE shows the strongest human-aligned color representations, especially in graded alignment beyond geometry.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations compare model color representations to geometric spaces (CIELAB) or discrete labels, which capture perceptual distance or category membership but not the graded, fuzzy way humans organize colors.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework using 86 graded categories fitted to human survey data, measuring three properties: category boundaries, category compactness, and graded alignment beyond color geometry. They evaluate eleven Vision Transformer encoders.
AI.result: Masked Autoencoders (MAE) achieve the strongest beyond-geometry alignment with non-overlapping confidence intervals. MAE preserves surface color globally, while language-supervised models encode color more in relation to foreground objects.
AI.conclusion: Human-like color grounding has multiple distinct aspects that should not be reduced to a single score; different encoder architectures capture different aspects of color perception.

title: Merging Reaction to Cognition: A Hybrid Cognitive Strategy for Odour Source Localisation in Natural Environments
summary: Chemical pollutants released into the environment are transported by turbulent flows, generating complex, intermittent plume structures that threaten ecosystems and human health. Rapid localisation of emission sources is critical, and field robots equipped with chemical sensors provide a viable means to perform this task. However, inferring source location from sensor readings remains difficult due to sparse detections and the absence of reliable concentration gradients. Existing approaches fall into two paradigms. Bio-inspired strategies rely on reactive behaviours triggered by detections, such as surge-casting, offering efficiency but requiring scenario-specific tuning. Cognitive strategies integrate observations into a probabilistic belief over source location. While more robust, they suffer from excessive exploration and strong dependence on belief accuracy. The Fast-Cognitive algorithm reduced this computational burden but preserved the fundamental limitations. Previous Markov chain analysis revealed that source-directed motions occur roughly twice as often following odour detections, indicating that reactive behaviours naturally emerge within cognitive frameworks. This work proposes a hybrid strategy that explicitly incorporates bio-inspired reactivity into belief-dependent motion planning. It introduces a detection-triggered switching mechanism formalising transitions between crossflow exploration and source-directed motion, prioritising source proximity over information gain. Behavioural parameters are derived directly from belief metrics, enabling adaptive reactivity without manual tuning. The approach is validated through simulations under three turbulence conditions and field experiments with an autonomous surface vehicle in the Mondego River, Portugal. Results show up to 50% reduction in travelled distance, 86% success rate, and 3.2m average localisation error.

AI.tldr: A hybrid cognitive-reactive algorithm for chemical source localization using robots, combining belief-based planning with detection-triggered reactive behaviors, achieving up to 50% distance reduction, 86% success rate, and 3.2m error in field tests.
AI.motivation: Chemical pollutants transported by turbulent flows create complex plume structures. Rapidly localizing emission sources is critical for ecosystem and human health protection, but existing methods (bio-inspired or purely cognitive) have limitations in efficiency, robustness, or computational burden.
AI.method: A hybrid strategy integrating bio-inspired reactivity into belief-dependent motion planning, using a detection-triggered switching mechanism between crossflow exploration and source-directed motion. Behavioral parameters derived from belief metrics enable adaptive reactivity without manual tuning.
AI.result: Validated via simulations under three turbulence conditions and field experiments with an autonomous surface vehicle in the Mondego River, Portugal. Achieved up to 50% reduction in traveled distance, 86% success rate, and 3.2m average localization error.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach significantly improves efficiency over purely cognitive methods while maintaining robustness, successfully merging the reactivity of bio-inspired strategies with the principled reasoning of cognitive frameworks for real-world chemical source localization.

title: Maximally Robust Satisficing Bayesian Optimization
comment: Accepted to the Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI) 2026
summary: Many design tasks can be cast as black-box function optimization, enabling use of Bayesian optimization to find an ideal design with minimal number of trials. However, often we do not actually need the optimum but instead a sufficiently good solution is enough, for instance a material that is durable enough for its intended use. In most cases there are multiple satisfactory solutions, forming a superlevel set of the function, raising a key question of which one to prefer. We answer this by explaining why robustness to input perturbations that may occur when the solution is deployed is a good criterion and by introduce a Bayesian optimization method that efficiently finds satisficing solutions that are robust to maximally large perturbations. In contrast to previous works, we assume the inputs can be accurately controlled during optimization, but will be perturbed after the deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Bayesian optimization method for finding satisficing (good enough) solutions that are robust to input perturbations after deployment, rather than searching for the global optimum.
AI.motivation: Many design tasks only require a sufficiently good solution rather than the exact optimum, and there are often multiple satisfactory solutions forming a superlevel set. The key question is which one to prefer among them.
AI.method: The authors propose a Bayesian optimization approach that efficiently finds satisficing solutions within the superlevel set that are robust to maximally large input perturbations expected after deployment.
AI.result: The method identifies robust satisficing solutions that tolerate large input perturbations, unlike previous works that assumed controllable inputs during both optimization and deployment.
AI.conclusion: Robustness to post-deployment input perturbations is a good criterion for selecting among multiple satisfactory solutions, and the proposed Bayesian optimization method can efficiently find such robust satisficing designs.

title: From Surface Forecasting to Observability Forecasting: A Latent World Model for Cloud-Aware EO Monitoring
summary: The bottleneck of Earth Observation processing chains is not the arrival of new imagery but whether the surface is actually visible when the image arrives. We study this as an observability forecasting problem on EarthNet2021. Given recent multispectral imagery and exogenous weather drivers, the goal is to predict whether the next acquisition will be usable and, if not, when a usable view is likely to return. To do this, we adapt LeWorldModel, a joint-embedding predictive architecture world model, to cloud-aware Earth Observation sequences. The final pipeline converts raw minicubes into episodic HDF5 sequences with five image channels (blue, green, red, near-infrared, cloud mask) and eight meteorological and calendar covariates. The resulting model has 18.0M trainable parameters and is trained from scratch on 23,904 training episodes. The trained leWorldModel is evaluated under a locked protocol: linear probes are fit on train only, calibration choices are set on an internal validation split, and the fitted heads are then frozen for valsplit, IID, OOD, and extreme evaluation. On the full frozen-bundle observability benchmark, LeWorldModel consistently outperforms persistence. For next-step usability, balanced accuracy ranges from 0.769 to 0.887, compared with 0.493 to 0.556 for persistence. For exact first-usable-horizon prediction, accuracy ranges from 0.602 to 0.806, compared with 0.120 to 0.369 for persistence. Against a frozen LightGBM baseline fit on the same training windows, LeWorldModel is better on continuous clear/cloud regression and on exact recovery timing on valsplit, IID, and extreme, while LightGBM is stronger on the simpler binary any-usable-within-six task and is more robust on OOD. In separate sampled diagnostic analyses, LeWM also produces strong ranking-based anomaly signals under synthetic temporal inconsistencies.

AI.tldr: This paper presents LeWorldModel, a joint-embedding predictive architecture adapted for cloud-aware Earth Observation, which forecasts whether satellite imagery will be usable (i.e., not cloud-obscured) and when a usable view will return, outperforming persistence and competing with LightGBM on various benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The bottleneck in Earth Observation processing is not the availability of new imagery, but whether the surface is actually visible (cloud-free) when the image arrives. The authors aim to forecast observability — predicting if the next acquisition will be usable and when a usable view will return.
AI.method: The authors adapt LeWorldModel, a joint-embedding predictive architecture world model, to cloud-aware Earth Observation sequences. They convert raw minicubes into episodic HDF5 sequences with five image channels (blue, green, red, NIR, cloud mask) and eight meteorological/calendar covariates. The model (18.0M parameters) is trained from scratch on 23,904 episodes and evaluated via a locked protocol with linear probes.
AI.result: LeWorldModel consistently outperforms persistence: next-step usability balanced accuracy ranges from 0.769 to 0.887 (vs. 0.493-0.556 for persistence). Exact first-usable-horizon accuracy ranges from 0.602 to 0.806 (vs. 0.120-0.369). Against LightGBM, LeWorldModel is better on continuous clear/cloud regression and exact recovery timing on most splits, while LightGBM is stronger on binary any-usable-within-six tasks and more robust on OOD. LeWM also produces strong ranking-based anomaly signals under synthetic temporal inconsistencies.
AI.conclusion: LeWorldModel is an effective approach for observability forecasting in Earth Observation, significantly outperforming simple persistence and competing well with gradient-boosted baselines on cloud-related forecasting tasks.

title: Learning Forward & Reverse Skills from a Single Unfinished Demonstration for Constrained Manipulation Tasks
summary: Learning from demonstration (LfD) enables robots to learn manipulation skills directly from expert demonstrations but remains challenging for contact-rich tasks involving geometric constraints and force interaction. Existing approaches typically require multiple complete demonstrations and do not support reverse skill execution. In this paper, we present a unified one-shot framework for constrained manipulation that learns both forward and reverse execution from a single, possibly unfinished demonstration. Our method decomposes demonstrations into non-contact and contact phases, with non-contact motion encoded with dynamic movement primitives (DMP), and contact motion represented as a sequence of screw motion primitives segmented by our proposed geometry-driven twist-direction segmentation algorithm. During execution, screw primitives are executed sequentially under admittance-guided pose correction and speed regulation, enabling task completion beyond the demonstrated trajectory length as well as reverse skill execution without additional learning data. Experiments on peg insertion, battery insertion, lock opening, and screw driving tasks demonstrate improved success rates and robustness over segmentation and one-shot trajectory learning baselines. Details are available on the project website: https://tuwien-asl.github.io/LfD-Screw/.

AI.tldr: A unified one-shot LfD framework that learns both forward and reverse constrained manipulation skills from a single, possibly unfinished demonstration by decomposing motion into non-contact (DMP) and contact (screw motion primitives with admittance control) phases.
AI.motivation: Existing LfD methods for contact-rich tasks require multiple complete demonstrations and cannot support reverse skill execution, limiting their practicality.
AI.method: Decomposes demonstrations into non-contact (DMP) and contact phases; contact motion segmented into screw primitives via geometry-driven twist-direction segmentation; executed with admittance-guided pose correction and speed regulation.
AI.result: Improved success rates and robustness over baselines on peg insertion, battery insertion, lock opening, and screw driving tasks; enables reverse execution and task completion beyond demonstrated trajectory length.
AI.conclusion: The proposed one-shot framework effectively handles constrained manipulation with geometric constraints and force interaction, enabling bidirectional skill execution from minimal demonstration data.

title: The Hyperspherical Geometry of CLIP Latent Space: A Semantic Mixture Model
summary: Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) representations form a semantic embedding space governed by cosine similarity, reflecting an intrinsic hyperspherical geometry. However, existing probabilistic interpretations typically rely on Gaussian assumptions, which fail to capture this directional and multimodal structure. We propose a principled density model for the CLIP latent space based on Mixtures of von Mises-Fisher (MovMF) distributions defined on the unit hypersphere. Using the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, we efficiently learn a probabilistic model in which each mixture component corresponds to a coherent semantic concept. This formulation yields a closed-form likelihood naturally aligned with hyperspherical geometry, enabling accurate and interpretable density estimation. Empirically, our model significantly improves long-tailed and out-of-distribution detection and provides a natural semantic decomposition, representing each embedding as a sparse probabilistic combination of interpretable concepts. These results suggest that CLIP latent space is more faithfully characterized as a hyperspherical semantic mixture rather than an isotropic Gaussian, establishing a simple and geometrically consistent probabilistic framework for modeling and understanding multimodal representations. Project page is available at https://xiaoyuzhizi.github.io/movmf-clip/.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Mixtures of von Mises-Fisher (MovMF) distributions to model CLIP's hyperspherical latent space, outperforming Gaussian assumptions for density estimation, out-of-distribution detection, and semantic decomposition.
AI.motivation: CLIP embeddings live on a hypersphere (cosine similarity space), but existing probabilistic models use Gaussian assumptions that ignore this directional geometry, leading to poor density estimation.
AI.method: Use Mixtures of von Mises-Fisher (MovMF) distributions on the unit hypersphere, learned via Expectation-Maximization (EM), where each component corresponds to a semantic concept.
AI.result: Significant improvements in long-tailed and out-of-distribution detection; natural semantic decomposition with sparse probabilistic concept combinations.
AI.conclusion: CLIP latent space is better modeled as a hyperspherical semantic mixture than an isotropic Gaussian, providing a geometrically consistent probabilistic framework.

title: Exploratory, Communicative, and Deployable: Vision-Driven Embodied Agents for Open-World Mobile Manipulation
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. 57 pages. Code available at https://github.com/InternRobotics/REAL
summary:
Real-world deployment of embodied agents requires active exploration, visual grounding, and interactive intent disambiguation. However, existing frameworks often rely on privileged simulator states or assume complete instructions, bypassing realistic deployment challenges. To bridge this gap, we present REAL, an agentic framework for open-world mobile manipulation. REAL establishes sim-to-real-consistent environment APIs without oracle perception and integrates a simulated user to enable human-in-the-loop interaction. Within this environment, we design diverse task compositions to drive data collection, supervised fine-tuning, and online reinforcement learning, systematically optimizing agent performance. To comprehensively evaluate this approach, we introduce REAL-Bench, a benchmark spanning 241 tasks across active exploration, visual distraction, articulated manipulation, and interactive disambiguation.
  Experimental results demonstrate that our trained agent outperforms leading commercial closed-source VLMs on interactive tasks with a 56.9% success rate. Further empirical analysis reveals that our hierarchical training pipeline successfully aligns the model's tool-use capabilities while maintaining robust open-vocabulary reasoning under extended exploration horizons. Finally, we deploy and evaluate our framework on a physical dual-arm mobile robot, where it achieves a 78.3% end-to-end success rate over 60 real-world episodes. These physical trials demonstrate robust zero-shot transferability to unseen household scenarios, validating that our sim-to-real-consistent design successfully bridges the reality gap for long-horizon mobile manipulation. Code is available at https://github.com/InternRobotics/REAL.

AI.tldr: REAL is an agentic framework for open-world mobile manipulation that bridges sim-to-real gaps, integrates human-in-the-loop interaction, and achieves strong performance on both simulated benchmarks (56.9% success rate) and real-world physical trials (78.3% success rate).
AI.motivation: Existing embodied agent frameworks rely on privileged simulator states or assume complete instructions, failing to address realistic deployment challenges such as active exploration, visual grounding, and interactive intent disambiguation.
AI.method: REAL establishes sim-to-real-consistent environment APIs without oracle perception, integrates a simulated user for human-in-the-loop interaction, and uses diverse task compositions for data collection, supervised fine-tuning, and online reinforcement learning to optimize agent performance.
AI.result: The trained agent outperforms leading commercial closed-source VLMs with a 56.9% success rate on interactive tasks. On a physical dual-arm mobile robot, it achieves a 78.3% end-to-end success rate over 60 real-world episodes with robust zero-shot transferability.
AI.conclusion: REAL's sim-to-real-consistent design successfully bridges the reality gap for long-horizon mobile manipulation, demonstrating strong zero-shot transfer to unseen household scenarios through its hierarchical training pipeline.

title: S-squared-VLA: Decoupling Semantic and Spatial Streams in Vision-Language-Action Models for Autonomous Driving
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated remarkable potential for high-level reasoning in autonomous driving, yet they fundamentally struggle to generate precise, low-level control actions. This limitation is rooted in a semantic-physical gap caused by the inherent mismatch between discrete language tokens and continuous trajectory planning. While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) architectures attempt to bridge this gap by unifying perception and control into a single policy, this entanglement creates a new bottleneck. Standard VLAs experience a severe spatial representation collapse, which irreversibly degrades the fine-grained spatial and geometric priors essential for safe, boundary-aware navigation. To address this limitation, we propose the S-squared-VLA, which explicitly decouples the semantic and spatial streams in Vision-Language-Action models. The semantic stream leverages hierarchical bridging to extract multi-scale VLM features for robust intent reasoning. In parallel, an independent spatial stream bypasses the autoregressive language bottleneck, directly preserving uncompressed spatial features from the visual encoder. By integrating auxiliary perception supervision, this stream explicitly equips the model with rich spatial and geometric priors. Finally, a dual-stream planning adapter fuses high-level semantic intent with precise spatial constraints via cascaded attention mechanisms. Evaluations on the NAVSIM closed-loop benchmark show that S-squared-VLA achieves a Predictive Driver Model Score (PDMS) of 87.1, establishing a new state-of-the-art for VLA models under a purely supervised fine-tuning (SFT) setting. By mitigating the spatial representation collapse of traditional VLMs, our framework significantly outperforms baselines, achieving the highest No Collision (NC) rate of 98.4 among all evaluated methods.

AI.tldr: S-squared-VLA proposes a decoupled semantic-spatial architecture for Vision-Language-Action models in autonomous driving, achieving 87.1 PDMS and 98.4% collision-free rate on NAVSIM benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs struggle with precise low-level control due to a semantic-physical gap from discrete-to-continuous mismatch, while VLAs suffer from spatial representation collapse that degrades geometric priors essential for safe navigation.
AI.method: S-squared-VLA decouples semantic and spatial streams: semantic stream uses hierarchical bridging for multi-scale VLM features; spatial stream bypasses autoregressive bottleneck to preserve uncompressed spatial features; dual-stream planning adapter fuses both via cascaded attention.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art 87.1 PDMS score on NAVSIM benchmark and highest No Collision rate of 98.4% among all evaluated methods under supervised fine-tuning setting.
AI.conclusion: Mitigating spatial representation collapse in VLMs through explicit semantic-spatial decoupling significantly improves both planning accuracy and safety in autonomous driving.

title: Optimal and Efficient Contextual Combinatorial Semi-bandits with General Function Approximation
comment: 59 pages (11 pages main body, 17 pages supplementary materials)
summary: We study the contextual combinatorial semi-bandit (CCSB) problem with general reward function approximation. At each round, the learner observes a context, selects a combinatorial action consisting of a subset of basic arms, and receives the reward of each selected arm; the goal is to maximize the cumulative reward over time. We propose SquareCB.Comb, a computationally efficient algorithm that, at each round, solves a convex optimization problem to sample a combinatorial action that balances exploration and exploitation. SquareCB.Comb scales to large arm sets and imposes no structural assumptions on the action set beyond a cardinality bound of $m$ on each combinatorial action. We prove that SquareCB.Comb achieves a minimax optimal regret bound of $O(\sqrt{m A T \log |\mathcal{F}|})$, where $A$ is the number of arms, $m$ is the maximum number of arms in a combinatorial action, $T$ is the time horizon, and $\mathcal{F}$ is the reward function class. In the realizable setting, this bound matches the state-of-the-art regret guarantees achieved by policy search-based algorithms in the more restricted slate recommendation settings, while simultaneously generalizing to arbitrary combinatorial action structures and general reward function approximation.

AI.tldr: SquareCB.Comb is a computationally efficient algorithm for contextual combinatorial semi-bandit problems that achieves minimax optimal regret of O(√(m A T log|F|)) without structural assumptions on action sets, generalizing previous slate recommendation results.
AI.motivation: To solve the contextual combinatorial semi-bandit problem with general reward function approximation, addressing limitations of previous methods that required restrictive structural assumptions on action sets or were limited to slate recommendation settings.
AI.method: SquareCB.Comb algorithm that solves a convex optimization problem at each round to sample a combinatorial action, balancing exploration and exploitation in a computationally efficient manner.
AI.result: Achieves minimax optimal regret bound of O(√(m A T log|F|)), where A is number of arms, m is max arms per action, T is time horizon, and F is reward function class.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method matches state-of-the-art regret guarantees in restricted settings while generalizing to arbitrary combinatorial action structures and general reward function approximation.

title: T3HG-Editor: Text-driven 3D Human Garment Editing with Body Priors Embedded in SMPL-X
summary: While 3D Gaussian Editing (3DGE) has seen substantial progress, text-driven 3D human garment editing remains largely underexplored. Existing 3DGE works typically follow a paradigm that applies 2D editing techniques to multi-view rendered images and updates 3D Gaussians based on the modified images. Extending such methods to 3D human garment editing suffers from low-fidelity outcomes, caused by introduced distortions and garment inconsistencies. A promising breakthrough opportunity arises from the SMPL eXpressive (SMPL-X) model that embodies rich prior information for virtual humans. Motivated by this insight, we propose a text-driven 3D human garment editor termed T3HG-Editor, which delivers high-fidelity and garment consistent results by leveraging geometry and joint priors embedded in SMPL-X. Specifically, T3HG-Editor contains three stages, namely obtainment of editable Gaussians, garment consistent editing, and Gaussian updating with overflow pruning. The obtainment of editable Gaussians begins with seeding Gaussians along SMPL-X normals to generate sufficient near surface Gaussians, followed by a 2D mask constraint that precisely localizes the target Gaussians to be edited. The garment consistent editing aggregates tokens corresponding to the same SMPL-X vertex across multiple views and propagates them to their original views, enforcing garment consistency without requiring additional training. Gaussian updating with overflow pruning employs a Signed Distance Function (SDF) defined on SMPL-X to construct a human distance field, which is then integrated with a 2D semantic mask to prune overflowing Gaussians, thus preventing contamination of non-target regions. Experiments on multiple subjects and diverse garment types demonstrate that T3HG-Editor outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both editing quality and garment consistency.

AI.tldr: T3HG-Editor is a text-driven 3D human garment editing method that leverages SMPL-X geometry and joint priors to achieve high-fidelity, garment-consistent editing through three stages: editable Gaussian obtainment, garment consistent editing via multi-view token aggregation, and Gaussian updating with SDF-based overflow pruning.
AI.motivation: Text-driven 3D human garment editing is underexplored, and existing 3DGE methods suffer from low fidelity due to distortions and garment inconsistencies. SMPL-X offers rich prior information for virtual humans, motivating its use.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) Obtain editable Gaussians by seeding along SMPL-X normals and applying 2D mask constraints; (2) Garment consistent editing by aggregating tokens across views for same SMPL-X vertices; (3) Gaussian updating with SDF-based overflow pruning to prevent contamination of non-target regions.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on multiple subjects and diverse garment types in both editing quality and garment consistency.
AI.conclusion: T3HG-Editor effectively addresses low-fidelity and inconsistency issues in 3D human garment editing by leveraging SMPL-X priors, achieving superior results without requiring additional training.

title: Discriminative Barrier Functions for Safe Adversarial Imitation Learning from Observation
summary: Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) algorithms are powerful tools for learning from and generalizing expert demonstrations, but they often rely on unconstrained exploration, rendering them unsafe for real-world deployment. Meanwhile, Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) can guarantee the safety of control systems, but the analytical design of CBFs can be time-consuming and esoteric. In this work, we address these limitations jointly by constraining reward function candidacy during IRL to the space of CBFs, yielding a formulation that exhibits safe online control with continuous experiential improvement. Crucially, this framework enables the data-driven recovery of barrier functions directly from unlabeled expert observations. We demonstrate that the recovered barrier function is robust to unsafe states entirely absent from the expert data. Furthermore, we benchmark our method against standard IRL baselines in a simulated navigation environment, demonstrating improved safety performance. Finally, we investigate the trade-offs of planning-based versus policy-based IRL methods across both simulation and a real world obstacle avoidance task.

AI.tldr: This paper integrates Inverse Reinforcement Learning with Control Barrier Functions to enable safe, data-driven recovery of barrier functions from expert demonstrations, showing improved safety in navigation tasks.
AI.motivation: IRL methods are unsafe due to unconstrained exploration, while CBFs require time-consuming analytical design. The authors aim to jointly solve both limitations.
AI.method: The authors constrain reward function candidacy during IRL to the space of CBFs, enabling data-driven recovery of barrier functions from unlabeled expert observations.
AI.result: The recovered barrier function is robust to unsafe states absent from expert data. The method outperforms standard IRL baselines in simulated navigation safety. Trade-offs between planning-based and policy-based IRL are investigated in simulation and real-world obstacle avoidance.
AI.conclusion: Constraining IRL reward functions to CBF space yields safe online control with continuous improvement, enabling practical deployment of safe learning from demonstrations.

title: Microstructure-Conditioned Surrogate Models for Graded Multiscale Optimization of Mycelium Composites
summary: Emerging sustainable materials increasingly rely on engineered hierarchy and microstructure to achieve control of their properties and mechanical behavior. Optimizing these materials with controllable microstructures requires efficient multiscale simulations. Data-driven surrogate models for the microscale can accelerate multiscale simulations, but require large amounts of data even for a fixed microstructure. When a range of microstructures is considered, as is the case in multiscale optimization, even more data is needed to train a surrogate. To overcome this challenge, we condition a hybrid physics-data surrogate on microstructural variables using a hypernetwork. This approach enables accurate predictions of multiscale mechanical behavior for a mycelium-woodchip composite material, even when trained on small datasets. The conditioned surrogate makes multiscale simulations of functionally graded structures tractable, and we validate it against a full FE^2 simulation. We optimize a graded multiscale disk, and reduce the peak stress by 42% compared to one with a random microstructure. Then, we go one step further, conditioning the network directly on manufacturing variables that can have a complex influence on the microstructure. This is a practical route to engineer the microscale for desired macroscale behavior. This contribution highlights the benefits of microarchitectured structures and demonstrates how conditioned surrogate models enable their multiscale optimization, which will accelerate the development and design of future sustainable materials and structures.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a hypernetwork-conditioned hybrid physics-data surrogate model that enables efficient multiscale optimization of microarchitectured sustainable materials (mycelium-woodchip composite), achieving 42% peak stress reduction in functionally graded structures with small training datasets.
AI.motivation: Traditional multiscale simulations require large amounts of data even for fixed microstructures, and when optimizing across a range of microstructures, the data demand becomes prohibitive. There is a need for efficient surrogate models that can handle varying microstructures with limited data.
AI.method: A hybrid physics-data surrogate model conditioned on microstructural variables using a hypernetwork architecture. The surrogate is trained on small datasets and enables multiscale simulations of functionally graded structures, validated against full FE^2 simulations. The approach is extended by conditioning directly on manufacturing variables.
AI.result: The conditioned surrogate enables accurate predictions for mycelium-woodchip composites with small datasets. In multiscale optimization of a graded disk, peak stress was reduced by 42% compared to a random microstructure baseline. The method also works with direct manufacturing variable conditioning.
AI.conclusion: Conditioned surrogate models offer a practical route to engineer microscale for desired macroscale behavior, accelerating the development and design of future sustainable materials and structures through efficient multiscale optimization.

title: FreeLit: Paired-Free Indoor Relighting via Physics-Guided Diffusion
comment: Accepted to ACM Multimedia (ACM MM) 2026. This is the accepted manuscript prior to publication
summary:
Image-based indoor scene relighting remains challenging due to the complex interplay between cluttered geometry and local illumination, requiring precise modeling of light position, color, and intensity. Existing data-driven methods implicitly learn this relationship via paired multi-illumination datasets. Nevertheless, this data is costly and fails to scale, which is essential for accurate light-source-level control. Conversely, inverse-rendering methods reduce the data dependency by incorporating physical priors; however, they lack the robustness of intrinsic estimation in challenging conditions.
  In this paper, we present FreeLit, a paired-free framework for controllable indoor relighting that explicitly manipulates light-source location, color, and intensity. Instead of relying on paired supervision, we construct a physics-guided illumination prior from intrinsic scene properties, generating a structured lightmap along with a pseudo-relit image to guide diffusion-based synthesis. To address instability in intrinsic estimation, especially in low-light scenes, we introduce a relighting-guided intrinsic stabilization strategy that enforces illumination-invariant reflectance through structure-aware distillation and consistency constraints. Furthermore, we propose controllability-oriented evaluation metrics to quantify alignment with user-specified illumination color and intensity. Experimental results demonstrate that FreeLit achieves stable, physically consistent, and controllable relighting, with improved robustness in low-light indoor scenes, without requiring paired supervision.

AI.tldr: FreeLit is a paired-free framework for controllable indoor scene relighting that uses physics-guided illumination priors and relighting-guided intrinsic stabilization to manipulate light source location, color, and intensity without requiring costly paired multi-illumination datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing data-driven methods require costly paired multi-illumination datasets that fail to scale, while inverse-rendering methods lack robustness in challenging conditions like low-light scenes.
AI.method: FreeLit constructs a physics-guided illumination prior from intrinsic scene properties to generate structured lightmaps and pseudo-relit images for diffusion-based synthesis. It also introduces a relighting-guided intrinsic stabilization strategy enforcing illumination-invariant reflectance via structure-aware distillation and consistency constraints.
AI.result: FreeLit achieves stable, physically consistent, and controllable relighting with improved robustness in low-light indoor scenes, requiring no paired supervision.
AI.conclusion: FreeLit effectively balances data efficiency and robustness by combining physics-guided priors with diffusion-based synthesis, enabling explicit user control over illumination without paired training data.

title: GigaWorld-Policy-0.5: A Faster and Stronger WAM Empowered by AutoResearch
summary: World Action Models (WAMs) improve robot policy learning by jointly modeling actions and future visual observations, using future scene evolution as dense supervision for physically grounded action generation.
