However, a common design in existing WAMs is to explicitly generate future videos at inference time, incurring substantial computational overhead and hindering real-time closed-loop deployment. GigaWorld-Policy addresses this issue with an action-centered formulation, where future visual dynamics are used during training while action-only decoding is used at inference time. Building upon this framework, we present GigaWorld-Policy-0.5, an enhanced action-centered WAM designed for more efficient robot control. During pretraining, GigaWorld-Policy-0.5 adopts a mixed Action-Conditioned World Modeling (AC-WM) and WAM training strategy. This strengthens the coupling between visual dynamics and robot actions and improves the transferability of action representations for downstream policy learning. For efficient inference, GigaWorld-Policy-0.5 introduces a Mixture-of-Transformers architecture that separates visual dynamics modeling and action generation into specialized experts, reducing active computation during action-only inference and achieving 85 ms inference latency on a local RTX 4090 setup. In addition, we employ an agent-based AutoResearch pipeline to systematically search training configurations, enabling more efficient identification of optimal experimental setups while reducing the time and manual intervention required for hyperparameter tuning. Experiments and ablations show that GigaWorld-Policy-0.5 preserves the training benefits of future visual dynamics while improving inference efficiency for robot control.

AI.tldr: GigaWorld-Policy-0.5 is an action-centered World Action Model that uses future visual dynamics during training but action-only decoding at inference, achieving 85ms latency on RTX 4090 via Mixture-of-Transformers architecture, with an AutoResearch pipeline for systematic hyperparameter tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing World Action Models (WAMs) explicitly generate future videos at inference time, causing substantial computational overhead that hinders real-time closed-loop deployment for robot control.
AI.method: 1) Action-centered formulation: uses future visual dynamics during training but action-only decoding at inference. 2) Mixed AC-WM and WAM pretraining strategy. 3) Mixture-of-Transformers architecture separating visual dynamics modeling and action generation into specialized experts. 4) Agent-based AutoResearch pipeline for systematic training configuration search.
AI.result: Achieves 85 ms inference latency on a local RTX 4090 setup, preserves training benefits of future visual dynamics while significantly improving inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: GigaWorld-Policy-0.5 effectively decouples training-time visual dynamics supervision from inference-time action-only decoding, enabling efficient real-time robot control without sacrificing the learning benefits of world models.

title: Conditional Invertible Neural Networks for Data-Driven UAV Control: A 2-D Proof of Concept
summary: We investigate conditional invertible neural networks (cINNs) as probabilistic inverse-dynamics models for multirotor control. For a planar X8 coaxial multicopter, we learn $p(u \mid s_t, c_t)$ from an incremental nonlinear dynamic inversion (INDI) teacher using rational-quadratic spline coupling and invertible linear mixing. Open-loop reproduction reaches $R^2 = 0.944$, mean CRPS 0.0915, and log-probability-error correlation $ρ= -0.60$. Over 15 closed-loop scenarios, position RMSE matches INDI (9.7 vs. 9.5 m), with 47 percent tracking acceptably; failures separate into attitude divergence under aggressive steps and phase lag under high-frequency references, isolating command bandwidth and data coverage as dominant failure mechanisms.

AI.tldr: cINNs learn probabilistic inverse dynamics for multicopter control, matching INDI performance in closed-loop (RMSE 9.7 vs 9.5 m) with 47% acceptable tracking, while failures stem from command bandwidth limits and data coverage gaps.
AI.motivation: To develop probabilistic inverse-dynamics models that can replace traditional INDI controllers while capturing multimodality and uncertainty in the control distribution.
AI.method: Conditional invertible neural networks (cINNs) with rational-quadratic spline coupling and invertible linear mixing, trained on INDI teacher data to model $p(u \mid s_t, c_t)$.
AI.result: Open-loop: R²=0.944, mean CRPS=0.0915, log-probability-error correlation ρ=-0.60. Closed-loop: position RMSE 9.7 m (INDI: 9.5 m), 47% acceptable tracking.
AI.conclusion: cINNs can approximate INDI performance but are limited by command bandwidth and data coverage; these are the dominant failure mechanisms causing attitude divergence and phase lag.

title: Fine-grained CLIP fine-tuning with self-annotated region alignment
summary: Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) has been shown to have limitations in its fine-grained dense feature representation, due to its pre-training focusing on matching the whole image to a text description. Considering the large data and computational burden in pre-training a vision-language model from scratch, a series of works aim to enhance the fine-grained ability of CLIP through a fine-tuning scheme. However, existing works suffer from a variety of limitations: additional region annotations are usually required, which limits the semantic diversity due to the predefined categories and leads to a large effort to process the training data; and they usually sacrifice CLIP's original ability for global visual representation. To bypass these limitations, we propose SFF-CLIP (Self-annotated Fine-grained Fine-tuning for CLIP), which only uses image-text pairs as input to boost the fine-grained representation ability in the CLIP fine-tuning, while maintaining the global visual-semantic consistency. Concretely, a run-time region-phrase alignment scheme is designed, which obtains concept phrases from the input sentence, and aligns them with corresponding extracted region-based features using text-specific heat maps. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SFF-CLIP leads to significant performance improvements on fine-grained dense feature representation, as well as maintaining the performance of the original CLIP on image-level tasks. Code will be released later.

AI.tldr: SFF-CLIP enhances CLIP's fine-grained dense feature representation through a self-annotated fine-tuning method using only image-text pairs, without requiring additional region annotations, while preserving CLIP's original image-level capabilities.
AI.motivation: CLIP has limited fine-grained dense feature representation due to its global image-text matching pre-training. Existing fine-tuning methods require additional region annotations (costly, limited semantic diversity) or sacrifice CLIP's global representation ability.
AI.method: SFF-CLIP proposes a run-time region-phrase alignment scheme that extracts concept phrases from input sentences and aligns them with region-based features using text-specific heat maps, all without extra annotations.
AI.result: Significant performance improvements on fine-grained dense feature tasks, while maintaining original CLIP performance on image-level tasks.
AI.conclusion: SFF-CLIP effectively bypasses limitations of prior CLIP fine-tuning methods by leveraging self-annotation from image-text pairs, achieving both fine-grained and global representation capabilities.

title: AeroMap3D: Anchoring Monocular UAV 6-DoF Localization to Visual-Geometric-Semantic Map Priors
summary: We present AeroMap3D, a monocular 6-DoF UAV localization system that anchors onboard imagery to visual, geometric, and semantic map priors for GNSS-denied navigation. AeroMap3D addresses two fundamental challenges in map-referenced aerial localization: the cross-view discrepancy between UAV imagery and satellite maps, and the structural inconsistency between bare-earth digital elevation models (DEMs) and urban scenes. First, we introduce a lightweight adapter that enables a dense matcher pretrained on internet-scale generic data to perform reliable UAV-to-map registration without finetuning. By estimating the scale ratio and yaw offset between the UAV image and map tile, the adapter removes the dominant geometric misalignment induced by altitude, camera field of view, and heading before dense correspondence estimation. Second, AeroMap3D lifts 2D UAV-map correspondences onto DEM terrain while using OpenStreetMap annotations to reject semantically unreliable matches before RANSAC-PnP pose estimation, thereby reducing errors caused by unmodeled building heights and off-nadir structures. Delayed map-based pose measurements are further fused with relative-motion priors using a delayed-state EKF for continuous trajectory estimation. Without UAV-Terra3D retraining or tuning, AeroMap3D localizes all trajectories across eight Austin sites within 50 m and achieves 5.88 m mean 3D error over 55 km of flight.

AI.tldr: AeroMap3D is a monocular 6-DoF UAV localization system using satellite maps, DEMs, and semantic annotations for GNSS-denied navigation, achieving 5.88m mean 3D error over 55km flight without retraining.
AI.motivation: To enable reliable UAV localization in GNSS-denied environments by anchoring onboard imagery to map priors, addressing cross-view discrepancy (UAV vs satellite) and structural inconsistency (bare-earth DEMs vs urban scenes with buildings).
AI.method: (1) A lightweight adapter enabling pretrained dense matcher for UAV-to-map registration without finetuning by estimating scale ratio and yaw offset; (2) Lifting 2D correspondences onto DEM terrain with OpenStreetMap semantic filtering to reject unreliable matches; (3) Delayed-state EKF fusing delayed map-based pose measurements with relative-motion priors.
AI.result: Localizes all trajectories across eight Austin sites within 50m, achieving 5.88m mean 3D error over 55km of flight, without any retraining or tuning on UAV-Terra3D.
AI.conclusion: AeroMap3D effectively combines visual, geometric, and semantic map priors for robust GNSS-denied UAV navigation, overcoming cross-view and structural challenges with a lightweight, training-free adaptation approach.

title: DAGR: State-Conditioned Goal Representations via Difference-Aware Goal Cross-Attention
summary: Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning hinges on how the goal is encoded. Contrastive, metric, temporal-distance, and information-theoretic encoders differ in objective. They still share one trait. None of them sees the current state. Such a state-independent embedding cannot mark which part of the goal still needs action. The policy must then recover that cue by inverting both encoders. We propose DAGR. It refines the static embedding of any late-fusion encoder into a state-conditioned one through multi-scale gated cross-attention. A near-identity gated residual preserves the base representation. Difference-aware Goal Cross-Attention then biases the attention scores using a per-token state-goal discrepancy map. On OGBench, DAGR improves navigation. Our ablations trace the gain to the gated residual, not to the difference bias that names the method. On manipulation and puzzle tasks it matches or falls below the base. DAGR is a structured refinement, not a universal improvement.

AI.tldr: DAGR refines goal encodings by making them state-conditioned via gated cross-attention, improving navigation tasks but not universally outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing goal-conditioned RL encoders are state-independent, so they cannot distinguish which parts of a goal still require action, forcing the policy to recover this cue by inverting both encoders.
AI.method: DAGR refines static goal embeddings into state-conditioned ones using multi-scale gated cross-attention with a near-identity residual, plus difference-aware goal cross-attention that biases attention scores with a per-token state-goal discrepancy map.
AI.result: On OGBench, DAGR improves navigation. Ablations show the gain comes from the gated residual, not the difference bias. On manipulation and puzzle tasks it matches or falls below the base.
AI.conclusion: DAGR is a structured refinement that yields task-specific improvements (navigation) but is not a universal improvement across all domains.

title: Learning Speaker Identity Beyond Language and Modality Constraints: Insights from the POLY-SIM 2026 Challenge
summary: Multimodal speaker identification systems typically assume the availability of complete and homogeneous audio-visual modalities during both training and testing, and assume each speaker only speaks a single language. However, in real-world applications, such assumptions often do not hold. Visual or audio information may be missing due to occlusions, camera or microphone failures, or privacy constraints. Multilingual speakers introduce additional complexity due to linguistic variability across languages. These situations constitute substantial challenges for the robustness and generalization capabilities of multimodal speaker identification systems. Aim of the POLY-SIM 2026 challenge is to address these aspects of speaker identification and to provide a standardized setup for the comparison of the proposed solutions.

AI.tldr: The POLY-SIM 2026 challenge addresses multimodal speaker identification under realistic conditions where audio-visual modalities may be incomplete/inhomogeneous and speakers may be multilingual.
AI.motivation: Real-world multimodal speaker identification faces challenges of missing modalities (occlusions, failures, privacy) and multilingual variability, which are not addressed by existing systems assuming complete, homogeneous, single-language data.
AI.method: The POLY-SIM 2026 challenge provides a standardized evaluation framework to compare solutions for robust multimodal speaker identification under incomplete modality and multilingual conditions.
AI.result: Not applicable (this is a challenge proposal/description, not a paper with experimental results).
AI.conclusion: The challenge aims to drive progress in robust and generalizable multimodal speaker identification by addressing realistic conditions of missing modalities and multilingualism.

title: Industrial Dexterity Benchmark: A Hardware-Software Benchmarking Platform for Industrial Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Dexterous manipulation remains a critical bottleneck in industrial automation; tasks such as cable routing, connector insertion, and precision assembly still rely heavily on manual labor despite decades of robotics research. This work presents a progression from classical, modular robotics pipelines toward an end-to-end multimodal imitation-learning framework for industrial dexterous manipulation. As a part of this work, we introduce three key contributions: a set of Industrial Dexterity Benchmark (IDB) boards aimed to mimic datacenter cable management, automotive cable harnesses, and gearbox assembly tasks; a scalable imitation learning framework (DAG-ROS); and a multimodal diffusion-based policy framework (AG-iDP3) that creates models fusing RGB images, point clouds, joint positions, and wrist-frame wrench data. Focusing on the datacenter cable manipulation board, we evaluate the performance of a task involving cleaning a single cable over variations of an end-to-end AI policy using 48 trials per configuration. The best performing configuration, a multimodal expansion Diffusion Policy (DP), includes a multi-view RGB image source passed through an R3M encoder and reaches a 78% grasp and insert combined task success rate. This performance marks a significant improvement over the 36% observed from the single-camera RGB DP baseline. Each of the tested configurations requires only approximately 100 teleoperated demonstrations per task phase. These results indicate that the correct learned policy can outperform classical vision and control robotic methods in robustness, generalization, and deployment efficiency, justifying a shift toward scalable robotic automation for high up-time industrial environments.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a shift from classical robotics to end-to-end multimodal imitation learning for industrial dexterous manipulation, introducing IDB benchmark boards, DAG-ROS framework, and AG-iDP3 policy. The best configuration (multimodal Diffusion Policy with R3M encoder) achieves 78% success on cable manipulation, significantly outperforming the 36% RGB baseline.
AI.motivation: Dexterous manipulation tasks (cable routing, connector insertion, precision assembly) remain heavily reliant on manual labor despite decades of robotics research, highlighting a critical bottleneck in industrial automation.
AI.method: Three contributions: (1) IDB benchmark boards mimicking real industrial tasks; (2) DAG-ROS imitation learning framework; (3) AG-iDP3 multimodal diffusion policy fusing RGB images, point clouds, joint positions, and wrist wrench data. Evaluated via 48 trials per configuration with ~100 teleoperated demonstrations per task phase.
AI.result: Best configuration (multimodal DP with R3M encoder + multi-view RGB) achieves 78% grasp-and-insert success, vs 36% for single-camera RGB DP baseline.
AI.conclusion: Learned policies can outperform classical vision/control methods in robustness, generalization, and deployment efficiency, justifying a shift toward scalable robotic automation for high-up-time industrial environments.

title: Constraint-Driven Model Optimization: An Industry Framework for Selecting Compression and Acceleration Techniques in Modern Machine Learning Systems
summary: The rapid deployment of machine learning systems across cloud, edge, and enterprise environments has brought model optimization to the forefront of systems-engineering. Despite a rich literature spanning quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation, parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT), and inference-time optimization, practitioners are often left navigating these techniques through heuristics rather than principled methodology. We argue that optimization should be formulated as a constraint-driven, multi-objective engineering decision and introduce a unified framework that characterizes any production deployment along five interacting constraint dimensions: data availability, latency budget, memory budget, accuracy tolerance, and retraining budget. Building on this taxonomy, we synthesize empirical gains reported across the research literature and map them to operational constraints rather than algorithmic categories. To ensure practical relevance, we selected these techniques by reviewing recent literature for methods that report measurable improvements against critical deployment bottlenecks. We propose a prescriptive decision framework and provide optimization pipelines for four representative industrial scenarios to illustrate it in practice. To the best of our knowledge, this work provides one of the first structured attempts to formalize model optimization as a constraint-aware, multi-objective engineering process, synthesizing quantitative evidence from the research literature.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a constraint-driven, multi-objective framework for model optimization that maps deployment techniques (quantization, pruning, distillation, PEFT, inference optimization) to five interacting dimensions: data availability, latency budget, memory budget, accuracy tolerance, and retraining budget. It provides a prescriptive decision framework with four industrial scenarios.
AI.motivation: Practitioners currently navigate model optimization techniques through heuristics rather than principled methodology, despite a rich literature. The field lacks a structured, constraint-aware formalism for making optimization decisions in production deployments.
AI.method: The authors introduce a unified framework characterizing production deployments along five constraint dimensions (data, latency, memory, accuracy, retraining budgets). They synthesize empirical gains from research literature, mapping techniques to operational constraints rather than algorithmic categories, and propose prescriptive decision pipelines for four representative industrial scenarios.
AI.result: A constraint-aware, multi-objective optimization framework with a prescriptive decision process, supported by synthesized quantitative evidence from literature and demonstrated through four industrial scenario pipelines.
AI.conclusion: This is one of the first structured attempts to formalize model optimization as a constraint-driven engineering process, bridging the gap between research techniques and practical deployment decisions.

title: WAVE-Stereo: Warp-Aligned Volume Encoding for Stereo Matching
summary: Existing iterative stereo matching methods primarily adopt two types of correspondence representation: explicit matching search via correlation volumes and local residual refinement via warped features, yet the two remain separately modeled. We propose WAVE-Stereo, built on a core insight: correlation volumes and feature warping provide complementary matching cues. \textbf{GeoWarp Correspondence Encoder (GWCE)} encodes matching search, residual alignment, and disparity prior in parallel at the ConvGRU input. To mitigate matching degradation in textureless regions, we propose \textbf{Periodic Global Context Propagation (PGCP)}, which propagates global spatial information in a periodic manner. On five real-world benchmarks -- Middlebury, ETH3D, KITTI 2012, KITTI 2015, and Booster -- WAVE-Stereo achieves competitive zero-shot generalization accuracy without any external foundation model prior, achieving 3.18\% D1-all on KITTI 2015, 4.42\% Bad-2.0 on Booster, and 66ms real-time inference, striking a favorable balance between accuracy and efficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/yamanoko-do/WAVE-Stereo.

AI.tldr: WAVE-Stereo proposes an iterative stereo matching method that jointly models correlation volumes and feature warping via a GeoWarp Correspondence Encoder, plus a Periodic Global Context Propagation module for textureless regions, achieving competitive zero-shot accuracy with real-time inference across five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing stereo methods treat explicit matching search (correlation volumes) and local residual refinement (warped features) as separate processes; the paper identifies that these two provide complementary matching cues and should be modeled jointly.
AI.method: (1) GeoWarp Correspondence Encoder (GWCE) encodes matching search, residual alignment, and disparity prior in parallel at the ConvGRU input. (2) Periodic Global Context Propagation (PGCP) periodically propagates global spatial information to mitigate degradation in textureless regions.
AI.result: 3.18% D1-all on KITTI 2015, 4.42% Bad-2.0 on Booster, 66ms real-time inference, competitive zero-shot generalization on five benchmarks (Middlebury, ETH3D, KITTI 2012, KITTI 2015, Booster).
AI.conclusion: The joint modeling of correlation volumes and feature warping, complemented by periodic global context, achieves a favorable balance between accuracy and efficiency without requiring any external foundation model.

title: PhysClaw-0: A Symbiotic Agentic System for Robot Autonomy via Language Corrections
summary: Autonomous data collection governs the volume and quality of real-world trajectories for manipulation policy learning. Existing pipelines reduce human effort via self-resetting, VLM verification, or language-guided correction, yet episode-scoped fixes must be reissued whenever the same failure recurs, so oversight cost grows with session length rather than with the number of distinct problems. We present PhysClaw-0, a human-robot symbiotic agentic system in which corrections are retained and reused across rounds. The collection loop collects, verifies, and resets autonomously, pausing for a remote operator only when a phase exhausts an explicit retry budget. An LLM parser maps each natural-language utterance to a structured adjustment stored in Corrective Memory, so addressed failure modes typically need not be corrected again under the same conditions. On a real-robot desktop-clearing testbed, PhysClaw-0 matches teleoperation episode success while reducing human working time to 16%. Language corrections improve verifier-human agreement in all four evaluated settings and raise average single-attempt success from 12.5% to 47.5% (arm-selection: 20.0% to 50.0%). Policies fine-tuned on PhysClaw-0 data match teleoperation-trained policy success at a fraction of collection human cost.

AI.tldr: PhysClaw-0 is a human-robot symbiotic system for autonomous data collection that uses Corrective Memory to retain and reuse human corrections across rounds, reducing human working time to 16% while matching teleoperation performance on a desktop-clearing task.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous data collection pipelines require re-issuing episode-scoped fixes whenever the same failure recurs, causing oversight cost to grow with session length rather than with the number of distinct problems.
AI.method: PhysClaw-0 uses an agentic loop that collects, verifies, and resets autonomously, pausing for a remote operator only when a phase exhausts a retry budget. An LLM parser maps natural-language corrections to structured adjustments stored in Corrective Memory for reuse.
AI.result: Reduced human working time to 16%, improved verifier-human agreement in all settings, raised single-attempt success from 12.5% to 47.5%, and matched teleoperation-trained policy success at lower human cost.
AI.conclusion: PhysClaw-0 demonstrates that retaining and reusing corrections across rounds significantly reduces human oversight cost while maintaining task performance, enabling more efficient autonomous data collection for manipulation policy learning.

title: Implementations of Quantum and Classical Topology-Aligned Architectures for Molecular Property Prediction
summary: For low-data and resource-constrained regimes typical of quantum chemistry, parameter-efficient learning is a key objective. Here, we propose a topology-aligned inductive bias in which the model architecture mirrors the molecular bond graph: atoms map to a fixed register of computational units, and bonds determine which pairs interact through shared learnable parameters. This principle is instantiated in two architectures: a variational quantum circuit (Iso-QGNN), and a parameter-matched classical message-passing model (Iso-CGNN). The models are benchmarked on HOMO-LUMO and dipole moment binary classification tasks over the QM9 benchmark. With 64 trainable parameters, the implementations achieve test AUCs of approximately 0.88 (quantum) and 0.91 (classical) on the gap task, and close to 0.78 (both) on the dipole task. The models reach 90% of asymptotic performance within about 250 training molecules and gradient norms remain stable throughout training. These results indicate that the topology-aligned inductive bias is the active ingredient driving parameter efficiency at QM9 scale, with implications for matched-baseline benchmarking in quantum machine learning.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a topology-aligned inductive bias for parameter-efficient learning in quantum chemistry, instantiated in quantum (Iso-QGNN) and classical (Iso-CGNN) models that mirror molecular bond graphs. With only 64 parameters, they achieve strong AUC scores on QM9 tasks (HOMO-LUMO gap ~0.88-0.91, dipole ~0.78), reach 90% performance within ~250 molecules, and show stable gradient norms.
AI.motivation: To achieve parameter-efficient learning in low-data and resource-constrained quantum chemistry regimes by designing an inductive bias that aligns model architecture with molecular bond topology.
AI.method: Two architectures: a variational quantum circuit (Iso-QGNN) and a parameter-matched classical message-passing model (Iso-CGNN), both using a topology-aligned inductive bias where atoms map to computational units and bonds determine pairwise interactions via shared learnable parameters.
AI.result: With 64 trainable parameters, test AUCs of ~0.88 (quantum) and ~0.91 (classical) on HOMO-LUMO gap, ~0.78 (both) on dipole moment classification. Models reach 90% asymptotic performance within ~250 training molecules with stable gradient norms.
AI.conclusion: The topology-aligned inductive bias is the key driver of parameter efficiency at QM9 scale, with implications for matched-baseline benchmarking in quantum machine learning.

title: Towards Spatial Supersensing in the Wild
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Project page: https://vsi-super-wild.github.io/
summary: Humans can efficiently parse continuous sensory streams, from hours to years, scaffolding an internal world model that grounds spatial reasoning and prediction. To mimic this capacity, spatial supersensing challenges multimodal models to move beyond linguistic understanding toward true world modeling. However, their benchmark relies on synthetic long videos, formed by concatenating random short clips, and is mostly limited to household scenes, leaving real-world continuity and diversity underexplored. To address the gap, we introduce $\textbf{VSI-Super-Wild}$, a large-scale benchmark for evaluating spatial supersensing over long temporal horizons in diverse in-the-wild scenes. Notably, inspired by cognitive studies on how humans structure experience, we systematically probe the full triad of world state: the agent (observer), objects (scene items), and the environment (places and global layout). In total, VSI-Super-Wild contains $\textbf{6,980}$ human-verified question-answer pairs derived from $\textbf{442}$ real-world videos spanning 8 scene categories, including long-form recordings exceeding 4 hours. Results on VSI-Super-Wild expose a fundamental disconnect: despite advances in static image understanding, models consistently fail at tasks that require coherent world-state tracking over time. We characterize how performance degrades with world-state complexity and temporal horizon, and diagnose four failure modes: spatial collapse, semantic shortcuts, insufficient update, and instance confusion. This taxonomy reveals that models lack mechanisms to bind objects, agents, and environments into a unified spatial world model, a fundamental gap that defines the path forward for spatial supersensing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VSI-Super-Wild, a large-scale benchmark with 6,980 QA pairs from 442 real-world long videos (up to 4+ hours) across 8 scene categories, testing spatial supersensing. Results show models fail at coherent world-state tracking over time, revealing four failure modes: spatial collapse, semantic shortcuts, insufficient update, and instance confusion.
AI.motivation: Existing spatial supersensing benchmarks rely on synthetic long videos formed by concatenating random short clips, limited to household scenes, lacking real-world continuity and diversity. The authors aim to address this gap by evaluating spatial understanding over long temporal horizons in diverse in-the-wild scenes.
AI.method: The benchmark VSI-Super-Wild contains 6,980 human-verified QA pairs from 442 real-world videos across 8 scene categories, including recordings exceeding 4 hours. It systematically probes the full triad of world state: agent (observer), objects (scene items), and environment (places and global layout), inspired by cognitive studies.
AI.result: Models consistently fail at tasks requiring coherent world-state tracking over time, despite advances in static image understanding. Performance degrades with world-state complexity and temporal horizon. Four failure modes are identified: spatial collapse, semantic shortcuts, insufficient update, and instance confusion.
AI.conclusion: Current models lack mechanisms to bind objects, agents, and environments into a unified spatial world model. This fundamental gap defines the path forward for spatial supersensing research.

title: Algebraic Representability as the Limiting Regime of Grokking: An Exactly Solvable Model with Holomorphic Activations
summary: Neural networks trained on modular arithmetic exhibit grokking, a delayed transition from memorisation to generalisation known to depend on model capacity: too little and the network memorises slowly or not at all, too much and it generalises almost immediately. What happens at the extreme of this spectrum, when the architecture's expressible function class collapses to a finite-dimensional algebraic variety? We study two-layer networks with a holomorphic monomial activation sigma(z)=z^k, trained on modular tasks encoded via roots of unity. Here the network output, regardless of hidden width, is confined to a (k+1)-dimensional subspace of characters of (Z_p)^2, an O(k/p^2) slice of the full function space. We give a complete algebraic characterisation of this subspace: a task is representable if and only if its discrete Fourier support lies on the diagonal u+v = k (mod p), which for linear-phase targets reduces to the arithmetic criterion m+n=k. This is not merely a constraint on eventual generalisation but on memorisation itself: because the outputs are algebraically confined, a non-representable target cannot be fit even on the training set, and we prove a positive lower bound on the training loss, independent of width. Across 585 runs the algebraic prediction matches the observed outcome with 99.8% accuracy, with no memorisation regime and no grokking; outcomes split cleanly into instant success and outright failure. This binary behaviour is the limiting case of the capacity-grokking relationship: when the expressible class shrinks to a fixed algebraic object, the question of when a network will grok dissolves into whether it can represent the target at all. A bottleneck ablation connects this extreme to standard networks, tracing a continuous path from representational failure, through memorisation without generalisation, to grokking with a shrinking gap as capacity grows.

AI.tldr: When neural networks' expressible function class collapses to a finite-dimensional algebraic variety (via monomial activation z^k on modular arithmetic tasks), grokking disappears and is replaced by binary success/failure determined solely by whether the target is algebraically representable. This is the limiting case of the capacity-grokking relationship.
AI.motivation: To understand the extreme end of the capacity-grokking spectrum—what happens when model capacity is so constrained that the network's expressible function class collapses to a finite-dimensional algebraic variety, and whether grokking persists or dissolves.
AI.method: Study two-layer networks with holomorphic monomial activation σ(z)=z^k on modular arithmetic tasks encoded via roots of unity. Provide algebraic characterisation of the (k+1)-dimensional subspace of characters the network can express. Validate across 585 runs and connect to standard networks via bottleneck ablation.
AI.result: A task is representable iff its discrete Fourier support lies on diagonal u+v≡k (mod p). Non-representable targets cannot be fit even on training set (positive lower bound on loss, independent of width). 99.8% accuracy across 585 runs. No grokking—binary success/failure with no memorisation regime.
AI.conclusion: Grokking dissolves into a simple representability question when the expressible class is sufficiently constrained. This binary behaviour is the limiting case of the capacity-grokking relationship, and the bottleneck ablation traces a continuous path from representational failure through memorisation to grokking as capacity grows.

title: Calibrated Closed-Form Uncertainty for Radiative Gaussian Splatting in Sparse-View CT
comment: 19 pages, 6 figures. Equal contribution: Chulin Zhao and Yiran Xu.(Co-first authors) Corresponding author: Yiran Xu (2617300@dundee.ac.uk)
summary: Radiative Gaussian splatting has made sparse-view CT reconstruction fast, but existing methods output point estimates with no notion of where the reconstruction can be trusted. We exploit a property of transmissive X-ray imaging that RGB splatting cannot claim -- projection and voxelization are strictly linear in the per-Gaussian densities -- to equip radiative Gaussians with a variational density posterior whose predictive variance propagates in closed form, exactly, in a single forward pass, in both volume space ($σ^2(x)=\sum_i g_i(x)^2 s_i^2$) and projection space ($\mathrm{Var}[I_p]=\sum_i w_{i,p}^2 s_i^2$). We present the first systematic calibration study for Gaussian-splatting CT (Spearman / AUSE / ECE with temperature scaling), showing that the resulting per-voxel uncertainty ranks true reconstruction error on 14 of 15 scenes of the official benchmark across three view budgets -- 9 of 15 additionally meeting our magnitude-calibration target after a single temperature -- while the perturbation-ensemble heuristic of concurrent work, transplanted to voxel space under the same protocol on our development scenes, does not (rank correlation as low as $-0.08$). We then dissect why uncalibrated acquisition scores can nevertheless select acceptable views, identifying three regimes -- flat (isotropic, balanced), pathological (degenerate coverage), and anisotropic -- and showing, in controlled single-scene testbeds, that principled uncertainty earns a measurable premium only in the last, motivating a coverage-gated, maturity-scheduled acquisition policy; the same calibrated posterior further points toward a dose-adaptive stopping rule, whose experimental validation we leave to future work.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces radiative Gaussian splatting with closed-form variational uncertainty propagation for sparse-view CT, demonstrating calibrated per-voxel uncertainty that outperforms perturbation-based heuristics, while analyzing acquisition regimes where principled uncertainty matters most.
AI.motivation: Existing Gaussian-splatting CT methods output point estimates without uncertainty quantification, making it unclear where reconstructions can be trusted. The authors exploit the linearity of transmissive X-ray imaging to equip radiative Gaussians with principled, closed-form predictive variance.
AI.method: Leverages the linearity of projection and voxelization in per-Gaussian densities to derive a variational density posterior with closed-form predictive variance in both volume space (σ²(x) = Σ g_i(x)² s_i²) and projection space (Var[I_p] = Σ w_{i,p}² s_i²). Evaluated via systematic calibration study (Spearman / AUSE / ECE with temperature scaling).
AI.result: Per-voxel uncertainty ranks true error on 14/15 benchmark scenes across three view budgets; 9/15 meet magnitude-calibration target after single temperature. Perturbation-ensemble heuristic shows poor performance (rank correlation as low as -0.08). Identifies three acquisition regimes (flat, pathological, anisotropic) where principled uncertainty yields measurable benefit only in the anisotropic case.
AI.conclusion: Closed-form variational uncertainty in Gaussian-splatting CT is well-calibrated and superior to perturbation-based heuristics. The benefit is regime-dependent, motivating a coverage-gated, maturity-scheduled acquisition policy. A dose-adaptive stopping rule is proposed for future work.

title: MxGPS: Multiplex Graph Transformers for a Power Grid Foundation Model
summary: Single-task fine-tuning of graph neural networks (GNNs) for power grid problems exhibits a systematic failure mode: models that achieve the lowest in-distribution error degrade the most under topology shift. We term this topology overfitting: the tendency of task-specific gradient signals to encode relational structure particular to the training topologies rather than the underlying physics, causing models to fail on unseen grids despite strong in-distribution performance. To expose and address this failure mode, we introduce MxGPS (Multiplex GPS), a multiplex graph transformer that runs K task-specialised GPS branches over a shared node encoder, jointly trained on Static State Estimation (SSE) and AC Power Flow (PF) via a self-supervised pre-training and multi-task fine-tuning protocol, with a cross-branch attention module evaluated in ablation. The joint SSE+PF objective forces the shared encoder to simultaneously satisfy complementary gradient signals, preventing it from overfitting to topology-specific relational structure. Under a 3-fold sliding-window cross-validation spanning four unseen topologies (14-, 24-, 162-, and 300-bus), MxGPS attains 0% boundary violation rate (BVR) on all four zero-shot Power Flow topologies. Critically, models with substantially lower in-distribution PF error degrade by 190% to 1400% under topology shift, whereas MxGPS degrades by only 39%, an inversion that directly implicates topology overfitting as the failure mechanism rather than insufficient model capacity. With only 1.6M parameters (12x fewer than the GridFM reference baseline), MxGPS demonstrates that multi-task joint training is a principled and parameter-efficient mechanism for topology-agnostic generalisation in power grid foundation models.

AI.tldr: MxGPS is a multiplex graph transformer that jointly trains on Static State Estimation and AC Power Flow tasks to prevent topology overfitting, achieving 0% boundary violation on unseen grids with only 1.6M parameters, while single-task models degrade 190-1400% under topology shift.
AI.motivation: Single-task fine-tuning of GNNs for power grids fails under topology shift—models with lowest in-distribution error degrade the most on unseen grids. The authors term this "topology overfitting" and seek to address this systematic failure mode.
AI.method: MxGPS is a multiplex graph transformer with K task-specialized GPS branches over a shared node encoder. It uses self-supervised pre-training and multi-task fine-tuning on SSE and PF jointly, with a cross-branch attention module. The joint objective forces the shared encoder to satisfy complementary gradient signals, preventing topology-specific overfitting.
AI.result: MxGPS achieves 0% BVR on all four zero-shot Power Flow topologies (14-, 24-, 162-, 300-bus). Under topology shift, single-task models degrade 190-1400% while MxGPS degrades only 39%. Achieves this with 1.6M parameters (12x fewer than GridFM baseline).
AI.conclusion: Multi-task joint training is a principled and parameter-efficient mechanism for topology-agnostic generalization in power grid foundation models. The performance inversion directly implicates topology overfitting as the failure mechanism, not insufficient model capacity.

title: DNA: Dual-stage Native Attribution for Generated Image Source Tracing
summary: The rapid evolution of image generation has produced numerous within-family variants, making source-model attribution of suspect images increasingly important for digital forensics. Existing proactive methods rely on watermark embedding or model modification, which may degrade visual quality and limit deployment flexibility. Passive methods often rely on large-scale supervised training or a single reconstruction signal, limiting their ability to handle unknown sources and distinguish highly similar within-family variants. We observe that attribution signals in latent generative models are naturally stratified across architectural levels: VAE-level cues reflect family-shared information, whereas backbone-level cues capture variant-specific behaviors. Motivated by this insight, we propose Dual-stage Native Attribution (DNA), a coarse-to-fine framework that follows this hierarchy without additional neural-network training. The coarse-grained stage uses Autoencoder Double-Reconstruction (AEDR) for efficient open-set family-level screening. The fine-grained stage performs closed-set model-level attribution with Native Prediction Consistency (NPC), which compares native prediction errors of within-family variants across multiple noise levels under semantic conditioning and attributes the source via normalized calibrated scores. To enable systematic evaluation, we construct DNA-30K, a benchmark for within-family variant attribution under open-set family-level evaluation. It comprises 30,000 images generated by 24 candidate models across six families spanning both denoising diffusion and flow matching, plus non-candidate generated and natural images as unknown sources. Experiments show that DNA achieves 89.11% end-to-end attribution accuracy on a task where random guessing accuracy is below 1% and outperforms the strongest baseline by 33.81% even when AEDR is used as the coarse-grained stage.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DNA (Dual-stage Native Attribution), a training-free coarse-to-fine framework for attributing generated images to their source models—even distinguishing highly similar within-family variants—by leveraging naturally stratified attribution signals in latent generative models (VAE-level for family, backbone-level for specific model). It achieves 89.11% attribution accuracy on the challenging DNA-30K benchmark, outperforming baselines by 33.81%.
AI.motivation: The rapid evolution of image generation has produced numerous within-family variants, making source-model attribution of suspect images increasingly important for digital forensics. Existing proactive methods degrade visual quality or limit flexibility, while passive methods struggle with unknown sources and distinguishing highly similar within-family variants.
AI.method: The authors propose a Dual-stage Native Attribution (DNA) framework that follows the natural hierarchy of attribution signals in latent generative models: (1) a coarse-grained stage using Autoencoder Double-Reconstruction (AEDR) for open-set family-level screening, and (2) a fine-grained stage using Native Prediction Consistency (NPC) that compares native prediction errors of within-family variants across multiple noise levels under semantic conditioning, attributing the source via normalized calibrated scores—all without additional neural-network training.
AI.result: Experiments show DNA achieves 89.11% end-to-end attribution accuracy on the DNA-30K benchmark (24 candidate models across six families, including non-candidate and natural images as unknowns), where random guessing accuracy is below 1%, and outperforms the strongest baseline by 33.81% even when AEDR is used as the coarse-grained stage.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DNA framework effectively addresses the challenging task of within-family variant attribution without training, leveraging the natural stratification of attribution signals in latent generative models, and significantly outperforms existing methods on a comprehensive benchmark.

title: Mono-Z Dark Matter Search with Neural Spline Flows Using CMS Run 2015D Open Data
comment: 29 pages, 9 figures, 17 tables. The first two authors contributed equally to this work
summary: We report a search for dark matter (DM) produced in association with a leptonically decaying \(Z\) boson at \(\sqrt{s}=13\) TeV using CMS Run 2015D open data corresponding to an integrated luminosity of \(2.32\,\mathrm{fb}^{-1}\) together with simplified-model Monte Carlo simulation. Events are selected in the mono-\(Z\rightarrow\ell^+\ell^-\) final state in both the \(μμ\) and \(ee\) channels. Forty kinematic observables are extracted from MINIAOD and MINIAODSIM, cleaned with physics-motivated selections, and reduced to a 37-dimensional feature vector. Five Neural Spline Flows are trained independently to model Standard Model background and mediator-specific DM signal densities. The per-event test statistic is constructed from the log-likelihood ratio between the signal and background density estimates, providing sensitivity across the full kinematic phase space without requiring a hard upper \(\mathrm{MET}\) threshold. A simultaneous profile-likelihood fit combining the two channels yields observed (expected) 95\% confidence level upper limits on the signal-strength parameter of \(μ<0.0177\) (\(0.0018\)) for the scalar mediator, \(μ<0.0362\) (\(0.0039\)) for the vector mediator, and \(μ<0.0498\) (\(0.0069\)) for the axial-vector mediator. The observed limits are weaker than expected because of a residual high-\(\mathrm{MET}\) background-modeling discrepancy rather than evidence for a DM signal. To our knowledge, this is the first application of Neural Spline Flow likelihood-ratio scoring to a mono-\(Z\) dark matter search using CMS Run 2015D open data simultaneously in the \(μμ\) and \(ee\) channels.

AI.tldr: This paper reports a dark matter search using CMS open data (2015D, 2.32 fb⁻¹ at 13 TeV) in the mono-Z→ℓ⁺ℓ⁻ channel. It employs Neural Spline Flows to model background and DM signal densities, constructs a per-event log-likelihood ratio test statistic, and sets observed (expected) 95% CL upper limits on signal strength for scalar, vector, and axial-vector mediators. Observed limits are weaker than expected due to high-MET background modeling discrepancy.
AI.motivation: The search targets dark matter produced in association with a leptonically decaying Z boson (mono-Z channel). It aims to test simplified DM mediator models without imposing a hard MET threshold, leveraging machine learning (Neural Spline Flows) for sensitivity across the full kinematic phase space.
AI.method: Analyzed CMS Run 2015D open data (2.32 fb⁻¹) at √s=13 TeV. Selected mono-Z→ℓ⁺ℓ⁻ events (μμ and ee channels). Extracted 40 kinematic observables → cleaned to 37-dimensional feature vectors. Trained 5 Neural Spline Flows to model SM background and mediator-specific DM signal densities. Constructed per-event test statistic from log-likelihood ratio. Performed simultaneous profile-likelihood fit combining both channels.
AI.result: Observed (expected) 95% CL upper limits on signal strength μ: scalar mediator <0.0177 (0.0018), vector mediator <0.0362 (0.0039), axial-vector mediator <0.0498 (0.0069). Observed limits weaker than expected due to a residual high-MET background-modeling discrepancy, not evidence of DM.
AI.conclusion: No dark matter signal observed. The Neural Spline Flow likelihood-ratio approach applied to mono-Z DM search in both μμ and ee channels is novel. The discrepancy in high-MET modeling suggests room for improved background estimation in future analyses with larger datasets.

title: Barnamala: Parameter-Efficient Handwritten Devanagari Recognition at Benchmark Saturation
comment: 14 pages, 2 figures, 7 tables. Code and artifacts available at https://github.com/Ampixa/barnamala
summary: We built a compact convolutional network (1.11 M parameters) for 46-class DHCD Devanagari recognition and reached 99.73%, the highest reported at 15.6x smaller than prior state-of-the-art. We have effectively reached the saturation point: every model tested, large teacher ensembles included, hits the same 11-error intrinsic floor. No configuration achieves a statistically clear win under exact McNemar tests with Wilson confidence intervals. Even without knowledge distillation, our student matches the nearest large-model baseline (17.32 M parameters; McNemar $p = 0.345$). Outside of DHCD, zero-shot on CMATERdb digits gives 76.6% and fine-tuning reaches 97.8%; corruption robustness is also far better than large baselines (mean corruption accuracy 75.7% vs. 38.7%). All artifacts are at https://github.com/Ampixa/barnamala.

AI.tldr: A compact 1.11M-parameter CNN achieves 99.73% accuracy on DHCD Devanagari recognition, reaching an apparent performance saturation point, while also demonstrating strong corruption robustness and competitive zero-shot/fine-tuned performance on CMATERdb digits.
AI.motivation: To determine whether Devanagari character recognition has reached a performance saturation point, and to show that a highly compact model can match or exceed much larger architectures while reducing computational cost.
AI.method: A compact convolutional network (1.11M parameters) is trained on the 46-class DHCD dataset. Statistical significance is evaluated using exact McNemar tests with Wilson confidence intervals. The model is also tested zero-shot and fine-tuned on CMATERdb digits, and evaluated on corruption robustness.
AI.result: 99.73% accuracy on DHCD (highest reported, 15.6x smaller than prior SOTA); no statistically significant win over baselines; zero-shot 76.6%, fine-tuned 97.8% on CMATERdb; corruption robustness 75.7% vs. 38.7% for large baselines.
AI.conclusion: The DHCD task has reached an intrinsic error floor where all models perform similarly; a compact model is sufficient, and offers superior corruption robustness over larger baselines.

title: NodeImport: Imbalanced Node Classification with Node Importance Assessment
summary: In real-world applications, node classification on graphs often faces the challenge of class imbalance, where majority classes dominate training, resulting in biased model performance. Traditional GNNs often struggle in such scenarios, as they tend to overfit to majority classes while underrepresenting minority classes. Existing solutions, which either prioritize nodes based on class size or synthesize new nodes for minority classes, often fall short of effectively addressing this imbalance issue. This paper introduces an approach to class-imbalanced node classification by utilizing a balanced meta-set for importance measurement, where a training node is considered significant if it enhances model performance under an unbiased setting. Our method identifies important nodes that can counteract class imbalance and utilizes them for model training, allowing for fine-grained and dynamic node selection throughout the training process. We theoretically derive a formula to directly assess node importance, reducing computational overhead and providing an intuitive threshold for node selection. Guided by this metric, we develop a novel framework that filters valuable labeled, unlabeled, and synthetic nodes that enhance model performance in an unbiased context. A key advantage of this framework is its separation of the synthetic node generation process from the filtering process, ensuring compatibility with various node generation methods. Furthermore, we introduce a strategy to construct a high-quality meta-set that closely approximates the overall feature distribution, ensuring robust representation of each class. We evaluate our framework, NodeImport, across multiple datasets using popular GNN architectures, demonstrating its superiority over existing baselines. Our results highlight the flexibility and effectiveness of the framework in mitigating class imbalance, leading to improved outcomes.

AI.tldr: NodeImport is a framework for class-imbalanced node classification on graphs that selects important nodes (labeled, unlabeled, and synthetic) via a balanced meta-set to measure node importance, enabling dynamic, fine-grained node selection to mitigate class imbalance bias.
AI.motivation: Real-world node classification suffers from class imbalance where majority classes dominate training, causing biased GNN performance. Existing methods (class-prioritized weighting or synthetic node generation) fail to effectively address this issue.
AI.method: The paper proposes NodeImport, which uses a balanced meta-set to compute node importance scores with a theoretically derived formula. It filters valuable labeled, unlabeled, and synthetic nodes dynamically during training, and decouples synthetic node generation from filtering for compatibility with various generation methods.
AI.result: Evaluated across multiple datasets and GNN architectures, NodeImport outperforms existing baselines, demonstrating improved class-imbalance mitigation and flexible node selection.
AI.conclusion: NodeImport effectively addresses class imbalance in node classification through principled, dynamic importance-based node selection, with theoretical support for computational efficiency and compatibility with diverse node generation approaches.

title: Groc-PO: Grounded Context Preference Optimization for Truthful Multimodal LLMs
summary: Despite the rapid progress of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), they still suffer from untruthfulness issues, such as visual hallucinations, content fabrication, and unfaithful reasoning, which substantially undermine their faithfulness and practical utility. Alignment methods based on human preference, such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), have been widely adopted to address these issues. However, multimodal reasoning errors often propagate across stages, and final-answer errors can often be traced to mistakes in early grounding stages, yet standard DPO typically applies preference optimization at the final-answer level. This credit-assignment challenge means that supervision for early grounding stages is indirect rather than stage-specific, making it difficult to suppress error propagation arising from grounding drift and context inconsistency. To address this, we propose Grounded Context Preference Optimization (Groc-PO), a grounded preference optimization framework for MLLMs. We further construct the Grounded Context Preference Dataset (GCPD), organizing multi-stage preference samples around three stages of Object Grounding, Contextual Grounding, and Grounded Reasoning, to capture the formation, integration, and utilization of grounded context. By introducing more explicit preference supervision over multiple grounded stages, Groc-PO strengthens context-dependent reasoning and mitigates cross-stage error propagation. Extensive experiments show that, compared with standard DPO and other strong baselines, Groc-PO achieves improved performance in hallucination mitigation, faithful reasoning, and overall reliability, supporting the value of more explicit grounded supervision for trustworthy multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Groc-PO, a grounded preference optimization framework for Multimodal LLMs that applies stage-specific preference supervision across object grounding, contextual grounding, and grounded reasoning stages (via the GCPD dataset), outperforming standard DPO in reducing hallucinations and improving faithful reasoning.
AI.motivation: Standard DPO applies preference optimization only at the final-answer level, failing to address error propagation from early grounding stages (e.g., visual hallucinations, grounding drift, context inconsistency) in multimodal reasoning pipelines.
AI.method: The authors propose Groc-PO, a grounded preference optimization framework, along with the GCPD dataset that organizes multi-stage preference samples around three stages: Object Grounding, Contextual Grounding, and Grounded Reasoning, providing stage-specific preference supervision.
AI.result: Groc-PO achieves improved performance over standard DPO and strong baselines in hallucination mitigation, faithful reasoning, and overall reliability.
AI.conclusion: More explicit grounded supervision across multiple stages strengthens context-dependent reasoning and mitigates cross-stage error propagation, supporting trustworthy multimodal reasoning.

title: M$^\text{4}$World: A Multi-view Multimodal Driving World Model for Interactive Object Manipulation and Minute-long Streaming
summary: Driving-world generation has emerged as a core capability for scalable autonomous-driving simulation, yet existing methods remain limited in object-level controllability and long-horizon stability. We present M$^\text{4}$World, a Multi-view and Multimodal generative driving world model that synthesizes future surround-view video streams and synchronized LiDAR scans while supporting interactive object Manipulation and stable Minute-long streaming. Fine-grained object manipulation is realized through a flexible conditioning interface that supports explicit control over both the spatial layout and visual appearance of individual objects. Stable minute-long streaming, on the other hand, is achieved through a multi-stage training framework that enables online causal generation in only four denoising steps while maintaining coherent world dynamics throughout extended rollouts. Building on these components, we introduce an efficient few-clip post-training as well as a suite of visual reference-conditioned generation models, preserving general generation ability while allowing rare-case customization for long-tail controllability. To assess controllability beyond realism, we further introduce an automated VLM-based judging pipeline that evaluates scene-level condition adherence, view-wise object controllability, and cross-view object consistency. Comprehensive experiments show that M$^\text{4}$World consistently delivers high generation quality, precise controllability, and stable minute-long streaming. Together with downstream long-tail augmentation and scene editing, these results demonstrate the potential of M$^\text{4}$World for controllable, scalable driving simulation.

AI.tldr: M$^\text{4}$World is a multi-view, multimodal generative driving world model that synthesizes surround-view video and LiDAR scans with object-level controllability and stable minute-long streaming, enabled by a flexible conditioning interface and multi-stage training.
AI.motivation: Driving-world generation lacks object-level controllability and long-horizon stability for scalable autonomous-driving simulation.
AI.method: Proposes a multi-view, multimodal generative model with (1) flexible conditioning for spatial layout and visual appearance control, (2) multi-stage training for 4-step causal generation, (3) few-clip post-training for rare-case customization, and (4) an automated VLM-based judging pipeline.
AI.result: Achieves high generation quality, precise controllability, and stable minute-long streaming; demonstrates downstream long-tail augmentation and scene editing capabilities.
AI.conclusion: M$^\text{4}$World shows strong potential for controllable, scalable driving simulation.

title: Heavy-Tailed Flow Matching via Random Clocks
summary: Heavy-tailed data arise in many domains where rare events carry disproportionate importance, such as imbalanced image datasets, financial returns, and weather extremes. Standard diffusion and flow-matching models typically begin from Gaussian noise or Gaussian source distributions, which yield tractable training targets but provide a poor inductive match for heavy-tailed data. We propose Heavy-Tailed Flow Matching via Random Clocks (HTFM), a framework that portrays heavy-tailed sources as mixtures of clock-conditioned Gaussian sources. Conditioning on a given clock path, the source distribution and flow are Gaussian; marginalizing over the clock gives a Gaussian scale mixture covering Gaussian, $α$-stable, and Student-t families. To make the clock-conditioned vector field practical, we encode the path-valued clock using truncated logsignature features, allowing the velocity field to adapt to the realized conditional space with negligible overhead. Empirically, on 2D imbalanced $α$-stable mixtures, CIFAR10-LT, and HRRR weather fields, HTFM improves mode coverage, sample quality, and tail-statistic recovery over Gaussian flow matching and competitive heavy-tailed baselines, while retaining the low-NFE sampling advantage of flow matching. Moreover, the random-clock formulation further provides a practical tail-control interface: by varying only the clock law or tail parameter, the same architecture can calibrate the ``heaviness'' of generated tails across different distribution families.

AI.tldr: HTFM extends flow matching to heavy-tailed data by using a random clock to mix Gaussian sources, enabling better tail recovery and sample quality across imbalanced, financial, and weather extremes.
AI.motivation: Standard diffusion/flow models assume Gaussian sources, which poorly match heavy-tailed data (e.g., imbalanced images, financial returns, weather extremes). This mismatch leads to inadequate tail modeling and poor inductive bias.
AI.method: HTFM models heavy-tailed sources as mixtures of Gaussian sources conditioned on a random clock path. The clock is encoded via truncated logsignature features, making the vector field practical. Marginalizing over the clock yields Gaussian scale mixtures (Gaussian, α-stable, Student-t).
AI.result: On 2D α-stable mixtures, CIFAR10-LT, and HRRR weather fields, HTFM improves mode coverage, sample quality, and tail-statistic recovery over Gaussian flow matching and heavy-tailed baselines, while retaining low-NFE sampling.
AI.conclusion: The random-clock formulation provides a practical tail-control interface: by varying only the clock law or tail parameter, the same architecture can calibrate tail heaviness across distribution families.

title: Anatomically Faithful but Temporally Blind: Auditing Attribution for Left-Ventricular Ejection-Fraction Estimation from Echocardiography
summary:
Background and Objective: Deep video models estimate left-ventricular ejection fraction (EF) from echocardiography with near-expert accuracy, and post-hoc attribution (Chefer relevance for transformers, Grad-CAM for CNNs) is increasingly used to certify that models "look at the right place." Yet whether these explanations are faithful both spatially and temporally is unaudited. Because EF is defined by the end-systolic (ES) and end-diastolic (ED) frames, a faithful explanation must localize the left ventricle (space) and the decisive frames (time).
  Methods: We fine-tune two distinct EF regressors on EchoNet-Dynamic -- a self-supervised VideoMAE transformer and a Kinetics-pretrained R(2+1)D CNN -- and audit each with architecture-matched attribution along three axes: intersection-over-relevance (IoR) against LV masks, deletion AUC, and a temporal localization index on ES/ED frames, each relative to chance with per-case 95% CIs over 50 studies. A tubelet-occlusion probe separates attribution failure from model behavior.
  Results: Both models are anatomically faithful -- IoR 2.91x (VideoMAE) and 1.98x (R(2+1)D) above chance -- yet temporally blind: temporal localization is indistinguishable from chance (0.97--1.00) and no better than random attribution. Occlusion shows the models do not preferentially rely on ES/ED (0.90x chance), so temporal blindness reflects model behavior, not an attribution artifact.
  Conclusions: Spatial faithfulness does not imply temporal faithfulness. Attribution can certify anatomical grounding while masking that a model ignores the clinically decisive frames -- a caution for XAI-based validation of video diagnostic models and a call for temporally-aware training and evaluation.

AI.tldr: Deep video models for EF estimation are spatially faithful (focus on left ventricle) but temporally blind—they ignore clinically decisive ES/ED frames—revealing that spatial attribution alone cannot certify clinically meaningful explanations.
AI.motivation: Post-hoc attribution methods (e.g., Grad-CAM, Chefer relevance) are used to certify that models "look at the right place," but whether these explanations are faithful both spatially and temporally has not been audited. Since EF is defined by ES/ED frames, temporal faithfulness is critical.
AI.method: Fine-tuned two EF regressors (VideoMAE transformer, R(2+1)D CNN) on EchoNet-Dynamic. Audited with architecture-matched attribution along three axes: IoR against LV masks, deletion AUC, and temporal localization index on ES/ED frames, each with per-case 95% CIs over 50 studies. Used tubelet-occlusion probe to separate attribution failure from model behavior.
AI.result: Both models are spatially faithful (IoR 2.91x for VideoMAE, 1.98x for R(2+1)D above chance) but temporally blind (temporal localization indistinguishable from chance, 0.97–1.00). Occlusion confirmed models do not preferentially rely on ES/ED frames (0.90x chance), indicating temporal blindness reflects model behavior, not attribution artifact.
AI.conclusion: Spatial faithfulness does not imply temporal faithfulness. Attribution can certify anatomical grounding while masking that models ignore clinically decisive frames. This is a caution for XAI-based validation of video diagnostic models and motivates temporally-aware training and evaluation.

title: Relevance-Aware Rule: Structural Deletion of Irrelevant Conditions in Decision Trees
summary: Decision trees generate interpretable if--then rules, yet they contain irrelevant conditions (IRCs). These IRCs arise from the structural mechanism of tree splitting and persist even in modern optimal sparse tree induction algorithms. Existing IRC deletion methods overlook this structural mechanism; therefore, they either preserve the original tree too loosely to remain reliable, or too strictly to achieve meaningful simplification. This study provides theoretical foundations for reliable IRC deletion by establishing theorems and propositions related to the underlying IRC mechanism. The key finding is that a binary split shifts class proportions in opposite directions relative to the parent. Specifically, an increase in the class-1 proportion along one branch necessitates an increase in the class-0 proportion along its sibling, thereby generating a C1-link and a C0-link. Based on this structural fact, we propose a structural IRC deletion framework. Relative to each leaf, links that increase the leaf-class proportion are matched, whereas links that increase the proportion of the opposite leaf-class are mismatched. These mismatched links are flagged as structurally suspicious IRC candidates. Rather than deleting them outright, the framework rigorously diagnoses their relevance by assessing prediction reliability. It selectively deletes conditions that are structurally and empirically irrelevant, while strictly protecting those whose deletion would reduce the rule's reliability. Experimental results confirm that the proposed framework achieves substantial rule simplification without sacrificing the reliability of the original tree.

AI.tldr: Decision trees contain irrelevant conditions (IRCs) due to their splitting mechanism. This paper provides theoretical foundations for IRC deletion and proposes a structural IRC deletion framework that identifies mismatched links (C1/C0 links) to selectively delete irrelevant conditions while preserving prediction reliability.
AI.motivation: Decision trees contain irrelevant conditions (IRCs) caused by the structural mechanism of tree splitting. Existing deletion methods either preserve the original tree too loosely (unreliable) or too strictly (no meaningful simplification). The authors aim to provide theoretical foundations for reliable IRC deletion.
AI.method: The paper establishes theorems about binary splits shifting class proportions in opposite directions per branch (C1-link and C0-link). A structural IRC deletion framework is proposed: (1) match links that increase leaf-class proportion, (2) identify mismatched links as IRC candidates, (3) diagnose relevance via prediction reliability assessment, (4) selectively delete structurally and empirically irrelevant conditions while protecting reliability-critical ones.
AI.result: The proposed framework achieves substantial rule simplification without sacrificing the reliability of the original decision tree.
AI.conclusion: The structural IRC deletion framework effectively removes IRCs by leveraging theoretical insights about split mechanisms and prediction reliability, balancing simplification with reliability preservation.

title: EgoProceVQA: A Novel Egocentric Procedural Understanding Task with Self-Skill-Exploration Agent
summary: Most daily activities are inherently procedural. However, existing evaluations for egocentric video understanding seldom address procedural understanding and largely overlook complex key-step-level reasoning under the widely used video question answering (VQA) paradigm for MLLMs. Such capabilities are crucial for building procedural AI assistants deployable on wearable devices. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Egocentric Procedural Understanding VQA task (EgoProceVQA), which systematically evaluates egocentric procedural reasoning abilities of current MLLMs and agents through six types of key-step-centric questions. Furthermore, we develop EgoProceGen, a data generation platform that efficiently constructs QA data tailored to different question types. Based on this platform, we build a benchmark with 3,600 questions, four common procedural scenarios, and 31 everyday procedural tasks. Evaluations on EgoProceVQA show that existing MLLMs and agents still have substantial room for improvement in procedural understanding. Therefore, we further propose EgoProceAgent, a self-skill-exploration agentic framework. We design a generic tool library for procedural understanding and a standardized sub-skill library shared across tools and models, enabling self-exploration without ground-truth supervision. By exploring how to compose and select sub-skills, the agent discovers effective skill strategies for diverse problems, and attains state-of-the-art performance among open-source models on multiple tasks. Together, our benchmark, generation platform, and agentic framework establish a unified foundation for EgoProceVQA. Project page: https://z1oong.github.io/EgoProceVQA/.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces EgoProceVQA, a benchmark for egocentric procedural understanding with 3,600 key-step-centric questions across 31 everyday tasks. It also presents EgoProceGen (a data generation platform) and EgoProceAgent (a self-skill-exploration agentic framework) that achieves SOTA among open-source models.
AI.motivation: Existing egocentric video evaluations rarely address procedural understanding and overlook complex key-step-level reasoning under the VQA paradigm, which is crucial for building procedural AI assistants on wearable devices.
AI.method: The authors propose (1) EgoProceVQA task with six types of key-step-centric questions, (2) EgoProceGen data generation platform, and (3) EgoProceAgent framework with a generic tool library and standardized sub-skill library enabling self-exploration without ground-truth supervision.
AI.result: Existing MLLMs and agents show substantial room for improvement; EgoProceAgent achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models on multiple tasks.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark, generation platform, and agentic framework together establish a unified foundation for egocentric procedural understanding VQA, demonstrating that self-skill-exploration can improve procedural reasoning capabilities.

title: AI-Augmented Adaptive Digital Twin Modeling for Brain Tumor Evolution Prediction and Treatment Scheduling
summary: Brain tumor progression exhibits spatially heterogeneous growth, patient-specific treatment response, and complex interactions with surrounding anatomy, making accurate long-term prediction challenging. We propose an AI-augmented adaptive digital twin (DT) framework for brain tumor evolution prediction and treatment scheduling. The framework integrates an interpretable reaction--diffusion (RD) model, a 3D residual learning module for model-form correction, patient-specific DT updating during recursive rollout, and model predictive control (MPC) for constrained chemotherapy and radiotherapy scheduling. Experiments on 387 synthetic tumor trajectories with 120-step evolution show that the baseline RD model captures tumor location and overall temporal behavior but underestimates heterogeneous tumor burden during long-horizon prediction. Hybrid RD--residual modeling reduces masked voxel-wise mean squared error by 84.3% and increases Dice overlap by 43.5% relative to the RD baseline under dense simulated observations. Online DT updating further reduces mean squared error by 45.9% and improves Dice overlap by 9.6% compared with the non-updated hybrid model. In MPC-based scheduling simulations, the updated DT controller reduces final tumor burden by 22.4% relative to a fixed treatment schedule under the terminal-burden objective. Together, these results demonstrate a unified framework for patient-specific initialization, mechanistic modeling, adaptive learning, and constrained treatment optimization. Although validated using patient-data-informed synthetic trajectories rather than clinical longitudinal data, the proposed framework establishes a foundation for future translation to real-world adaptive treatment planning.

AI.tldr: An AI-augmented adaptive digital twin framework integrating reaction-diffusion models, residual learning, and model predictive control improves brain tumor evolution prediction and treatment scheduling, reducing MSE by 84.3% and tumor burden by 22.4% in synthetic experiments.
AI.motivation: Brain tumor progression is spatially heterogeneous, patient-specific, and interacts with complex anatomy, making accurate long-term prediction and treatment scheduling extremely challenging.
AI.method: The framework combines an interpretable reaction-diffusion (RD) model with a 3D residual learning module for model-form correction, online digital twin updating during recursive rollout, and model predictive control (MPC) for constrained chemotherapy and radiotherapy scheduling.
AI.result: Hybrid RD-residual modeling reduced masked voxel-wise MSE by 84.3% and increased Dice overlap by 43.5% over RD baseline. Online DT updating further reduced MSE by 45.9% and improved Dice by 9.6%. MPC-based scheduling reduced final tumor burden by 22.4% versus fixed schedules.
AI.conclusion: The unified framework demonstrates feasibility for patient-specific initialization, mechanistic modeling, adaptive learning, and constrained treatment optimization, laying groundwork for real-world adaptive treatment planning despite current validation on synthetic data.

title: Prospective clinical indication, post-hoc report leakage, and fusion design in multi-image chest radiograph classification: a patient-clustered evaluation
summary: Chest radiograph datasets often combine multiple images with Clinical Indication, Findings, and Impression, although these inputs are produced at different stages of care. We evaluated 15,000 ReXGradient-160K studies with two readable images and five CheXbert-derived report observations. Frozen DenseNet-121 and Bio+ClinicalBERT encoders were used to compare image-only, Indication-only, fixed-order multimodal, random-swap, DeepSets, and SectionGuard-MI models. Findings and Impression were evaluated only as post-hoc leakage controls. Models were trained with five seeds, and public-test uncertainty was estimated with 2,000 patient-cluster bootstrap replicates. Under U-Ones, macro AUROC was 0.643 for the primary image, 0.694 for two images, 0.749 for Indication, and 0.780 for ordinary two-image-plus-Indication fusion. SectionGuard-MI achieved AUROC 0.783 and AUPRC 0.260. Relative to ordinary fusion, its paired AUROC difference was 0.0031 (95% CI, -0.0042 to 0.0104; adjusted p=0.374), while its AUPRC difference was 0.0289 (95% CI, 0.0095 to 0.0413; adjusted p=0.004). DeepSets had the highest prospective AUROC point estimate (0.787), and random-swap fusion had the highest prospective AUPRC point estimate (0.265) with better calibration than SectionGuard-MI. Full report text alone reached AUROC 0.979 and AUPRC 0.836; AUROC remained above 0.973 after exact or expanded masking. These results show that prospective Indication is strongly associated with report-derived targets, permutation-aware fusion is competitive, and post-hoc report text creates substantial report-label circularity.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates multimodal fusion strategies for chest radiograph diagnosis using 15,000 studies, finding that prospective Indication text alone achieves strong performance (AUROC 0.749), fusion methods provide marginal gains (SectionGuard-MI AUROC 0.783), and full report text creates circularity (AUROC 0.979), highlighting that post-hoc report leakage is a major concern.
