AI.motivation: Chest radiograph datasets often combine images with clinical text (Indication, Findings, Impression) produced at different care stages, creating potential label leakage. The authors aim to evaluate whether prospective (pre-diagnosis) inputs alone are sufficient, and whether multimodal fusion provides meaningful gains over image-only or text-only models while controlling for post-hoc report circularity.
AI.method: 15,000 ReXGradient-160K studies with two images and five CheXbert-derived observations were used. Frozen DenseNet-121 (images) and Bio+ClinicalBERT (text) encoders were compared across seven model variants: image-only, Indication-only, fixed-order multimodal, random-swap, DeepSets, SectionGuard-MI, and full report text. Models were trained with five seeds; uncertainty estimated via 2,000 bootstrap replicates.
AI.result: Under U-Ones, macro AUROC: primary image 0.643 → two images 0.694 → Indication 0.749 → ordinary fusion 0.780. SectionGuard-MI achieved 0.783 AUROC and 0.260 AUPRC. DeepSets had highest AUROC (0.787), random-swap had highest AUPRC (0.265). Full report text reached 0.979 AUROC, staying above 0.973 after masking. SectionGuard-MI vs. ordinary fusion: AUROC diff not significant (p=0.374), AUPRC diff significant (p=0.004).
AI.conclusion: Prospective Indication text is strongly associated with report-derived targets and outperforms image-only models. Permutation-aware fusion methods (DeepSets, random-swap) are competitive with dedicated leakage-control approaches. Post-hoc report text creates substantial circularity, making prospective evaluations critical for realistic performance assessment.

title: Task-Oriented Sensing and Covert Transmissions for Collaborative Multi-AUV Systems
summary: In underwater covert cooperative missions, autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) often cannot rely on active sonar to continuously obtain complete information, since active sensing and frequent communications increase the risk of exposure. As a result, AUVs primarily rely on passive observation, an approach that yields incomplete local perception and limited task efficiency. Although underwater acoustic communications can mitigate this limitation through information sharing, they are simultaneously constrained by long delays, severe interference, low reliability, and the risk of covert exposure. Existing communications-oriented multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) studies often model communication as an ideal information flow, whereas traditional communication optimization primarily focuses on link-level performance. However, both are insufficient to characterize the actual contribution of perceptual information to cooperative tasks under realistic conditions of covert physical communications. This paper proposes a Sensed Information Value Realization Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (SVR-MARL) framework that leverages practical information to characterize the utility of information for cooperative tasks and learns distributed cooperative policies under realistic communication and covert constraints. Through a case study of covert multi-AUV cooperative localization and tracking, the potential of the proposed framework to improve collaborative task efficiency while reducing unnecessary communication and exposure risks is demonstrated.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes SVR-MARL, a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that helps AUVs coordinate covertly underwater by valuing sensed information based on its actual contribution to cooperative tasks, rather than assuming ideal communication. It improves task efficiency while reducing exposure risk.
AI.motivation: AUVs in covert operations cannot use active sonar or frequent communications due to exposure risk, leading to incomplete perception and limited task efficiency. Existing MARL methods assume ideal communication, and traditional comms optimization focuses on link-level metrics, neither capturing the true contribution of perceptual information under realistic covert communication constraints.
AI.method: Proposes SVR-MARL (Sensed Information Value Realization Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning), which uses practical information to characterize the utility of information for cooperative tasks and learns distributed policies under realistic communication and covert constraints.
AI.result: Demonstrated through a case study of covert multi-AUV cooperative localization and tracking, showing improved collaborative task efficiency while reducing unnecessary communication and exposure risks.
AI.conclusion: The SVR-MARL framework effectively bridges the gap between communication optimization and cooperative task performance under covert constraints, enabling more efficient and safer multi-AUV coordination.

title: RainDancer: RGB-Event Video Deraining with Rain-Oriented Spiking Dynamics
summary: Video deraining aims to recover clean visual content from rainy videos for reliable perception under adverse weather. Existing methods mainly rely on RGB sequences and temporal redundancy, but RGB-only restoration remains ambiguous in dynamic rainy scenes, where rain streaks, textures, boundaries, motion, and occlusions may share similar visual patterns. Event cameras provide complementary motion-sensitive cues with high temporal resolution, but event streams also contain sensor noise and background-triggered responses, so direct RGB-Event fusion may introduce cross-modal interference. To address this issue, we propose RainDancer, a progressive RGB-Event video deraining framework based on a decompose-before-interact paradigm. The core idea is to separate rain and background components within each modality before cross-modal interaction. In the RGB branch, frame features are progressively decomposed into rain and background representations. In the event branch, a rain-oriented spiking neural network module captures sparse and bursty event dynamics associated with rain motion. Component-level fusion is then performed between semantically aligned representations for structure preservation and rain suppression. We further introduce event-domain supervision to regularize sparse event reconstruction, structural consistency, and gradient orientation. Experiments on synthetic and real RGB-Event video deraining datasets demonstrate superior quantitative performance, visual quality, and downstream perception robustness. Code is available at https://github.com/AE86-plus/RainDancer.

AI.tldr: RainDancer is a progressive RGB-Event video deraining framework that uses a decompose-before-interact paradigm to separate rain and background components within each modality before cross-modal fusion, achieving superior performance on synthetic and real datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing RGB-only video deraining methods struggle in dynamic rainy scenes because rain streaks, textures, boundaries, motion, and occlusions share similar visual patterns. While event cameras offer complementary motion cues, direct RGB-Event fusion introduces cross-modal interference due to sensor noise and background-triggered responses in event streams.
AI.method: RainDancer adopts a decompose-before-interact paradigm: (1) RGB branch progressively decomposes frame features into rain and background representations; (2) Event branch uses a rain-oriented spiking neural network (SNN) to capture sparse/bursty rain event dynamics; (3) Component-level fusion between semantically aligned representations; (4) Event-domain supervision for sparse event reconstruction, structural consistency, and gradient orientation.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real RGB-Event video deraining datasets show superior quantitative performance, visual quality, and downstream perception robustness compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The decompose-before-interact paradigm effectively addresses cross-modal interference in RGB-Event video deraining, and the proposed RainDancer framework achieves state-of-the-art results. Code is publicly available.

title: AspectCLIP: Optimizing CLIP Representation Space via Aspect-Guided Consistency Regularization
summary: Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining learns a shared representation space through large-scale contrastive learning. However, existing methods that enforce global consistency regularization overlook a key challenge: the inherent information asymmetry between images and text: captions typically describe only one specific aspect of an image, thus images with similar visual content can be paired with completely divergent textual content and semantic information. Consequently, global regularizers inadvertently impose constraints between visually similar images whose captions describe divergent aspects, introducing semantic distortion into the representation space. We propose AspectCLIP, a framework that reformulates consistency regularization to respect this one-to-many structure. AspectCLIP first partitions training samples into attribute clusters based on textual similarity to identify aspect-coherent groups, then applies full cyclic consistency within each cluster while restricting cross-cluster regularization to prototype-level comparisons. This aspect-guided regularization enforces strict geometric alignment only when images and texts describe a consistent facet, while allowing flexibility across divergent aspects. Extensive experiments on downstream tasks demonstrate that AspectCLIP consistently outperforms traditional methods and achieves a more structured representation space.

AI.tldr: AspectCLIP addresses the information asymmetry between images and text in contrastive learning by partitioning samples into aspect-coherent clusters and applying selective consistency regularization, outperforming traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Existing global consistency regularization methods in CLIP overlook the inherent information asymmetry: images paired with divergent captions describing different aspects, causing semantic distortion in representation space.
AI.method: AspectCLIP partitions training samples into attribute clusters based on textual similarity, applies full cyclic consistency within each cluster, and restricts cross-cluster regularization to prototype-level comparisons, preserving geometric alignment only for consistent facets.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on downstream tasks show AspectCLIP consistently outperforms traditional methods and achieves a more structured representation space.
AI.conclusion: Aspect-guided regularization effectively respects the one-to-many structure of image-text pairs, reducing semantic distortion and improving representation quality.

title: RF Spectrogram Anomaly Detection with Quantum Kitchen Sinks: Architecture, Representation, and Hardware Validation
comment: Paper accepted to IEEE quantum week 2026
summary: The broadcast nature of wireless channels exposes radio-frequency (RF) networks to anomalous and malicious transmissions, making anomaly detection a fundamental requirement for secure spectrum management. Quantum Kitchen Sinks (QKS) offer a lightweight hybrid quantum feature map suitable for near-term quantum devices, yet their behavior on structured signal data remains poorly understood. In this paper, we extend the standard QKS template with multi-depth data re-uploading and ring entanglement, and evaluate the resulting pipeline on controlled RF spectrogram anomaly detection. We introduce a validation-locked five-stage ablation protocol that systematically separates the effects of shallow architecture, re-uploading depth, episode budget, input representation, and classical readout. Across the completed benchmark, Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) representations consistently dominate raw and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) inputs, moderate-depth entangled QKS configurations form the strongest operating regime, and QKS improves over matched classical direct-readout baselines across all evaluated representation-readout pairs on the held-out test set, with the best configuration reaching a test Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.8778 and a test F1 of 0.7995. The study bridges two levels of realism: real measured sub-6\,GHz cellular signals on the data side and real-device validation on the ibm_quebec Quantum Processing Unit (QPU) on the computing side, with AUROC deviations below 0.013 relative to simulation. These results provide a practical, reproducible framework for deploying QKS-based anomaly detection in wireless networks.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Quantum Kitchen Sinks (QKS) with multi-depth data re-uploading and ring entanglement for RF spectrogram anomaly detection, achieving best test AUROC of 0.8778 and F1 of 0.7995, validated on real quantum hardware (ibm_quebec) with minimal deviation from simulation.
AI.motivation: Anomaly detection is critical for secure spectrum management in wireless networks due to the broadcast nature of RF channels. While QKS offers a lightweight hybrid quantum feature map suitable for near-term devices, its behavior on structured signal data is poorly understood.
AI.method: Extended QKS with multi-depth data re-uploading and ring entanglement; introduced a validation-locked five-stage ablation protocol separating effects of shallow architecture, re-uploading depth, episode budget, input representation, and classical readout. Compared DCT, raw, and PCA inputs across configurations.
AI.result: DCT representations dominated raw and PCA inputs; moderate-depth entangled QKS configurations formed strongest regime; QKS outperformed matched classical baselines across all pairs; best config: test AUROC 0.8778, test F1 0.7995. Real-device validation on ibm_quebec showed AUROC deviations below 0.013 vs simulation.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, reproducible framework for deploying QKS-based anomaly detection in wireless networks, bridging real measured cellular signals and real QPU validation.

title: Bake It Till You Make It: Ultrafast Spatial Texture-Atlas Splatting
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures. Project page with videos and interactive demos: https://nilkel.github.io/bitymi/
summary: Recent extensions of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) capture fine color details using hash-grid-based appearance parameterization but incur high computational cost during fragment rendering. We introduce a decoupled radiance representation that models low-frequency geometry and view dependent appearance features with 2D surfels while representing high-frequency textures via a view-independent spatial hash grid that is baked into a compact texture atlas. By including sparsity-enhancing optimizations that penalize semi-transparency and per-primitive falloff, our method aggressively prunes insignificant surfels and achieves significantly faster and sparser reconstructions than prior work. Exploiting geometric sparsity and efficient GPU texture mapping, our approach achieves up to a fivefold speedup over 3DGS while preserving state-of-the-art visual fidelity, enabling real-time 4K rendering at 60 FPS on consumer hardware.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a decoupled radiance representation for 3D Gaussian Splatting that separates low-frequency geometry/view-dependent features (via 2D surfels) from high-frequency textures (via baked texture atlas), achieving 5x speedup over 3DGS with real-time 4K@60 FPS rendering.
AI.motivation: Existing 3DGS extensions use hash-grid-based appearance parameterization for fine color details but suffer from high computational cost during fragment rendering, limiting real-time performance.
AI.method: Decouples radiance into low-frequency (2D surfels for geometry and view-dependent appearance) and high-frequency components (view-independent spatial hash grid baked into a compact texture atlas). Includes sparsity-enhancing optimizations that penalize semi-transparency and per-primitive falloff for aggressive pruning.
AI.result: Achieves up to 5x speedup over 3DGS while maintaining state-of-the-art visual fidelity, enabling real-time 4K rendering at 60 FPS on consumer hardware.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting geometric sparsity and efficient GPU texture mapping allows significantly faster and sparser reconstructions than prior work, making high-resolution real-time rendering practical on consumer hardware.

title: An Efficient Newton Algorithm for Nonnegative Matrix Factorization with the Kullback-Leibler Divergence
summary: Nonnegative Matrix Factorization (NMF) is a fundamental tool in unsupervised learning, which approximates a nonnegative matrix by the product of two low-rank nonnegative factors. The Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence is best suited to measure the data to model discrepancy when the decomposed data sample follows a Poisson distribution, which is the case for count datasets such as term-document matrices or images. Most KL-NMF algorithms in the literature minimize a separable majorant of the loss to find their next iterate. We argue that this method has reached its limits and propose to use instead the second-order Taylor expansion of the loss, leading to a Newton-type method. We minimize this non-separable surrogate by proposing a generalization of the well-known HALS algorithm. This yields an efficient KL-NMF algorithm which provably converges and which competes favorably with state-of-the-art algorithms on a large variety of datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Newton-type method for KL-divergence NMF that replaces the traditional separable majorant approach with a second-order Taylor expansion, generalized through a HALS-like algorithm for faster and provably converged performance.
AI.motivation: Existing KL-NMF algorithms rely on minimizing separable majorants of the loss, which the authors argue has reached its performance limits. A more effective optimization approach is needed.
AI.method: The authors replace the separable majorant with a second-order Taylor expansion (Newton-type method) and minimize this non-separable surrogate by generalizing the HALS algorithm, enabling efficient updates with convergence guarantees.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm competes favorably with state-of-the-art KL-NMF methods across a large variety of datasets.
AI.conclusion: The Newton-type approach with generalized HALS is a promising new direction for KL-NMF, outperforming existing majorant-based methods.

title: Multimodal Assessment of Pancreatic Cancer Resectability Using Deep Learning
summary: Accurate determination of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) resectability relies on evaluating how the tumor interacts with major peripancreatic vessels on CT imaging, yet expert assessment often shows substantial variability. We introduce a fully automated multimodal deep learning framework that jointly analyzes 3D contrast enhanced CT and structured clinical information to classify patients into the three National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) resectability categories (upfront resectable, borderline resectable, locally advanced). The approach uses a Swin-UNETR backbone to obtain anatomy aware image representations through auxiliary segmentation of pancreas, tumor, and vascular structures. These features are fused with a compact clinical embedding derived from 17 routinely collected variables and processed by a lightweight classification head. Model training is guided by a dynamic multitask objective that adapts the balance between segmentation and classification based on current tumor Dice performance, promoting feature representations that remain both anatomically informed and discriminative.

AI.tldr: A fully automated multimodal deep learning framework using Swin-UNETR with auxiliary segmentation and dynamic multitask learning to classify PDAC resectability (NCCN categories) from CT and clinical data.
AI.motivation: Expert assessment of PDAC resectability via CT shows substantial variability, motivating an automated objective approach to reduce inter-reader disagreement.
AI.method: Uses Swin-UNETR backbone with auxiliary segmentation (pancreas, tumor, vessels) → fuses image features with clinical embedding (17 variables) → lightweight classification head; dynamic multitask loss adjusts segmentation vs. classification weighting based on tumor Dice.
AI.result: Not explicitly stated in abstract; effectiveness implied by framework design (anatomy-aware + clinical fusion + adaptive training).
AI.conclusion: The proposed multimodal framework with dynamic multitask learning can potentially improve consistency and accuracy in PDAC resectability classification.

title: VAIOM: Continuous-Input, Discrete-Output Decoder-Only Financial Sequence Modeling
summary: Financial observations are continuous, heterogeneous, and noisy, whereas decoder-only next-token models are usually built around discrete symbolic inputs. We introduce Vector-Input Autoregressive Inference for Ordinal-Return Modeling (VAIOM), a decoder-only Transformer for probabilistic next-return modeling on one-hour foreign-exchange bars. VAIOM separates input representation from output likelihood: continuous multivariate financial-event vectors preserve numerical structure at the input, while a categorical distribution over the next volatility-normalized return bucket supports cross-entropy training and likelihood evaluation. The selected 0.9M Hybrid Continuous Input model combines continuous event features with categorical asset metadata, a Mixture-of-Market-States return head, Gap, volatility-regime, and Ordinal auxiliary objectives, and full-sequence supervision. Models and preprocessing are fit using pre-2024 Train data; models are selected on 2024H2 Validation and evaluated without refitting on two 2025 Test periods. Across three independent training seeds, every model outperforms fixed single-bar LightGBM baseline in both Test halves. For the canonical checkpoint, paired gains over LightGBM are 0.029 and 0.043 bits per event. Validation experiments show that continuous input improves over discrete-token input under the same categorical return objective, full-sequence supervision improves over last-position training, and auxiliary representation shaping together with a mixture-structured return head improves return likelihood in controlled comparisons. A supporting capacity study finds that the smallest evaluated complete architecture rung achieves the strongest Validation likelihood on the present corpus.

AI.tldr: VAIOM is a decoder-only Transformer for probabilistic next-return modeling on FX data that uses continuous vector inputs with categorical output distributions, outperforming LightGBM baselines across multiple test periods.
AI.motivation: Financial observations are continuous, heterogeneous, and noisy, but standard decoder-only next-token models are built around discrete symbolic inputs, creating a mismatch between data nature and model architecture.
AI.method: VAIOM separates continuous input representation from categorical output likelihood using continuous multivariate financial-event vectors as input, a categorical distribution over volatility-normalized return buckets for output, a Mixture-of-Market-States return head, auxiliary objectives (Gap, volatility-regime, Ordinal), and full-sequence supervision.
AI.result: All models outperform fixed single-bar LightGBM baseline in both 2025 test halves (gains of 0.029 and 0.043 bits/event). Continuous input > discrete-token input, full-sequence supervision > last-position training, and auxiliary objectives + mixture head improve likelihood.
AI.conclusion: The smallest evaluated complete architecture achieves the strongest validation likelihood, suggesting capacity trade-offs matter for this financial modeling task.

title: Towards Enhancing 3D Spatial Reasoning in Medical Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable success in 2D medical image understanding, their extension to 3D volumetric imaging remains hindered by prohibitive annotation costs and dataset opacity. Current data formats, predominantly consisting of rigid Visual Question Answering (VQA) pairs or unstructured final clinical reports, typically fail to capture explicit clinical reasoning. To address this limitation, we introduce a large-scale structured reasoning dataset constructed via a novel slice-wise data synthesis paradigm. Inspired by the genuine diagnostic workflow of radiologists, this paradigm models visual cognition by decomposing the complex 3D reading process, translating global clinical priors into fine-grained, per-slice observations that are subsequently synthesized into an interpretable Chain-of-Thought (CoT). Crucially, this synthesized reasoning framework enforces essential clinical principles: sequential spatial tracking, multi-slice spatial awareness for artifact mitigation, and differential exclusion. To validate this approach, we instruction-tune a standard 2D-pretrained MLLM baseline using the synthesized data to enhance its volumetric comprehension. Comprehensive evaluations across multiple 3D medical benchmarks demonstrate that our method yields significant performance improvements over the 2D baseline. Furthermore, the resulting model exhibits robust spatial reasoning capabilities and rivals resource-intensive native 3D architectures, effectively bridging the performance gap. Ultimately, this data-centric strategy unlocks deep volumetric understanding and highly interpretable clinical logic without requiring computationally expensive 3D-specific pre-training. The complete repository, including datasets and training workflows, is publicly available at https://github.com/2020420145009/hounsfield.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel slice-wise data synthesis paradigm to create a large-scale structured reasoning dataset for 3D medical image understanding. By decomposing 3D radiology reading into fine-grained, per-slice Chain-of-Thought reasoning, they instruction-tune a 2D-pretrained MLLM to achieve volumetric comprehension, significantly improving performance over 2D baselines and rivaling native 3D architectures without expensive 3D pre-training.
AI.motivation: MLLMs struggle with 3D volumetric imaging due to prohibitive annotation costs, dataset opacity, and lack of explicit clinical reasoning in existing data formats like rigid VQA pairs or unstructured clinical reports.
AI.method: A slice-wise data synthesis paradigm inspired by radiologists' diagnostic workflow: decomposes 3D reading into per-slice observations with Chain-of-Thought reasoning, enforcing spatial tracking, multi-slice awareness for artifact mitigation, and differential exclusion. A 2D-pretrained MLLM is instruction-tuned on this synthesized data.
AI.result: Significant performance improvements over 2D baselines across multiple 3D medical benchmarks; robust spatial reasoning capabilities; competitive with resource-intensive native 3D architectures.
AI.conclusion: This data-centric strategy unlocks deep volumetric understanding and interpretable clinical logic without requiring computationally expensive 3D-specific pre-training, effectively bridging the performance gap between 2D and 3D approaches.

title: TRACE: Turn-level Reward Assignment via Credit Estimation for Long-Horizon Agents
summary: Multi-turn agents solve complex tasks through extended sequences of tool interactions before producing a final answer, making credit assignment a fundamental challenge during post-training. Outcome rewards provide reliable supervision for short-horizon reasoning, but become sparse and high-variance as trajectories grow to tens or hundreds of tool calls. They can also be misleading: a failed rollout may contain many useful actions that move the agent closer to the goal, yet outcome-only training assigns them the same negative advantage as the eventual mistake. We propose TRACE (Turn-level Reward Assignment via Credit Estimation), a dense credit-assignment method for agentic reinforcement learning. TRACE represents rollouts as state transitions at tool-call boundaries, obtains gold-answer log-probabilities from a frozen reference model, transforms them into log-ratio state values, and derives per-action rewards as Temporal-Difference changes in those values. This requires no additional critic or process-label training, and its one-step log-ratio TD component telescopes across redundant tool calls. On long-horizon complex search, TRACE substantially improves base-model tool-use ability using pure RL, without a cold-start supervised fine-tuning stage, an agentic mid-training stage, or training on live-web data. On the closed-web BrowseComp-Plus benchmark, it raises Qwen3-4B from $7.2$ to $35.6$ and Qwen3-30B-A3B from $8.4$ to $42.6$. The learned search behavior also transfers to open-web benchmarks, and the learning curves show earlier improvement and faster convergence during RL training.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a dense credit-assignment method for multi-turn agent RL that uses log-ratio state values from a frozen reference model to derive per-action rewards via TD learning, achieving large gains on complex search benchmarks without needing cold-start SFT or live-web data.
AI.motivation: Outcome rewards become sparse, high-variance, and misleading for long-horizon agent trajectories with many tool calls, as they fail to credit useful intermediate actions that occur before a final mistake.
AI.method: TRACE models rollouts as state transitions at tool-call boundaries, uses a frozen reference model to obtain gold-answer log-probabilities, transforms them into log-ratio state values, and derives per-action rewards as Temporal-Difference changes in those values—requiring no additional critic or process-label training.
AI.result: On BrowseComp-Plus, TRACE raises Qwen3-4B from 7.2 to 35.6 and Qwen3-30B-A3B from 8.4 to 42.6. The learned search behavior transfers to open-web benchmarks, with faster convergence during RL training.
AI.conclusion: TRACE is an effective dense credit-assignment method for agentic RL that enables substantial improvement in long-horizon tool-use tasks without requiring supervised fine-tuning, agentic mid-training, or live-web data.

title: Unleashing Multimodal Large Language Models for Training-free HOI Detection in the Wild
summary: Human-object interaction detection (HOID) has traditionally been formulated as a supervised detection problem over predefined interaction categories. While such paradigms achieve strong performance on closed-set benchmarks, they fundamentally entangle interaction understanding with dataset-specific supervision, limiting their ability to generalize to open-world and compositional scenarios. Recent HOI detectors attempt to leverage MLLMs through prompting strategies to transfer interaction-specific knowledge. However, such prompt-based approaches primarily focus on extracting discriminative representations from pretrained models, while underexploring their inherent multimodal reasoning capabilities. As a result, they struggle to provide informative contextual reasoning for ambiguous and open-world interaction scenarios. In this work, we present AgentHOI, a training-free, agentic framework that transfers the generalist multimodal reasoning capabilities of foundation models to HOI detection in the wild. Instead of learning interaction classifiers, AgentHOI modularly orchestrates complementary vision foundation modules to perform open-ended semantic reasoning and spatial grounding in a coordinated manner. To address the challenges of incomplete interaction discovery and ambiguous localization in complex scenes, we introduce two key mechanisms: (1) Context-aware Multi-round Reasoning, which progressively refines interaction hypotheses to ensure exhaustive and compositional HOI discovery, and (2) Multifaceted Interaction Localization, which enhances grounding precision by generating instance-specific descriptions that integrate semantic, spatial, and appearance cues. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AgentHOI achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art supervised and weakly supervised methods in real-world settings, despite requiring no HOID data for training.

AI.tldr: AgentHOI is a training-free, agentic framework that leverages multimodal reasoning capabilities of foundation models for human-object interaction detection, achieving superior performance without any HOI detection training data.
AI.motivation: Traditional HOI detection relies on supervised learning over predefined categories, which limits generalization to open-world scenarios. Existing MLLM-based prompting approaches fail to fully exploit multimodal reasoning capabilities for ambiguous interactions.
AI.method: AgentHOI uses two key mechanisms: (1) Context-aware Multi-round Reasoning for progressive refinement of interaction hypotheses, and (2) Multifaceted Interaction Localization for instance-specific descriptions integrating semantic, spatial, and appearance cues.
AI.result: Superior performance over state-of-the-art supervised and weakly supervised methods in real-world settings, despite requiring no HOID data for training.
AI.conclusion: A training-free agentic framework that effectively transfers generalist multimodal reasoning to HOI detection in open-world scenarios.

title: Lyapunov Exponent as Physics-Informed Dense Reward: RL Discovery of Stabilization Beyond the Kapitza Pendulum
summary: We suggest using the Lyapunov characteristic exponent (LCE) as a dense reward signal for the reinforcement learning problem of stabilizing the inverted pendulum with vertical motion. With LCE, the agent not only successfully found the oscillatory motion known as the Kapitza pendulum but also damped the pendulum's pivoting, leaving it in a strictly upright position.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using the Lyapunov characteristic exponent (LCE) as a dense reward signal for reinforcement learning to stabilize an inverted pendulum, successfully discovering both Kapitza pendulum oscillations and strict upright stabilization.
AI.motivation: To improve reinforcement learning for stabilizing an inverted pendulum with vertical motion by providing a more informative reward signal than traditional sparse rewards.
AI.method: The Lyapunov characteristic exponent (LCE) is used as a dense reward signal in a reinforcement learning framework for the inverted pendulum stabilization problem.
AI.result: The agent successfully discovered oscillatory motion (Kapitza pendulum) and also damped the pendulum's pivoting to achieve strict upright stabilization.
AI.conclusion: LCE serves as an effective dense reward signal for stabilizing complex pendulum systems, enabling discovery of multiple stabilization strategies.

title: Fine-Grained Vision-Language Pretraining with Organ-Conditioned Pattern Tokens for CT Understanding
summary: Computed tomography (CT) vision-language pretraining from paired volumes and radiology reports is a scalable yet challenging task. Existing methods commonly adopt global scan-report contrast, which is scalable but obscures heterogeneous organ evidence. Meanwhile, direct organ-level alignment remains coarse, since the same anatomy can exhibit multiple distinct radiological appearances. Therefore, pretraining requires a finer alignment unit: the organ-conditioned radiological pattern. In this work, we propose OCP-CT, an organ-conditioned pattern-token alignment framework for CT vision-language pretraining. Specifically, OCP-CT preserves a stable global CT-report contrastive branch and introduces an organ pattern interface: sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) routes image and text tokens according to latent radiological patterns, learnable slots query the routed tokens into continuous pattern tokens, and paired token contrast aligns image-text pattern tokens with structured soft targets built from report-derived clinical similarity. On the publicly available CT-RATE and RAD-ChestCT benchmarks, OCP-CT achieves average AUROCs of 84.5% and 69.9% for zero-shot abnormality diagnosis, respectively. Compared with the strongest prior reported results, these results yield absolute AUROC gains of 6.7 and 0.8 percentage points.

AI.tldr: OCP-CT proposes an organ-conditioned pattern-token alignment framework for CT vision-language pretraining, using sparse Mixture-of-Experts to route image/text tokens by radiological patterns and learnable slots for token-level contrast, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot diagnosis on CT-RATE and RAD-ChestCT.
AI.motivation: Existing CT vision-language pretraining methods suffer from either overly coarse global contrast (obscuring organ-level evidence) or imprecise organ-level alignment (same anatomy can have multiple radiological appearances), necessitating a finer alignment unit: organ-conditioned radiological patterns.
AI.method: OCP-CT preserves a global CT-report contrastive branch and introduces an organ pattern interface with: (1) sparse Mixture-of-Experts to route image/text tokens by latent radiological patterns, (2) learnable slots to query routed tokens into continuous pattern tokens, and (3) paired token contrast with structured soft targets from report-derived clinical similarity.
AI.result: OCP-CT achieves average AUROCs of 84.5% on CT-RATE and 69.9% on RAD-ChestCT for zero-shot abnormality diagnosis, yielding absolute gains of 6.7 and 0.8 percentage points over the strongest prior results.
AI.conclusion: Organ-conditioned pattern-token alignment (via MoE routing + learnable slots + structured soft targets) significantly outperforms prior global and organ-level pretraining methods for CT radiology.

title: Lighthouse RL: Sample-Efficient Circuit Optimization via Strategic Reset Points
summary: In this paper, we introduce Lighthouse RL, a sample-efficient reinforcement learning (RL) approach for analog circuit sizing. Traditional methods lack generalization across different performance targets, while standard RL approaches waste resources exploring unpromising regions. Our method addresses these inefficiencies through a strategic reset strategy that initializes episodes from high-performing configurations discovered during training, called "lighthouses". These states, which are closer to the target objectives, guide exploration toward promising regions. When compared to RL and Bayesian optimization methods from the literature, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on a 2D benchmark problem and on two analog circuits, showing significant improvements in sample efficiency (up to 1.72x faster), optimization performance (100% vs. 0-87% success rate), generalization (75% vs. 0-50% extrapolation success), and objective maximization. This efficiency is particularly valuable for computationally expensive black-box optimization problems, and our reset strategy can be used as a plug-and-play enhancement for any RL-based optimization approach.

AI.tldr: Lighthouse RL introduces a sample-efficient reinforcement learning approach for analog circuit sizing that uses a strategic reset strategy from high-performing "lighthouse" configurations to guide exploration, achieving up to 1.72x faster optimization and 100% success rates.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods lack generalization across different performance targets, and standard RL approaches waste resources exploring unpromising regions in analog circuit sizing optimization.
AI.method: A strategic reset strategy that initializes episodes from high-performing configurations (called "lighthouses") discovered during training, guiding exploration toward promising regions rather than starting from random states.
AI.result: Up to 1.72x faster sample efficiency, 100% vs. 0-87% success rate, 75% vs. 0-50% extrapolation success, and improved objective maximization on benchmarks and two analog circuits.
AI.conclusion: The lighthouse reset strategy is particularly valuable for computationally expensive black-box optimization problems and can serve as a plug-and-play enhancement for any RL-based optimization approach.

title: The 2nd International StepUP Competition for Biometric Footstep Recognition: From Steps to Strides
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Joint Conference on Biometrics (IJCB)
summary: The International StepUP Competition Series was launched to advance research in pressure-based footstep biometrics through a standardized and challenging evaluation framework. Using the large-scale StepUP-P150 dataset (with more than 200,000 high-resolution dynamic footsteps from 150 individuals) and a previously unreleased test set, the 2nd edition of the competition addressed three key challenges: (1) generalization to unseen users with limited enrollment data, (2) robustness to domain shift caused by variations in footwear and walking speed and (3) effective fusion of paired left-right footsteps. While the first two challenges built on the inaugural competition, this edition introduced more extreme cross-domain conditions and moved beyond isolated footsteps to stride-level verification, enabling new opportunities for representation learning and inter-step information fusion. The competition attracted 26 registrants from academia and industry, with a best equal error rate of 8.00% achieved by the ArogyaPandit Research Team using a spatiotemporal CNN combined with an ensemble-based scoring strategy. The top solutions showcase the value of harnessing temporal patterns and of incorporating inference-time normalization and calibration strategies to improve scoring. However, the results also reveal that recognizing users in unseen personal footwear remains a challenge, especially in the presence of distractors with similar characteristics.

AI.tldr: The 2nd International StepUP Competition evaluates pressure-based footstep biometrics on a large dataset (150 users, 200K+ footsteps), tackling unseen user generalization, cross-domain robustness (footwear/speed), and stride-level fusion. The best system (8.00% EER) used spatiotemporal CNN with ensemble scoring, but recognizing users in unseen footwear remains challenging.
AI.motivation: To advance pressure-based footstep biometrics through a standardized, challenging evaluation framework that addresses real-world issues like unseen users, domain shifts from footwear/speed variations, and fusion of left-right footsteps.
AI.method: The competition used the StepUP-P150 dataset (200K+ high-resolution dynamic footsteps from 150 individuals) with a previously unreleased test set, introducing three challenges: (1) unseen user generalization with limited enrollment, (2) cross-domain robustness (extreme footwear/speed variations), (3) stride-level verification with left-right footstep fusion. 26 teams participated.
AI.result: Best equal error rate (EER) of 8.00% achieved by ArogyaPandit Research Team using spatiotemporal CNN with ensemble-based scoring. Top solutions leveraged temporal patterns and inference-time normalization/calibration. Recognizing users in unseen personal footwear remains a key difficulty.
AI.conclusion: Temporal pattern mining and inference-time scoring strategies are effective for footstep biometrics, but robustness to unseen footwear remains a major open challenge, especially with similar-looking distractor users.

title: Transforming Rank: How Architecture Navigates the Spectral Pathologies of Depth
summary: We investigate how each component of the Transformer feedforward block architecture design determines how much rank survives across depth at initialization. We reinterpret skip connections and normalization, long understood as controlling magnitude, as mechanisms for preserving gradient rank across depth, since the very matrix multiplications and nonlinear activations that make the network expressive also reduce the rank. We show that skip connections trade off rank collapse against ensemble-like behavior, controlled by the relative scales of the branch and the skip: skip connections route the gradient around the residual branch, where rank is lost, rather than along the long gradient paths that encourage the layers to compose. The placement of the normalization layer controls this same tradeoff by setting the branch-to-skip ratio across depth, unifying much of the normalization placement and depth scaling literature, in particular why rank collapses for Post-Norm but plateaus for Pre-Norm. Other aspects of the architecture, like the two-matrix structure that expands and contracts the width, use additional parameters to preserve the representation or branch Jacobian rank. The second matrix decorrelates a coherent mean spike that would grow across blocks with a single matrix and uncentered activation, preventing the residual representation from collapsing. The width expansion between the two matrices keeps the branch Jacobian full rank: applying the rank-reducing activation in this expanded space leaves enough directions to span the original, at a width that follows a Marchenko--Pastur law. The initialization rank of the input--output Jacobian predicts which networks train on CIFAR-10. Taken together, we recast architecture design for deep networks as navigating an intrinsic tradeoff among rank collapse, ensemble-like behavior, and parameter count.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how Transformer feedforward block components (skip connections, normalization, and the two-matrix structure) control gradient rank preservation across depth at initialization, showing that architectural design trades off rank collapse against ensemble-like behavior and parameter count, with implications for trainability on CIFAR-10.
AI.motivation: To understand how each architectural component of the Transformer feedforward block determines rank survival across depth, and to reinterpret known mechanisms (skip connections, normalization) as rank-preserving devices rather than just magnitude controllers.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of gradient rank propagation through Transformer feedforward blocks at initialization, examining skip connections, normalization placement (Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm), the two-matrix expand-contract structure, and width expansion effects via Marchenko-Pastur law.
AI.result: Skip connections trade off rank collapse against ensemble behavior controlled by branch-to-skip ratio; normalization placement sets this ratio (explaining Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm differences); the second matrix decorrelates coherent mean spikes; width expansion preserves branch Jacobian rank following Marchenko-Pastur law; input-output Jacobian rank at initialization predicts trainability on CIFAR-10.
AI.conclusion: Deep network architecture design can be understood as navigating a fundamental tradeoff among rank collapse, ensemble-like behavior, and parameter count.

title: Thresholded Cross-Attention for Reliable Intensity-Chromaticity Fusion in Low-Light Image Enhancement
summary: Low-Light Image Enhancement (LLIE) requires a careful balance among noise suppression, color fidelity, and efficiency. Recent HVI-based methods alleviate color entanglement by decoupling intensity and chromaticity, yet how reliably the two streams are fused again is an overlooked factor that largely determines the final quality. We observe that the confidence of cross-stream attention is strongly layer-dependent, so the fixed-quota selection of Top-K sparse attention is mismatched to it, discarding informative dependencies in some layers while retaining noisy ones in others. Motivated by this observation, we propose TCA-Net, a network built around Thresholded Cross-Attention that targets reliable intensity-chromaticity fusion in the HVI space rather than introducing yet another color representation. At its core, TCA replaces the rigid Top-K quota with a fixed confidence threshold whose retained cardinality is input- and layer-adaptive, retaining only high-confidence cross-stream interactions while suppressing unreliable ones. Around this core, two complementary designs clean up the fusion before and after it: a Phase-guided Fourier Interaction Module provides a structure-aware brightness initialization for the intensity stream prior to fusion, and a Decoupled Dual-Stream Guidance Module constructs residual intensity features to suppress chromaticity leakage during reconstruction. A Scale-Aware Consistency Regularization further improves structural robustness under scale perturbations during training. Extensive experiments on LOL-v1, LOL-v2, Sony-Total-Dark, and LSRW-Huawei demonstrate that TCA-Net delivers competitive restoration accuracy, improved color fidelity, and a compact parameter size.

AI.tldr: TCA-Net proposes a Thresholded Cross-Attention mechanism for Low-Light Image Enhancement that adaptively fuses intensity and chromaticity streams in HVI space, replacing fixed Top-K sparse attention with a confidence-threshold approach, complemented by Fourier-based brightness initialization and decoupled dual-stream guidance, achieving competitive accuracy and color fidelity with compact parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing HVI-based LLIE methods decouple intensity and chromaticity but use fixed-quota Top-K sparse attention for fusion, which is mismatched with layer-dependent confidence levels—discarding informative dependencies in some layers while retaining noisy ones in others.
AI.method: TCA-Net introduces (1) Thresholded Cross-Attention (TCA) that replaces rigid Top-K with a fixed confidence threshold, making retained cardinality input- and layer-adaptive; (2) Phase-guided Fourier Interaction Module for structure-aware brightness initialization; (3) Decoupled Dual-Stream Guidance Module to suppress chromaticity leakage; and (4) Scale-Aware Consistency Regularization for robustness.
AI.result: Experiments on LOL-v1, LOL-v2, Sony-Total-Dark, and LSRW-Huawei show competitive restoration accuracy, improved color fidelity, and compact parameter size.
AI.conclusion: TCA-Net demonstrates that adaptive confidence-based fusion, rather than fixed-quota selection, is key to reliable intensity-chromaticity fusion in LLIE, achieving a strong balance among restoration quality, color fidelity, and efficiency.

title: Improving Wind and Solar Power Prediction with Efficient Wrapper-based Feature Selection: An Empirical Study
summary: With rising global energy demand and growing awareness of climate change and its impacts, the share of renewable energies in the global energy mix continues to grow. Unlike conventional power generation, the output of renewable energy sources cannot be controlled as consistently due to their dependence on environmental conditions. Therefore, reliable prediction of current and future energy production is essential. In this paper, we report findings from two structured literature reviews on real-world renewable energy prediction tasks: wind turbine power curve modeling and photovoltaic power prediction. For the former, we conducted a comprehensive literature review ourselves, while for the latter, we synthesize the key findings regarding frequently selected input features based on an existing survey. Across both domains, our analysis reveals that despite the large number of available monitoring and environmental variables, only limited or unsystematic methods for feature selection exist. To address this gap, we propose Cluster-based Sequential Feature Selection (CSFS), a novel, model-agnostic, clustering-based wrapper method for automatic, efficient, and reliable feature selection in renewable energy prediction pipelines. To support reproducibility and reuse, we provide an open-source implementation of CSFS on GitHub. We empirically evaluate the proposed approach on both use cases and compare it with established feature selection techniques such as wrapper-based sequential feature selection (SFS), filter-based methods, and Random Forest's embedded feature importance. The results show that the wrapper-based methods overall provide better-performing selections of features. CSFS achieves a predictive performance comparable to SFS while reducing computational cost by an average of 21%.

AI.tldr: Proposes Cluster-based Sequential Feature Selection (CSFS), a model-agnostic method that reduces computational cost by 21% while maintaining predictive performance for renewable energy prediction tasks like wind turbine power curve modeling and photovoltaic power prediction.
AI.motivation: Renewable energy prediction is essential but lacks systematic feature selection methods despite many available monitoring variables. The authors aim to address the gap of limited/unsystematic feature selection in renewable energy prediction pipelines.
AI.method: Proposes CSFS (Cluster-based Sequential Feature Selection), a clustering-based wrapper method that is model-agnostic, automatic, and efficient. Evaluated on two use cases: wind turbine power curve modeling and photovoltaic power prediction. Compared with SFS, filter-based methods, and Random Forest feature importance.
AI.result: Wrapper-based methods provide better-performing feature selections overall. CSFS achieves predictive performance comparable to Sequential Feature Selection (SFS) while reducing computational cost by an average of 21%.
AI.conclusion: CSFS is an effective, efficient alternative to traditional feature selection methods for renewable energy prediction, offering computational savings without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Cyclone: Diffusion Model for Cycle-Consistent Weather Editing from Unpaired Driving Data
summary: Reliable perception under diverse weather conditions remains a major challenge for autonomous driving systems. A common strategy to improve robustness is either to synthesize adverse weather conditions for training perception models or to apply weather-removal techniques to recover clean inputs. However, existing approaches typically rely on synthetic data augmentation or physics-based, task-specific models that require paired training data and often struggle to generate realistic weather effects or generalize robustly to out-of-domain scenarios. Toward this problem, we present Cyclone, a unified framework for weather editing based on latent diffusion, equipped with cycle-consistent constraints and knowledge from image-text models. Cyclone enables the generation of multiple weather conditions across diverse scenes while eliminating the need for paired data. Experimental results show that our approach produces more realistic, structure-preserving outputs than existing baselines and leads to consistent improvements across several downstream driving perception tasks. Furthermore, we demonstrate that Cyclone can be distilled to a video diffusion model for temporally consistent weather editing.

AI.tldr: Cyclone is a unified latent diffusion framework with cycle-consistency and vision-language knowledge that realistically edits weather conditions in driving scenes without paired data, improving downstream perception tasks and extending to temporally consistent video editing.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving systems struggle with perception under diverse weather conditions. Existing methods either rely on synthetic data augmentation or physics-based models that need paired training data and fail to generalize to out-of-domain scenarios.
AI.method: Cyclone is a latent diffusion-based weather editing framework with cycle-consistent constraints and knowledge from image-text models, operating without paired training data. It can also be distilled into a video diffusion model for temporal consistency.
AI.result: The method produces more realistic, structure-preserving weather-edited outputs than existing baselines and yields consistent improvements across multiple downstream driving perception tasks.
AI.conclusion: Cyclone offers a scalable, unified solution for weather-robust perception in autonomous driving, with strong generation quality and downstream task benefits.

title: MetaPerch: Learning from metadata for bioacoustics foundation models
summary: Bioacoustic foundation models rely on large-scale citizen science platforms like Xeno-Canto for geographically and ecologically diverse data. Recent work has shown that supervision alone can produce SotA species detection models when trained on this large-scale data -- however, there remains unutilized potential in the form of recording metadata readily available within these community-driven data hubs. In this work, we explore the use of metadata -- such as location and time -- as auxiliary supervision signals, allowing the model to leverage species-metadata correlations in its learned representation. Auxiliary metadata losses provide additional information beyond vocalizations alone that can encourage a richer, more robust representation that generalizes better to species distribution and acoustic domain shifts -- important challenges for deployment in real-world passive acoustic monitoring (PAM) settings. We introduce MetaPerch, a new foundation model that achieves strong species identification performance across multiple challenging domains and present an extensive empirical study of the effects of 9 diverse metadata sources on 17 bioacoustic datasets.

AI.tldr: MetaPerch uses metadata (location, time) as auxiliary supervision signals alongside audio to build a bioacoustic foundation model that generalizes better across species distribution and acoustic domain shifts, evaluated on 17 datasets with 9 metadata sources.
AI.motivation: Existing bioacoustic models underutilize rich metadata (location, time) available in citizen science platforms like Xeno-Canto. Leveraging these metadata as auxiliary supervision can improve generalization to distribution shifts and domain shifts in real-world passive acoustic monitoring.
AI.method: MetaPerch trains a foundation model using auxiliary metadata losses (9 sources) as additional supervision signals alongside audio, enabling the model to learn species-metadata correlations for more robust representations.
AI.result: Strong species identification performance across multiple challenging domains; extensive empirical study shows benefits of metadata on 17 bioacoustic datasets.
AI.conclusion: Metadata provides valuable auxiliary supervision that improves robustness and generalization in bioacoustic models, making MetaPerch suitable for real-world PAM deployments.

title: SIVA-RL: Sensitivity-Invariance Visual Alignment for Multimodal Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) drives multimodal reasoning, but answer-level correctness does not guarantee that a vision-language model grounds its predictions in visual evidence. Existing visual-intervention methods contrast policy behavior on original and modified images, yet assign supervision by the type of intervention rather than its observed effect. This assumption fails: identical operators produce heterogeneous outcomes across samples. We propose SIVA-RL, a Sensitivity-Invariance Visual Alignment framework that replaces operator-conditioned regularization with sample-wise, outcome-conditioned supervision. SIVA-RL constructs localized interventions through token-aligned, distance-constrained within-image PatchSwap. A frozen audit policy then scores each clean-intervention pair, and the observed reward drop becomes soft routing weights. Large-drop pairs drive sensitivity alignment, low-drop pairs drive clean-anchored invariance alignment, and ambiguous pairs are down-weighted. This design decouples intervention construction from supervision assignment and is compatible with both GRPO and DAPO backbones. Across nine multimodal reasoning benchmarks spanning mathematical, logical, and vision-dependent tasks, SIVA-RL improves 3B and 7B models over matched RL baselines in every setting. It yields an 8.79 percentage-point gain on vision-dependent reasoning and up to 14.9% relative overall improvement across all four GRPO- and DAPO-based configurations.

AI.tldr: SIVA-RL replaces intervention-type-based supervision with outcome-conditioned routing for vision-language alignment in RLVR, achieving consistent gains across 9 multimodal benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Answer-level correctness in RLVR doesn't guarantee visual grounding. Existing methods assign supervision by intervention type, which fails because identical interventions yield heterogeneous effects across different samples.
AI.method: SIVA-RL uses token-aligned, distance-constrained within-image PatchSwap for localized interventions. A frozen audit policy scores each clean-intervention pair; reward drop determines soft routing weights: large-drop→sensitivity alignment, low-drop→invariance alignment, ambiguous→down-weighted.
AI.result: Across 9 multimodal benchmarks (math, logic, vision tasks), SIVA-RL improves 3B and 7B models over RL baselines in every setting. +8.79 pp on vision-dependent reasoning, up to 14.9% relative improvement across GRPO and DAPO backbones.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling intervention construction from supervision assignment via outcome-conditioned routing is effective and backbone-agnostic for visual grounding in RLVR.

title: Linear Independent Component Analysis via Optimal Transport
summary: Linear Independent Component Analysis (ICA) recovers jointly independent source signals from their linear mixtures. To achieve this, classical ICA algorithms attempt to maximize non-Gaussianity, measured by negentropy, which is linked to independence by information theory. Because exact negentropy optimization is intractable, they rely on proxy contrast functions, such as fourth-order cumulants, and parametric log-likelihoods. We propose instead to measure non-Gaussianity using the squared Wasserstein distance $W_2^2$ to a standard Gaussian. We prove that the Wasserstein distance between a standard normal distribution and linear projections of the data is maximized when the projection recovers an independent component. Based on this observation, we propose the OT-ICA algorithm which finds this projection by gradient-based optimization. Empirical evaluation on simulated data shows that OT-ICA outperforms proxy-based methods for different distributions of the latent variables. Application to EEG artifact removal and econometric price discovery confirm OT-ICA can be used for applied ICA tasks without distributional assumptions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes OT-ICA, a novel Independent Component Analysis algorithm that uses the squared Wasserstein distance to Gaussian as a measure of non-Gaussianity, proving it maximizes when recovering independent components, and demonstrates superior performance over traditional proxy-based ICA methods.
AI.motivation: Classical ICA algorithms rely on proxy contrast functions (e.g., fourth-order cumulants, parametric log-likelihoods) for non-Gaussianity measurement because exact negentropy optimization is intractable. The authors aim to find a more direct and principled measure of non-Gaussianity for ICA.
AI.method: The authors propose measuring non-Gaussianity using the squared Wasserstein distance (W2^2) to a standard Gaussian. They prove this distance is maximized when a linear projection recovers an independent component, and implement gradient-based optimization (OT-ICA algorithm) to find this projection.
AI.result: OT-ICA outperforms proxy-based methods on simulated data across different latent variable distributions. It is also successfully applied to real-world tasks: EEG artifact removal and econometric price discovery, without requiring distributional assumptions.
AI.conclusion: The Wasserstein distance provides a theoretically sound and practical alternative to proxy-based non-Gaussianity measures for ICA, enabling effective application to diverse real-world tasks without distributional assumptions.

title: A novel unsupervised machine learning strategy to handle multimodal cardiac PET/MRI data
summary: Arrhythmogenic left ventricular cardiomyopathy is a genetic myocardial disease difficult to diagnose due to the lack of gold standard criteria. Simultaneous PET/MR imaging, combined with multiparametric quantitative analysis, could facilitate the identification of different profiles related to the phenotype and progression of cardiomyopathy. This preliminary study focuses on a methodological strategy for dealing with PET/MRI data, including inter-patient data linkage and regional analysis. Two-step clustering was applied to T1 and T2 maps, LGE, and 18F-FDG-PET images of 99 patients genetically diagnosed with arrhythmogenic left ventricular cardiomyopathy. Each patient's images were independently z-scored and summed into a single volume, which was clustered into supervoxels. Thirty-two inter-patient groups of supervoxels were obtained by spectral clustering. An "abnormality" score was assigned to each cluster and modality, and used to visualise abnormal regions likely associated with disease. They enabled the generation of automated textual and bullseye health reports for each patient, which were compared with cardiac imager assessments using balanced accuracy in repeated nested cross-validation. This approach was further validated on a larger cohort of 167 numerical phantoms. The reports generated by clustering accurately identified most of the cardiac physicians' observations (BA = 0.76 $\pm$ 0.04 in repeated nested cross-validation on patients, and BA $\ge$ 0.8 on phantoms). Furthermore, the identified abnormal clusters closely matched their visual observations, facilitating the identification of varying degrees of fibrosis or inflammation on the images. This approach enables a more systematic handling of multimodal PET/MRI data to characterise myocardial heterogeneity in arrhythmogenic left ventricular cardiomyopathy patients.

AI.tldr: This study uses simultaneous PET/MR imaging with multiparametric quantitative analysis and two-step clustering to identify abnormal myocardial regions in arrhythmogenic left ventricular cardiomyopathy patients, achieving accurate automated health reports (BA=0.76±0.04 in patients, BA≥0.8 on phantoms) that closely match cardiac physician assessments.
AI.motivation: Arrhythmogenic left ventricular cardiomyopathy is difficult to diagnose due to the lack of gold standard criteria, and there is a need for better methodological strategies to handle multimodal PET/MRI data for characterizing myocardial heterogeneity.
AI.method: Two-step clustering (supervoxel clustering followed by spectral clustering) was applied to z-scored and summed T1/T2 maps, LGE, and 18F-FDG-PET images from 99 genetically diagnosed patients; abnormality scores were assigned per cluster/modality for automated report generation, validated via nested cross-validation and on 167 numerical phantoms.
AI.result: Automated reports achieved balanced accuracy of 0.76±0.04 in repeated nested cross-validation on patients and ≥0.8 on phantoms, closely matching cardiac physicians' visual observations of fibrosis/inflammation.
AI.conclusion: This clustering-based approach enables systematic handling of multimodal PET/MRI data to characterize myocardial heterogeneity in arrhythmogenic left ventricular cardiomyopathy, facilitating automated health report generation.

title: Leveraging unlabelled data for generalizable neural population decoding
summary: Robust and accurate neural decoders are integral to neurotechnologies such as brain-computer interfaces and closed-loop experiments. Recent work has shown that tokenizing neural data at the spike level facilitates multi-session pretraining and delivers state-of-the-art decoding performance. However, current spike-based models are restricted to supervised learning (SL), limiting training to datasets with paired behavioural labels. To address this limitation, we introduce MOJO (Masked autOencoder-based JOint training), a training framework for spike-tokenizing models that jointly leverages self-supervised learning (SSL) via masked autoencoding and SL objectives. We evaluate MOJO on three spiking datasets spanning monkey motor cortex during reaching tasks and multi-regional mouse recordings during vision and decision making tasks, demonstrating superior performance over purely SL-trained models. This improvement is especially pronounced when training with limited labelled data, particularly in few-shot finetuning, where only a small amount of labelled data from a new session is available. Incorporating SSL also yields more interpretable neuronal representations, improving performance on brain region classification and spike-statistics prediction without explicit optimization for these tasks. We further show that MOJO generalizes beyond spiking data to human electrocorticography during speech, where it continues to outperform purely SL-trained models and achieves performance comparable to neuro-foundation models (NFMs) designed specifically for continuous signals. Overall, augmenting spike-tokenizing models with SSL improves performance in label-impoverished settings and enables the use of unlabelled data across various tasks and species, while generalizing to other neural modalities. These results suggest a path towards more flexible and scalable data usage when training NFMs.

AI.tldr: MOJO introduces a joint self-supervised and supervised learning framework for spike-tokenizing neural decoders, outperforming purely supervised models across multiple species and neural modalities, especially in label-limited settings.
AI.motivation: Current spike-based neural decoding models are restricted to supervised learning, limiting training to datasets with paired behavioral labels and preventing the use of abundant unlabeled neural data.
AI.method: MOJO (Masked autOencoder-based JOint training) combines self-supervised learning via masked autoencoding with supervised learning objectives for spike-tokenizing models, enabling joint training on both labeled and unlabeled data.
AI.result: Outperforms purely SL-trained models on monkey motor cortex, multi-regional mouse recordings, and human ECoG speech data; improvements are especially pronounced in few-shot finetuning; yields more interpretable neuronal representations; achieves performance comparable to NFMs designed for continuous signals.
AI.conclusion: Augmenting spike-tokenizing models with SSL improves performance in label-impoverished settings, enables use of unlabeled data across tasks and species, generalizes to other neural modalities, and suggests a path towards more flexible and scalable data usage for training neuro-foundation models.

title: Peak-End-Net: A Peak-End Rule Inspired Framework for Generalizable Video Aesthetic Assessment
comment: Accepted to ACM MM 2026, Code: https://github.com/AMAP-ML/Peak-End-Net
summary: Video aesthetic assessment (VAA) aims to predict how aesthetically pleasing a video is, yet remains far less explored than other visual assessment tasks. Its progress is hindered not only by the scarcity of large-scale benchmarks, but also by the intrinsic subjectivity of aesthetic judgment, which is shaped by human perception. In this paper, we revisit VAA from a psychological perspective and propose \textit{Peak-End-Net}, a lightweight and interpretable framework inspired by the \textit{peak-end rule}, which suggests that people tend to judge a temporal experience mainly according to its salient moments and the ending. Building on this intuition, we first transfer knowledge from image aesthetic assessment (IAA) to VAA by introducing a pretrained IAA head to produce frame-wise aesthetic priors, which serve as surrogate signals for identifying aesthetically salient moments and guiding \textit{peak-end rule}-based temporal aggregation. To further capture how a video evolves aesthetically over time, we design an aesthetic rhythm encoder that models temporal progression beyond isolated moments. Additionally, we refine the overall assessment through a dynamic gated fusion mechanism to improve robustness under distribution shift. Our method is built on a frozen vision transformer (ViT) and requires only a small number of trainable parameters, making it scalable and parameter-efficient. Extensive experiments on two existing VAA benchmarks, including in-domain evaluation on VADB and cross-domain testing on DIVIDE-3K, demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, affirming the value of psychologically grounded modeling for VAA. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/AMAP-ML/Peak-End-Net.

AI.tldr: Peak-End-Net is a lightweight, interpretable framework for video aesthetic assessment inspired by the peak-end rule from psychology. It uses a pretrained image aesthetic assessment head for frame-wise priors, an aesthetic rhythm encoder for temporal progression, and a dynamic gated fusion mechanism for robustness. Achieving SOTA on VADB and DIVIDE-3K, it demonstrates the value of psychologically grounded modeling with minimal trainable parameters.
AI.motivation: Video aesthetic assessment is underexplored due to lack of large-scale benchmarks and the inherent subjectivity of aesthetic judgment shaped by human perception. The authors aim to address these challenges by revisiting VAA from a psychological perspective.
AI.method: (1) Transfer knowledge from IAA to VAA via a pretrained IAA head for frame-wise aesthetic priors; (2) Peak-end rule-based temporal aggregation to identify salient moments and endings; (3) Aesthetic rhythm encoder to model temporal evolution; (4) Dynamic gated fusion for robustness under distribution shift; (5) Built on a frozen ViT with few trainable parameters.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on two VAA benchmarks: in-domain evaluation on VADB and cross-domain testing on DIVIDE-3K.
AI.conclusion: Psychologically grounded modeling (peak-end rule) is valuable and effective for VAA. The approach is lightweight, interpretable, and parameter-efficient.

title: PlumeQuant: Uncertainty-aware consistency assessment of methane plume masks and emission-rate estimates
comment: 50 pages, 9 figures. Supplementary material provided as an ancillary file. Code and derived data archived at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21282534
summary: Imaging spectrometers increasingly distribute source-resolved methane plume products in which the plume mask, integrated mass enhancement (IME), plume length, emission rate, and uncertainty are physically and algorithmically linked. Using 63 EMIT-derived Carbon Mapper plume records from 27 scenes, we show that these published scalar quantities do not uniquely constrain the plume boundary: substantially different yet plausible masks reproduce the same IME, plume length, and emission rate. Genetic-algorithm (GA) ensembles conditioned on the published IME and plume length make this equifinality explicit: the high-confidence core selected by nearly all target-consistent masks covers a median of 13% of the plausible footprint envelope, and ambiguity is largest for weak, low-overlap plumes. The diagnostics come from PlumeQuant, which recomputes IME, plume length, emission rate, and five-term uncertainty from distributed product components under stated conventions and evaluates four mask representations: the distributed reference mask, a transparent Carbon Mapper-informed analogue (CM-like), the GA ensemble, and optional expert edits. The CM-like mask is generated per plume without access to the reference mask or published quantities, with settings fixed once on a scene-disjoint 44-plume development split. It reproduced published IME with +0.72% median difference and emission rate with +0.16% (6.98% mean absolute), reached 0.843 median intersection-over-union against the reference masks, and matched the published uncertainty scale (median ratio 1.01). Holdout mean absolute errors were 7.6% (IME), 9.5% (length), and 6.1% (rate). These are product-level consistency diagnostics, not independent validation. They flag weak, offset, or ambiguous plumes for expert review.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that published methane plume metrics (IME, plume length, emission rate) from imaging spectrometers do not uniquely define the plume boundary, using genetic-algorithm ensembles to reveal equifinality. The authors introduce PlumeQuant, a tool that recomputes these quantities under consistent conventions, and evaluate a transparent Carbon Mapper-like (CM-like) mask generation method that achieves high consistency with published products (0.843 median IoU, +0.72% IME difference, +0.16% emission rate difference) while flagging ambiguous plumes for expert review.
AI.motivation: To reveal and quantify the equifinality (multiple plausible solutions) in published methane plume products from imaging spectrometers, where different plume boundaries can yield the same scalar metrics (IME, length, emission rate).
AI.method: Used genetic-algorithm (GA) ensembles conditioned on published IME and plume length to generate alternative masks; developed PlumeQuant to recompute metrics under consistent conventions; created a CM-like mask generator using fixed settings on a 44-plume development split, tested on 63 EMIT-derived Carbon Mapper plume records from 27 scenes.
AI.result: High-confidence core covers median 13% of plausible footprint envelope; CM-like masks achieved 0.843 median IoU with reference masks, +0.72% median IME difference, +0.16% emission rate difference (6.98% mean absolute), and 1.01 median uncertainty ratio; holdout MAEs were 7.6% (IME), 9.5% (length), 6.1% (rate).
AI.conclusion: Published scalar plume metrics underdetermine the actual plume boundary, especially for weak, low-overlap plumes. PlumeQuant and the CM-like mask generator provide consistency diagnostics that can flag problematic plumes for expert review, but these are not independent validation tools.

title: CF-Net: Conflict Fusion with Speaker Normalisation and Certainty Weighting for Ambivalence/Hesitancy Recognition
summary: Detecting ambivalence and hesitancy (AH) in unconstrained video is challenging because the target signal is inherently ambiguous and expressed through subtle cross-modal incongruence rather than prototypical affect. We present CF-Net, a deep multimodal network submitted to the 3rd Edition of the AH Video Recognition Challenge (ABAW 11th, ECCV 2026), targeting the BAH dataset. CF-Net encodes visual, audio, and transcript streams with frozen SigLIP2, HuBERT, and DistilBERT backbones, normalises backbone features per speaker to reduce identity leakage, and fuses them via a ConflictFusion module that explicitly computes pairwise cross-modal incongruence. Training combines certainty-weighted focal loss, manifold mixup, and modality dropout; an auxiliary certainty-regression head leverages ambiguity annotations to stabilise learning on genuinely borderline samples. CF-Net achieves a Macro F1 of 0.7155 on the BAH validation set and 0.7364 (AP = 0.7492) on the private challenge test set.

AI.tldr: CF-Net is a multimodal deep network for detecting ambivalence/hesitancy in video, using frozen backbones (SigLIP2, HuBERT, DistilBERT), per-speaker normalization, a ConflictFusion module for cross-modal incongruence, and specialized training techniques to achieve 0.7364 Macro F1 on the BAH challenge test set.
AI.motivation: Ambivalence and hesitancy detection in unconstrained video is challenging because the signal is subtle, ambiguous, and expressed through cross-modal incongruence rather than clear prototypical affect patterns.
AI.method: CF-Net encodes visual, audio, and transcript streams using frozen SigLIP2, HuBERT, and DistilBERT backbones respectively; normalizes features per speaker to reduce identity leakage; fuses them via a ConflictFusion module that explicitly computes pairwise cross-modal incongruence; and trains with certainty-weighted focal loss, manifold mixup, modality dropout, and an auxiliary certainty-regression head.
AI.result: CF-Net achieves Macro F1 of 0.7155 on the BAH validation set and 0.7364 (AP = 0.7492) on the private challenge test set.
